But the energy behind the argument was pastoral, not metaphysical. Interest could enslave the poor, destroy households, and concentrate wealth in ways that violated justice and charity. What made usury so dangerous was precisely that it dressed extraction in legality — it made personal gain disconnected from service enforceable in court, and made the borrower’s dependence a profitable asset for the lender.

And yet, from early on, the tradition was more discriminating than a flat ban suggests. Over time, Christian thinkers nuanced the principle: lending might be compensated for genuine risk, for opportunity forgone, for labor lost — provided the arrangement did not exploit vulnerability. That nuancing, which later centuries would systematize, was not backsliding. It was the moral calculus doing its proper work: trying to align return with contribution and need, rather than with power or entitlement. The full flowering of that analysis belongs to the Scholastics and Franciscans of the coming chapters. But the question they refined was asked here first, and asked sharply: when does gain from a loan correspond to anything real, and when is it simply the strong billing the weak for their weakness?

sections.title: Charity, Debt, and Obligation
paragraphs: Christianity also introduced a moral vocabulary for debt and obligation that reached far beyond the courtroom — indeed, it planted debt in the middle of its central prayer. “Forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors”: in Matthew’s Greek the words are bookkeeping terms, opheilemata, the entries in a ledger. Every time the community prayed, it rehearsed the idea that debts are the kind of thing that can, and sometimes must, be released. Behind the prayer stood Israel’s institutional memory: the sabbatical year’s release of debts, the Jubilee’s great unwinding of accumulated dispossession — a legal architecture built on the premise that debt must not be allowed to compound into permanent bondage.

In this vocabulary, debt was never merely a contractual claim; it created a relationship, and the relationship had duties on both ends. Borrowers were morally responsible — the tradition never romanticized default — but so were lenders, a symmetry Roman law did not require. Wealth carried duties beyond legal rights. Giving, forgiving, and sharing were not optional decorations on economic life; they were ethical imperatives with social and spiritual consequences.

The fathers stated the point with a bluntness that still startles. Basil told the rich man hoarding grain that the bread in his cupboard belonged to the hungry, the cloak in his chest to the naked, the money in his vaults to the destitute. Ambrose told the wealthy that in giving to the poor they were not being generous with their own property but returning what was already owed — restitution, not largesse. This was not a rhetorical flourish; it was a theory of property. The rich were stewards, not owners in any absolute sense. The earth had been given to all; title deeds were a human administrative convenience layered on top of a more fundamental common destination of goods. Clement of Alexandria, writing for the prosperous merchants of his city and asking whether a rich man could even be saved, answered yes — but only the rich man who held his wealth as an instrument for service, sitting loose to it, ready to spend it on his neighbor.

Nor did this remain a matter of sermons. The first believers in Jerusalem, Acts reports, held their possessions in common, selling property as anyone had need — an experiment in total sharing that the Church remembered ever after as both an ideal and a warning about how hard the ideal is to sustain. Three centuries later, Basil converted his preaching into architecture: outside Caesarea he built a sprawling charitable complex — hospital, hospice, poorhouse, staff — that contemporaries called “the new city,” and that historians point to as a landmark in the invention of the hospital itself. Here is an early sighting of a pattern this book will track closely: the movement from charity as personal virtue to charity as institution. A sermon relieves the conscience; a building with a budget relieves the poor. Basil understood that mercy, to persist, must be organized — and organized mercy needs endowment, administration, and accounts, which is to say it needs a moral economics.

Charity, in short, was the mechanism that anchored the economy to life. Property and capital existed not for personal aggrandizement but for the sustaining of those in need; profit was legitimate only insofar as it could coexist with generosity, restraint, and care. Through this lens, commerce, lending, and accumulation were never morally neutral. Every one of them was an occasion to serve life — or a temptation toward extraction.

sections.title: The Moral Danger of Avarice
paragraphs: At the center of the Christian analysis stood a diagnosis: avarice. The Greek moralists had a word for it, pleonexia — the itch for more — and the New Testament gave it a famous epitaph: the love of money is a root of all kinds of evil. Note the precision, which popular quotation always mangles: not money, but the love of it. The coin is innocent. The appetite is not.

When the desert monks and their successors began cataloguing the fundamental disorders of the soul — the lists that John Cassian carried west as eight evil “thoughts” and Gregory the Great later trimmed to the seven deadly sins — the love of money earned a permanent place on the roster. Paul had gone further still and called greed idolatry: the diagnosis, once again, of a rival god. Avarice was not one appetite among many. It was a whole orientation of the person.

Unlike poverty, which limited human possibility, or charity, which extended it, avarice threatened everything at once — soul, household, and city. It performed exactly the inversion Aristotle had feared: it transformed a means into an end and made money sovereign. It justified extracting wealth from the weaker and less fortunate, since accumulation had become its own warrant. And it dissolved the moral distinctions on which a decent economic life depends — between necessity and luxury, contribution and speculation, production and manipulation. The avaricious eye sees only one quality in anything: whether it can be turned into more.

In pressing this diagnosis, Christianity’s early engagement with commerce, property, and money preserved and deepened the insight Aristotle had begun. Economic activity is moral. It forms character. It shapes communities. It orders life, or disorders it — and now, the tradition added, it does so all the way down to the soul.

sections.title: Wealth as a Spiritual Test
paragraphs: There is a reason the Gospels return so insistently to money — by one common count, Jesus talks about wealth and possessions more than about almost any other moral subject. In the Christian imagination, wealth became a test: not a sin in itself, but a revealer of what the heart actually serves.

The test shows up in story after story. A rich young man, sincerely devout, is invited to sell everything and follow — and walks away grieving, “for he had great possessions”; his wealth had answered the question for him. A camel, Jesus remarks, passes through a needle’s eye more easily than a rich man enters the kingdom — though with God, he adds, even that is possible. A tax collector named Zacchaeus, on the other hand, passes the test spectacularly: half his goods to the poor on the spot, and fourfold restitution to anyone he has defrauded — repentance taking the concrete form of economic repair. A destitute widow drops two small coins in the temple treasury and is declared to have given more than all the wealthy donors, because the measure of a gift is what it costs the giver. And in the parable of Lazarus, a rich man is damned not for fraud or violence but for the beggar he stepped over at his own gate — wealth as a test failed by simple, well-fed inattention.

Strangest of all is the parable of the unjust steward, in which a manager about to be sacked hurriedly discounts his master’s debtors’ bills to buy himself friends — and is praised for it. Commentators have puzzled over the story for two thousand years, but its punchline points somewhere definite: use mammon, while you have it, to make friends. Convert money into relationship before it evaporates. Wealth’s highest use, the parable hints, is to be spent into bonds between people — which is about as far from accumulation as an economic instruction can get.

The teaching crystallizes in a single saying: you cannot serve God and mammon. Jesus personifies wealth as a rival master — and the word “serve” is doing the work. Money makes an excellent servant and an intolerable god. The question is never merely how much you have, but who is serving whom; which is, the reader will notice, precisely the question this book keeps asking about capital.

Note what the test framework does not say. It does not say wealth is evil. The tradition remembered rich disciples — Joseph of Arimathea, who buried Jesus in his own new tomb; Lydia, who bankrolled hospitality for the mission — and Clement’s Alexandria was full of believers whose commerce funded the church’s charity. Wealth was dangerous the way strong medicine is dangerous: capable of great service, catastrophic when it takes over the patient. That is why economic life belonged to spiritual formation. Every possession, every loan, every bargain was a small examination, and the sum of a person’s economic choices was, quite literally, an account — one that would be audited.

sections.title: Preparing for the Scholastics
paragraphs: By the late Middle Ages, these convictions had matured into a rich moral vocabulary, and the vocabulary was about to become a science. Scholars began to formalize reasoning about just price, commutative justice, risk, obligation, and the legitimacy of merchants. Ethical reflection on the economy was no longer only monastic or pastoral; it was becoming systematic, analytical, and applicable to the urban commerce and early banking of a Europe filling up with towns. The Franciscans and the other scholastic thinkers of the next chapters would refine these ideas into a framework capable of guiding commerce, lending, and production without losing sight of the human and moral ends that Aristotle and the early Church had fixed in view.

For Christianity, as we have seen, did not reject commerce or property. It demanded accountability from them. It asked whether wealth served life or subordinated life to wealth’s own expansion; it taught that every transaction, every loan, every contract, and every profit could be morally evaluated; and it introduced the language that would later allow ethical banking, charitable funds, and eventually non-extractive capital to exist on purpose rather than by accident.

Aristotle gave us the grammar; Christianity added the conscience. The dialogue between household needs, civic life, and moral duty became richer, more relational, and more institutionally aware. And out of that dialogue would come the Scholastic Revolution — a period in which moral reasoning and market activity were not yet separated, and economic instruments were judged not only for efficiency or legality, but for justice, equity, and service to life.

chapters.title: Chapter 4: The Scholastics Discover the Market

paragraphs: Christianity gave economic life a conscience. The Scholastics gave it a method.

By the time medieval theologians and canon lawyers turned seriously to commerce, the world around them had changed. Europe in the twelfth and thirteenth centuries was no longer only a landscape of rural estates, monasteries, and local barter. Towns were growing, trade routes stretching, merchants rising in importance; money circulated more widely, credit became more necessary, contracts grew more complex, and the great fairs and new universities were filling up with people who had questions. The moral puzzles of economic life could no longer be answered simply by warning against avarice and commanding almsgiving. Those warnings remained necessary. They were no longer enough.

A merchant who bought cloth in one city and sold it in another needed to know whether his profit was legitimate. A lender who faced real risk needed to know whether compensation was always usury. Buyer and seller needed to know whether a price was just when market conditions shifted; a craftsman, whether he could charge more for skill, time, scarcity, or reputation; a community, when exchange served mutual benefit and when it had become exploitation.

Here is one of the most important facts in this book: the Scholastics did not respond by rejecting commerce. They had inherited the full ancient and Christian suspicion of greed, usury, and unnatural accumulation, and they did not conclude from it that market activity was corrupt. Instead they distinguished. They asked when buying and selling were just, when profit was deserved, when risk mattered, when fraud or coercion poisoned an exchange, when price reflected real conditions, and when money-making tipped into moral disorder.

In doing so, they discovered the market as a moral field — not a neutral mechanism, not a self-justifying system, not a sphere conveniently outside theology, law, and virtue, but not a mere den of sin either. The market became a place where human beings could cooperate, deceive, serve, exploit, share risk, meet needs, and pursue gain rightly or wrongly. That was a revolution in moral reasoning.

The Scholastics did not create economics as a modern discipline. They did something different, and in some ways more interesting: they developed a way of thinking about economic activity before economics had separated itself from ethics. Their questions were not only “what price will clear the market?” or “what contract will be enforceable?” but: What is owed? What is fair? What is proportionate, what voluntary, what exploitative? What kind of gain corresponds to real service, and what forms of profit are compatible with justice? Their answers were not always consistent, and they evolved. The achievement lies in the seriousness of the attempt: they tried to think economically without surrendering moral judgment.

sections.title: Commutative Justice
paragraphs: At the center of Scholastic economic thought sits commutative justice.

Justice, for Thomas Aquinas (1225–1274) and the tradition around him, is the virtue by which each person is given what is due — right order in relationships. Since relationships differ, justice takes different forms. Distributive justice concerns how a community allocates common goods, honors, burdens, and resources among its members. Legal justice concerns what individuals owe the common good. Commutative justice concerns exchanges between persons: buying, selling, lending, renting, hiring, contracting, compensating, restoring. It asks whether an exchange is rightly ordered between the parties.

That question already marks a profound shift away from treating market exchange as morally settled by consent. Consent matters — a coerced exchange is unjust, and a deceived party has not truly agreed. But consent alone is not enough. One can formally agree under necessity, ignorance, unequal power, or manipulated conditions; one can accept terms that are legal and still unjust; one can sign a contract and still be wronged.

For the Scholastics, justice in exchange requires proportionality. What is given and what is received should stand in some reasonable relation, and neither party should treat the other’s vulnerability, ignorance, or necessity as an opportunity. The seller should not deceive the buyer about quality; the buyer should not exploit the seller’s desperation; the lender should not profit merely because the borrower has no alternative; the merchant should not engineer scarcity to inflate gain beyond service. None of this demands mathematical equality in every bargain — the Scholastics knew that economic life contains uncertainty, haggling, customary variation, and real differences of place and time. What they insisted on is that agreement does not purify an exchange. Exchange must answer to justice.

The modern relevance is immediate, because extractive structures run on formal consent. The tenant signed. The worker accepted. The founder agreed to the term sheet; the debtor took the loan; the user clicked the terms of service; the supplier entered the contract; the country accepted the financing; the open-source maintainer chose the license; the company accepted the acquisition offer. Scholastic reasoning asks the deeper question: what kind of relationship did the exchange create, and was it just? Did one party profit from the other’s necessity? Did information asymmetry become an instrument of domination? Did the stronger party shift risk downward while capturing the upside? Did the contract preserve reciprocity, or did it formalize extraction? Did the exchange serve real need, or monetize vulnerability?
Commutative justice, in other words, is not a medieval curiosity. It is one of the lost foundations of moral market reasoning.

sections.title: Aquinas and the Just Price
paragraphs: The just price is one of history’s most misunderstood ideas. It is routinely imagined as a fixed price decreed by theologians according to some abstract moral formula — a priest with a rate card. The caricature misses everything interesting. The just price was never “the morally correct number” imposed from outside the market. It was an attempt to say when a price could be considered fair, given the realities of human need, custom, scarcity, cost, risk, quality, and common estimation — Aquinas takes it up in the Summa’s questions on cheating in trade, and his treatment is strikingly supple.

For Aquinas, buying and selling exist for mutual advantage: one person needs what another has, and both can benefit. But because exchange can be corrupted by deception and unequal need, it must be governed by justice: the seller who knowingly overcharges beyond reason, or the buyer who knowingly underpays beyond reason, violates the equality proper to exchange. At the same time, Aquinas recognizes that prices are not exact. Human goods rarely have a single determinate value; worth depends partly on estimation, usefulness, scarcity, and circumstance. The just price is therefore not a precise point but something more like a morally permissible zone — within it, bargaining is legitimate; outside it, exchange becomes exploitative.

This matters because it shows what the tradition was and was not against. Scholastic moral economy was not opposed to market valuation. It was opposed to treating market valuation as self-justifying. The market may help reveal value; it does not automatically sanctify price. A price can emerge from supply and demand and still be unjust if the conditions generating it are disordered. Famine prices may reflect scarcity — or hoarding. Housing prices may reflect demand — or speculation, exclusionary zoning, underbuilding, financialization, and monopoly. Wages may reflect bargaining power, but bargaining power itself may be unjustly distributed. A founder’s valuation may reflect investor appetite, and the appetite may be driven by expectations that will later deform the company.

Aquinas gives us permission to ask: what stands behind the price? Not permission to replace prices with moral intuition — prices carry information, coordinate activity, register scarcity, preference, cost, urgency, and expectation, and ignoring them ends badly. But the just price tradition insists that price is never only information. It is also a moral relation. A price can tell us what someone can get. It cannot, by itself, tell us what someone is due.

That distinction matters everywhere capital touches life. A landlord may be able to charge more because the tenants have nowhere else to go. A pharmaceutical company may be able to charge more because patients need the medicine to live. A platform may be able to take a larger cut because sellers cannot reach customers any other way; a dominant cloud provider may be able to impose terms because switching costs are crushing; a venture investor may be able to demand control because the founder is desperate for runway. In every case the market price reveals real power. It does not necessarily reveal justice. The Scholastic question is never “did the market permit this?” It is “does this exchange preserve right relation between the parties?”

sections.title: Merchant Legitimacy
paragraphs: One of the most consequential Scholastic developments was the moral rehabilitation of the merchant.

Earlier moral imagination had eyed merchants with suspicion. They bought only to sell dearer; they seemed to profit without transforming the good; they stood between producer and consumer, and their gain looked parasitic. A farmer grows grain, a baker makes bread — their contributions are visible. What exactly does the merchant add?

Sometimes, the Scholastics answered, quite a lot. A merchant may transport goods from where they are abundant to where they are needed. He may assume risk, and bear the costs of storage, travel, spoilage, theft, delay, and uncertainty. He may hold hard-won knowledge of places, qualities, timing, and demand. He may put goods within reach of people who would otherwise go without, and coordinate across distance and difference. In such cases profit is not automatically suspect. It may correspond to service.

This is a crucial step toward a moral theory of productive capital: gain can be legitimate when it reflects real contribution — labor, skill, risk, coordination, knowledge, patience, responsibility. The merchant’s profit is never justified merely because he managed to obtain it. It is justified when it corresponds to the useful service he provides within the exchange.

The distinction is delicate, and the tradition knew it. The merchant who moves grain from abundance to need serves life; the merchant who hoards grain during famine to raise prices feeds on it. Bearing risk may deserve compensation; manufacturing scarcity creates the conditions of extraction. Superior knowledge used to coordinate trade is legitimate; superior knowledge used to deceive violates justice. The merchant’s moral status hangs entirely on the relation between gain and service.

For modern finance and entrepreneurship, no principle could be more pointed, because so much of the modern economy profits from intermediation. The question is always whether the intermediation performs real coordination or merely collects value at a chokepoint. A payment processor can reduce friction and enable exchange — or extract rents from dependency. A marketplace can connect buyers and sellers — or become a gatekeeper squeezing both. A venture investor can supply patient risk capital, judgment, and resilience — or impose exit pressure and control rights that subordinate the company’s mission. A private equity firm can genuinely improve operations — or strip assets, load debt, and leave fragility behind. A platform can solve a coordination problem — or enclose the field of coordination and charge tribute to everyone who must cross it.

So the Scholastic rehabilitation of the merchant licenses nothing by itself. What it gives us is a test: name the service that justifies the gain. If no real service can be named, or the gain is out of all proportion to it, or the “service” consists of creating a dependency the intermediary then monetizes, the activity has begun its slide from productive intermediation toward extraction.

sections.title: Risk, Fairness, and Reciprocity
paragraphs: The Scholastics also had to confront risk, because commerce lives in time. Goods spoil, ships sink, debtors default, markets shift, harvests fail, rulers change the rules, merchants get robbed, investments die, labor goes to waste. To participate in commerce is to face uncertainty. The moral question is whether that uncertainty can justify gain.

A crude anti-commercial answer says no: profit is greed with paperwork. A crude pro-commercial answer says yes: all gain is baptized by the risks of enterprise. The Scholastic approach is more careful. Risk matters — but not all claimed risk is equal. Compensation for risk may be legitimate when the risk is real, the gain proportionate, the terms transparent, and the arrangement does not exploit necessity.

The stakes were highest in lending. The traditional usury prohibition treated a loan for consumption very differently from a partnership in productive enterprise. When a poor man borrows to survive, charging him for the mere use of money profits from need. But when capital is placed into a risky venture, where the provider may genuinely lose everything, the analysis changes. Is the return money breeding money — or compensation for shared danger, forgone opportunity, labor, expense, participation in production? Later Scholastic and Franciscan thinkers would refine these distinctions into a full apparatus; the direction is already visible here. Moral economy must learn to distinguish sterile extraction from fruitful participation.

The distinction was difficult then and is difficult now. Investors claim risk to justify return — sometimes rightly, since without capital at risk, research, infrastructure, new products, and early-stage organizations would never begin. Patient capital can serve life. But risk can also be deployed rhetorically to underwrite disproportionate claims. An investor may hold control rights far exceeding the capital actually at hazard. A fund may demand high returns while shifting the downside onto workers, suppliers, founders, or the public. A lender may price risk so that repayment becomes impossible — manufacturing the default that justifies seizing the collateral. A shareholder may demand unlimited upside while limited liability walls off the personal downside. A platform may bear almost no entrepreneurial risk once its network position is entrenched, and go on extracting from those who depend on it indefinitely.

The question is never whether risk exists. It is how risk and reward are distributed. Reciprocity requires that those who receive gain bear responsibility proportionate to their claim; that return correspond to contribution; that upside not be surgically severed from downside; that one party’s security not be purchased with another party’s fragility.

And here the Scholastic tradition speaks directly to modern non-extractive finance. A repayment cap is not merely a financial mechanism. It is an institutional attempt to preserve proportionality. It says: capital may receive return, but not an infinite claim; risk may be compensated, but not converted into permanent domination; investors may share in surplus, but may not own the enterprise’s future without limit. The financial claim must remain ordered to the productive activity it helped make possible. That is Scholastic reasoning in contemporary dress.

sections.title: Commerce as Morally Embedded Cooperation
paragraphs: One of the deepest Scholastic lessons is that commerce is morally ambivalent because cooperation is morally ambivalent.

Human beings trade because they need one another. No household, craftsman, farmer, merchant, builder, scholar, or city can supply everything alone. Exchange lets gifts, skills, goods, and knowledge circulate; it helps strangers serve each other across distance; it can create peaceable interdependence and reward attention to another’s need. But cooperation can rot into domination. Interdependence becomes dependency; knowledge becomes manipulation; scarcity becomes leverage; contract becomes coercion in legal form; price becomes a mask for power.

This is why the Scholastics never let commerce write its own rules. Commerce must be embedded — in virtues, law, custom, conscience, and the common good; surrounded by practices that restrain fraud, coercion, exploitation, and avarice; interpreted by moral reason; accountable to justice. Embeddedness does not mean fixing every price centrally or micromanaging every bargain. It means exchange happens inside a larger moral order: the market is not the highest court of appeal, economic outcomes can be judged by standards the market did not generate, legality is not justice, and profitability is not legitimacy.

Precisely this is what modern economic thought struggles to hold onto. Once the market is imagined as autonomous, moral critique starts to look external — an intervention, a constraint, a regulatory afterthought, a philanthropic garnish, a personal preference. The Scholastics assumed the opposite. Morality is not added to economic life from outside; economic life is already moral, because it consists of human acts in relation to other humans. Buying, selling, lending, borrowing, hiring, investing, and owning are acts of relationship. They can honor what is due, or violate it.

sections.title: The Moral Meaning of Contract
paragraphs: Contracts sat at the heart of Scholastic moral reasoning because contracts formalize obligation. A contract is more than an agreement; it is a structured relationship between parties across time, defining expectations, duties, risks, remedies, and claims. It can protect fairness by making promises explicit. It can also encode injustice, when one party uses superior power to impose terms the other cannot reasonably refuse.

The Scholastics understood that the form of an agreement does not exhaust its morality. A contract may be invalid or compromised by fraud, coercion, grave imbalance, hidden information, or the exploitation of necessity. This matters enormously, because contract is one of the main ways extraction becomes respectable. Once an exploitative relationship is written down, signed, and enforceable, it looks clean. The violence disappears into procedure.

Modern economies are full of such procedural cleanliness: terms of service no user reads; debt covenants that quietly transfer control; employment contracts that restrict a person’s future livelihood; supplier agreements that impose impossible delivery risks; venture term sheets that bury control implications in technical language; acquisition documents that dissolve a mission under the vocabulary of fiduciary duty; licenses that let commons-based labor be enclosed by private platforms.

The Scholastic instinct is to look beneath the form to the justice of the relationship. Was the agreement truly informed, or made under necessity? Were the burdens proportionate and the risks transparent? Did one party acquire hidden power over the other? Does the contract preserve the purpose of the underlying activity, or convert that activity into an instrument of extraction?

This is why contract design is moral architecture — why the clauses themselves are morally load-bearing: redemption rights, covenants, governance rights, liquidation preferences, transfer restrictions, exit provisions, asset locks, repayment caps, fiduciary duties, stewardship mechanisms. The Scholastics had none of our instruments. They had the principle that governs all of them: economic justice must be built into the form of obligation.

sections.title: Usury and the Search for Moral Distinction
paragraphs: No issue forced Scholastic thought to become more precise than usury.

The inherited prohibition was severe, and not arbitrary. To charge for the mere use of money — above all when lending to the needy — made another person’s necessity profitable. It let money generate gain without visible labor or risk. It threatened to turn debt into domination, and it violated both charity and commutative justice. Aquinas devoted a full question of the Summa to it.

Yet commercial life kept growing more complicated. Credit was necessary; merchants needed financing; trade involved time and hazard. A person who lent might suffer real loss, miss real opportunities, incur real expenses, share real danger. The blunt category of “interest” did not capture every form of compensation.

So the Scholastics did what they did best: they distinguished. Loans were not partnerships. Consumption loans were not productive investment. Certain compensation for actual loss was not illicit gain. Real risk was not guaranteed extraction; necessity was not enterprise; money as sterile possession was not capital participating in productive activity. The distinctions were imperfect and sometimes strained, and clever people could work moral exceptions into loopholes. But the effort itself is the point. The Scholastics were trying to keep moral reasoning from collapsing into either rigid anti-economics or convenient permissiveness. Their governing question — when does return on money correspond to real participation in productive risk, and when does it become extraction? — is one of the central questions of this book.

It returns in modern finance wearing new language and the same moral structure. When does an investor deserve return? When is interest legitimate? When does a dividend reflect shared surplus, and a management fee real service? When does carried interest reward skill, and when does it harvest asymmetric power? When does debt discipline a firm, and when does it dominate it? When does venture capital enable innovation, and when does it subordinate the enterprise to exit? When does private equity improve productivity, and when does it strip value? When does a platform earn revenue for service, and when does it collect tribute from dependency?

The Scholastics did not answer all of these. They bequeathed something more durable: the habit of distinction. A moral economy cannot survive on slogans. It must distinguish, carefully, between superficially similar practices with different moral meanings.

sections.title: Against Both Naivete and Cynicism
paragraphs: The Scholastic approach steers between two temptations that still distort economic debate.

The first is naivete: the belief that economic life can be governed by good intentions. This underestimates scarcity, incentives, uncertainty, coordination, and self-interest. It produces moral language without institutional competence — condemning markets without understanding what they do well, dreaming of justice while ignoring the machinery by which goods are actually produced, allocated, and sustained.

The second is cynicism: the belief that because economic life is complex, moral judgment is irrelevant or childish. This treats legality, profitability, or market price as sufficient; assumes that whatever sophisticated parties agree to must be legitimate; hides power behind technical language; and mistakes complexity for moral exemption.

The Scholastics resisted both. They were morally serious without being economically naive, attentive to commerce without being morally passive. They understood that the world of exchange requires practical reasoning rather than purity fantasies — that merchants, lenders, buyers, sellers, and rulers face real constraints — and they refused to let the constraints abolish conscience.

That balance is exactly what the present moment needs. A contemporary moral economy must be able to read term sheets, balance sheets, fund structures, procurement systems, corporate law, software licenses, platform incentives, and capital stacks — and still ask whether those instruments serve justice. It must become technically literate without becoming morally captured. The Scholastics are the standing proof that the combination is possible: disciplined moral analysis under conditions of economic complexity.

sections.title: From Aquinas to the Franciscans
paragraphs: Aquinas is central to this story, but he is not its end. His framework — commutative justice, the just price, legitimate exchange, moral limits — gives the tradition its architecture. But the commercial world kept developing, and new questions pressed harder. What exactly is capital? Can money placed in enterprise become productive? What should we make of entrepreneurial foresight — is uncertainty itself economically meaningful? Can a merchant’s knowledge of future conditions justify gain? When does investment participate in production, rather than merely profit from lending?

These questions found their sharpest interrogators in an unexpected quarter: the Franciscans — famous for poverty, not finance. Yet precisely because they took poverty seriously, they were forced to think carefully about money. They lived among towns, merchants, debtors, craftsmen, and the poor; they saw commerce sustain life and exploit it, often on the same street. And they understood that condemning all economic complexity would do the poor no favors, if what the poor needed was credit, markets, and institutions sturdy enough to protect them from worse forms of extraction.

Peter John Olivi and the Franciscans after him would push moral economy into new territory: a more sophisticated account of productive capital, entrepreneurial judgment, risk, and value over time. Bernardino of Siena would defend the dignity of the merchant under the right conditions. The Monti di Pietà would attempt to institutionalize ethical lending in brick and ledger. If Aquinas supplies the architecture of justice in exchange, the Franciscans begin to show how capital itself can be morally analyzed.

Their question is still ours: can capital be structured so that it serves the poor, supports enterprise, and remains morally disciplined? The next chapter turns to it — and finds, among friars, merchants, and charitable credit institutions, that moral economy begins to look surprisingly like financial design.

chapters.title: Chapter 5: Franciscans, Merchants, and the Birth of Capital

paragraphs: If you had toured Europe around the year 1290 looking for someone to explain finance to you, the very last person you would have picked was a Franciscan friar. The Franciscans were, by vow and by design, the poorest people in Christendom. Their founder, Francis of Assisi, had renounced property so thoroughly that he handed his clothes back to his own father in the public square. His followers begged their bread, held goods in common when they held any at all, and built their lives around Christ, fraternity, prayer, preaching, and solidarity with the poor. Accumulation was not their temptation. Renunciation was the whole point.
And yet it was the Franciscans, of all people, who did some of the clearest thinking anyone had yet done about capital.

The paradox is easier to understand than it looks. Because they took poverty seriously, the Franciscans had to take money seriously. They could not wave finance away as a neutral technical field, and they could not dismiss it as merely wicked and beneath analysis. They lived in the swelling towns of medieval Europe, cheek by jowl with merchants, artisans, debtors, widows, workers, and the urban poor, and they watched the machinery of credit operate at close range. They saw loans carry a household through a failed harvest, and loans quietly strip a household of everything it owned. They saw merchants meet real needs, and commerce turn predatory — sometimes in the same market square. And they noticed something charity alone could not fix: the poor suffered not only from a shortage of alms but from badly built economic institutions.
So the tradition most devoted to owning nothing became one of the traditions most capable of thinking carefully about what ownership and investment are for.

This does not make the Franciscans defenders of capitalism in any modern sense — that would be anachronistic. They never imagined shareholder primacy, private equity, venture capital, global financial markets, platform monopolies, or the exit-driven startup. But the questions they asked have not aged. When is capital productive? When does money participate in real economic activity? When is profit connected to labor, risk, foresight, and service — and when does lending become extraction? How do you lend to the poor without destroying the charitable fund that serves them? How do you discipline commerce morally without rejecting it?

With questions like these, moral economy stops being a sermon and starts becoming financial architecture. The Scholastics had taught Europe to ask whether an exchange was just. The Franciscans sharpened the question: can capital itself be ordered toward life?

sections.title: Poverty and Economic Intelligence
paragraphs: Franciscan poverty was never simply an ethical preference for having less. It was a spiritual and social witness. It challenged the possessive logic of wealth, proclaimed dependence on God, created solidarity with people who could not insulate themselves from need, and refused to build security through domination.
But poverty did something else as well: it clarified vision.

Stand near the poor and you see parts of the economy the comfortable can ignore. You see what debt costs when the harvest fails, the work disappears, the illness arrives, the rent comes due, or the tools go to the pawnbroker. You learn the difference between a loan that preserves a household and a loan that transfers it, item by item, to someone else. You see that a small amount of credit at the right moment can save a family, and that a small amount of extraction, compounding quietly, can ruin one. You see that a market is not an abstraction but a meeting place of bargaining power, information, urgency, and necessity.

For the poor, in other words, bad financial design has never been theoretical. A contract could mean the loss of tools. A pledge could mean the loss of bedding. A missed payment could mean shame, hunger, displacement, or the collapse of a family’s fragile stability. Whether a loan was fair or predatory could decide whether a household survived.

That is why it sells the Franciscans short to file their economics under “medieval attitudes toward money.” What they built is closer to a pastoral economics of vulnerability: a sustained inquiry into how economic structures land on the people least able to absorb harm.

The inquiry has not gone out of date. A founder with no alternatives can be pressured into destructive investment terms. A tenant with nowhere else to go accepts the exploitative rent. A worker without savings stays in degrading work. A small supplier at a platform’s mercy absorbs every new fee and algorithm change. A public institution that runs on proprietary digital infrastructure discovers it can no longer govern itself. Vulnerability is still monetizable.
Franciscan moral economy begins by refusing to let vulnerability become someone else’s business model.

sections.title: Olivi and Productive Capital
paragraphs: Peter John Olivi matters as much as anyone in this story, because he found words for something earlier moral theology had struggled to name: capital can be more than sterile money. He was a brilliant and contentious Provençal friar who spent much of his career under suspicion — after his death the authorities condemned his writings and destroyed his tomb, a fate reserved for people whose ideas refuse to stay put.

The old condemnation of usury rested partly on the idea that money, as money, is barren. It does not reproduce naturally; it is a medium of exchange, a measure, a convention. To demand payment merely for its use looked like making money breed from money, detached from real labor or production — unnatural, said Aristotle. Christian tradition deepened the objection, because lending at interest could exploit the needy and violate charity.

But commercial life kept complicating the theory. What if money is not hoarded, and not lent for consumption, but put to work — spent on tools, inventory, equipment, transport, or labor? What if it funds a merchant’s voyage, or lets a craftsman expand production, or moves goods from where they are abundant to where they are needed? And what of the person who supplies that money and then waits, exposed to uncertainty, delay, and possible loss?

Olivi saw that money committed to enterprise is not morally identical to money sitting in a strongbox. Ordered toward productive activity, it changes character. It becomes capital in a richer sense: not mere currency but a claim embedded in future-oriented work.

This was a large step, and it helps to notice what it did not do. It did not abolish the problem of usury. It did not make every return on money legitimate. It did not hand capital a blank check. What it opened was a possibility: profit from capital may correspond to real economic contribution — when the capital participates in productive risk and service.

And inside that possibility you can watch the birth of a moral distinction modern finance badly needs. There is a difference between return that corresponds to productive participation and return that extracts from need; between sharing risk and imposing debt; between enabling enterprise and controlling it; between patient capital and predatory capital; between a claim that stays proportionate to contribution and one that expands without limit.
Olivi did not give us modern non-extractive finance. He made it thinkable. He taught us to ask when capital is a partner in production, and when it becomes production’s master.

sections.title: Entrepreneurial Foresight
paragraphs: For Olivi, productive capital was inseparable from foresight. Commerce happens in time. A merchant does not simply swap goods in a static present; he judges where goods will be needed, what they may be worth, how long the road will take, what dangers may arise, what demand may exist, and whether the whole venture is worth the trouble. He acts under uncertainty — and that, Olivi saw, is a real contribution.

The merchant who knows grain is plentiful in one valley and scarce in the next creates value by moving it. The trader who anticipates demand coordinates production before the need becomes visible. The craftsman who buys tools before the orders arrive takes a genuine risk. The builder of useful infrastructure ahead of any revenue serves a future good that does not yet exist.

Modern economics takes entrepreneurship seriously — the Austrians especially, with their accounts of discovery, uncertainty, and dispersed knowledge. The Franciscan contribution is different. It asks not whether entrepreneurial foresight creates profit, but when the profit is morally justified.

Foresight can serve life: it can spot unmet need, coordinate resources, bear uncertainty, and call goods into being. It can also curdle into speculation. It can profit from an anticipated scarcity without relieving it, trade on informational advantage, corner a market, engineer scarcity in order to sell relief at a premium, or anticipate another person’s desperation and position itself to collect. The moral issue is never foresight itself. It is the purpose and structure of foresight. Entrepreneurial judgment is legitimate when it participates in real provision, bears real uncertainty, and serves genuine need. It turns extractive when it profits by manipulating need, enclosing access, or creating dependency.

You could hardly ask for a more contemporary distinction. The founder who builds open digital infrastructure before there is a market for it is exercising life-serving foresight, and the patient, mission-aligned fund that backs her participates in it. The platform that predicts where dependency will arise and installs itself as the unavoidable intermediary is exercising the other kind. The property investor who sees a neighborhood improving and captures, through speculation, the value its own residents are creating has foresight too — just not in the service of the community creating the value. The financier who profits from impending distress may be very intelligent. Intelligence is not the same thing as justice.
Economic intelligence, in short, requires moral interpretation. To foresee is not automatically to serve. The question is whether foresight is ordered toward provision or toward extraction.

sections.title: Risk-Bearing and Uncertainty
paragraphs: Risk is capital’s favorite moral credential. The claim is familiar — those who risk capital deserve return — and there is truth in it. Without a willingness to hazard resources, no new enterprise, trade route, technology, farm, workshop, or piece of infrastructure would ever get started. Someone must bear the uncertainty. The Franciscan and Scholastic traditions accepted this. They did not treat all gain from uncertain enterprise as immoral: where capital is genuinely exposed to loss and enables productive activity, a return may be justified.

But risk is not a magic word that sanctifies whatever follows it. Risk must be real. It must be proportionate. It must be shared in a way a fair-minded person could explain. It must not serve as a pretext for domination, or let one party keep the upside while handing the downside to others, or convert a temporary contribution into permanent control.

On exactly these points, modern finance is often morally confused. Investors may take real risk at the start of an enterprise; but give them uncapped upside, control rights, liquidation preferences, anti-dilution protections, and exit powers over the company’s future, and the return soon outgrows the original risk. A lender faces default risk; but a loan built to seize collateral at the first predictable stumble makes the risk less a vulnerability than a strategy. A private equity owner claims entrepreneurial risk while loading the acquired company with debt that the company, not the owner, must carry. A platform takes early risk, then uses network dominance to collect rent from participants who have no realistic exit.
The moral question is never whether risk existed at some point. It is whether the continuing claim stays proportionate to continuing contribution and vulnerability.

This is one of the reasons repayment caps matter. A cap concedes that capital may deserve a return for its risk, its time, and its contribution — and then insists that the claim end, or at least transform, once it has been satisfied. Capital does not acquire infinite rights by having once been brave. Past risk does not justify perpetual extraction. The principle sits comfortably in the trajectory Olivi opened: if capital is legitimate because it participates in enterprise, its legitimacy lasts as long as the participation. When contribution and claim part company, the return loses its title.
Risk-bearing can serve life. Risk rhetoric can conceal extraction. Moral economy has to be able to tell them apart.

sections.title: Bernardino of Siena and Merchant Dignity
paragraphs: The tradition did not stop with Olivi. Bernardino of Siena (1380–1444), the most celebrated preacher of his century — he could fill Siena’s great shell-shaped Campo with listeners — carried the analysis a step further, and in a surprising direction: he developed a powerful account of the merchant’s dignity.

A Franciscan sticking up for merchants sounds like a category error. But Bernardino understood that commerce, rightly ordered, serves the common good. Merchants move goods, provide needed services, assume risks, employ workers, and coordinate economic life. Their work can be honorable — when it serves real needs and respects moral limits.

He did not simply bless profit. He distinguished good merchants from bad ones. The good merchant needed diligence, knowledge, reliability, prudence, truthfulness, and a concern for justice. Commerce, in other words, required more than cleverness; it required virtue. And the dignity it conferred did not come from wealth. It came from service. The merchant is dignified when he helps provision the community, and dangerous when he manipulates, hoards, deceives, exploits, or takes gain without proportionate service. Buying and selling — the very same activity — can be honorable or dishonorable, depending on what it is ordered toward. Economic roles are judged by purpose. The merchant is not good because he profits, and not bad because he profits. He is good when his profit corresponds to useful, just, and responsible service.

Substitute “founder” for “merchant” and Bernardino preaches remarkably well today. Entrepreneurship is often treated as a moral good in itself; founders are celebrated for disruption, scale, fundraising, ambition, and innovation. Moral economy asks Bernardino’s questions instead. What kind of entrepreneur? What kind of innovation, of disruption, of scale, of capital? What kind of dependence is being created, what kind of power concentrated, what kind of life served? A founder building useful tools, dignified work, resilient institutions, open infrastructure, or community-rooted enterprise stands in the line of merchant dignity. A founder building addiction, surveillance, manipulation, artificial scarcity, regulatory evasion, or dependency does not become morally legitimate simply by being entrepreneurial.

Commerce, in Bernardino’s telling, can be a vocation — but only while it remains accountable to the common good. Hold that thought; it returns when we reach the Protestants. The merchant, the entrepreneur, and the investor can all have real dignity. It comes from service, not from success.

sections.title: The Monti di Pietà and Ethical Banking
paragraphs: In 1462, in Perugia, Franciscan preaching helped bring into being one of the most important institutional experiments in the history of moral economy: the first Monte di Pietà, literally a “mount of piety” — a heap of money assembled for mercy’s sake.

The crisis it answered was old and painfully practical. The poor needed credit — for illness, unemployment, a failed harvest, a dowry, tools, rent, any of the ordinary emergencies of a fragile life — and the credit on offer was often ruinous. Without loans, households collapsed. With predatory loans, they were trapped.

The Monti attempted a third way: charitable lending institutions, religiously inspired and often publicly supported, making small loans against pledges at low cost. Their aim was not to maximize profit but to prevent exploitation, keep households intact, and give the poor an alternative to the usurer. They were financial institutions built around a moral purpose, and they spread through the towns of Italy.

Here moral economy becomes concrete. It was no longer enough to declare usury wrong; someone had to design a lender that met the same needs the predators met without becoming a predator itself. Moral critique had to become operational — statutes, governance, collateral rules, accounting systems, reserves, rates, audits, limits.

And the Monti promptly ran into the problem every non-extractive financial institution has faced since: the fund must survive in order to serve. A depleted fund helps no one. Unfunded administration collapses. Ignored defaults eat the capital base. Set the lending rate too high and the institution is morally compromised; set it too low and it is unsustainable. Make the collateral rules too strict and the poor are excluded; too loose and the fund fails. Weak governance invites insiders to help themselves. An unprotected mission drifts, quietly, toward ordinary banking. The Church itself eventually acknowledged the bind: the Fifth Lateran Council ruled in 1515 that a Monte could charge modest fees to cover its costs without committing usury — an official admission that good intentions need an operating budget.

This is the moral complexity of financial design. Life-serving finance cannot run on good intentions. It requires structure, a disciplined account of sustainability, moral accounting, and governance strong enough to resist both depletion and extraction.

For Non-Extractive Capital, the lesson could hardly be more direct. A fund that exists to serve productive enterprise must not destroy itself through naive generosity — and must not preserve itself into becoming indistinguishable from the extractive finance it was created to replace. It has to define legitimate return, necessary reserves, administrative costs, repayment discipline, loss absorption, and mission protection, and it has to do so transparently and accountably.

The Monti were imperfect. They could drift, be captured, and rationalize fees until they edged toward the practices they opposed. That is precisely why they are instructive. They exhibit the permanent challenge of non-extractive finance: how to preserve the fund without letting preservation become the justification for extraction.

sections.title: The Moral Problem of Sustainability
paragraphs: The Monti force an uncomfortable truth into view: sustainability can become the moral argument for mission drift.

The original logic is sound. A charitable or non-extractive fund must not be depleted; if it disappears, it serves no one. Charging enough to cover administrative costs, losses, and the preservation of capital may be morally legitimate — morally necessary, even. The poor are not served by an institution that collapses after one generation.

But watch the argument stretch. First the institution charges enough to survive. Then enough to build reserves. Then enough to expand. Then enough to compete. Then enough to attract capital. Then enough to satisfy stakeholders who have grown fond of surplus. And then the original mission is a story told about an institution whose real logic has changed.

The language shifts as the logic does. Prudence becomes growth. Sustainability becomes competitiveness. Administrative necessity becomes margin. The institution continues to serve — but service now reports to self-preservation and expansion.

This should sound familiar. A nonprofit bends toward funders whose priorities reshape its mission. A social enterprise signs growth terms inconsistent with its purpose. An ethical lender raises rates to scale faster. An impact fund adopts return expectations that quietly redefine impact. A platform built for community starts optimizing for engagement. An open-source company accepts capital that requires enclosure. A mission-driven firm sells to a buyer whose governance cannot hold the mission.

The Monti remind us that moral finance must guard not only against external enemies but against the internal logic of institutional survival. Survival is necessary. It is not the highest good. A fund exists for its mission, and if preserving the fund requires betraying the mission, the fund has become an idol of itself.

So non-extractive design needs explicit limits, and not only on returns. It needs caps on returns and caps on rationalizations; repayment formulas and mission tests; governance rights and governance duties; accounting for money and accounting for moral drift; reserves, and rules about why the reserves exist; sustainability, and clarity about what must never be done in its name.
The problem, as the Monti show, is ancient: how do you keep a life-serving financial institution from slowly learning to feed on life?

sections.title: Moral Accounting Before Modern Finance
paragraphs: The Monti reveal one more thing that modern finance often forgets: accounting is moral.

A ledger is not mere record-keeping. It is a way of seeing. It tells an institution what counts — what must be preserved, what may be spent, what is owed, what is surplus, what is loss, what requires explanation. Every ledger embodies a moral imagination.

Track only financial return, and you will see your borrowers one way. Track household preservation, avoided exploitation, the causes of default, collateral losses, administrative burden, and mission integrity, and you will see them quite differently. A fund that tracks only distributions to investors behaves differently from one that also tracks resilience reserves, founder control, worker stability, open-source contribution, dependency reduction, and community benefit. A company that tracks only revenue growth makes different decisions from one that also watches durability, mission fidelity, customer vulnerability, ecological cost, and governance health.

The Monti kept books because their mission demanded discipline. They needed to know whether the fund was being depleted; they tracked pledges, loans, repayments, losses, costs, and reserves. A morally serious ledger would ask one thing more: is the institution drifting toward extraction?

For a contemporary fund that claims to be non-extractive, the connection is direct. Branding and intention prove nothing; extraction risk has to be accounted for. Are repayment claims proportionate to productive surplus? Does the cash flow reflect durable enterprise, or pressure, underinvestment, wage suppression, and dependency? Are the portfolio companies becoming more resilient or more fragile? Is the capital being repaid from life-serving surplus, or from hidden harm?

The moral ledger cannot replace the financial ledger. But the financial ledger is incomplete without it. None of this requires a bureaucracy of ethical micromeasurement. It requires identifying the few moral distinctions central to the fund’s purpose and making them visible where decisions actually get made — in governance, reporting, and the papers that reach the board.

For the Monti, the key questions included: Is the fund preserved? Are the poor protected from usury? Are the costs justified? Is collateral handled fairly? Are the administrators trustworthy? Is the institution serving need without preying on it? For Non-Extractive Capital, the analogous questions might be: Is the company generating durable surplus? Do repayments come from genuine free cash flow rather than distress? Do the founder and steward body retain mission control? Are returns capped and proportionate? Is the enterprise becoming more resilient? Are workers, users, communities, and commons being strengthened rather than depleted? Does the capital structure protect the mission beyond the current leadership?
Accounting should make questions like these harder to evade. That is the moral meaning of a ledger.

sections.title: Productive Capital and the Common Good
paragraphs: The Franciscan contribution lets us say more precisely what productive capital means. It is not simply capital that generates profit — extractive capital does that too. Productive capital participates in the creation, preservation, or circulation of real goods, in a way that serves life and stays proportionate to its contribution. It is connected to real economic activity. It bears real risk or cost. It enables useful work. It respects the integrity of the people and institutions it funds. It accepts limits. It does not make vulnerability the basis of its position. It remains accountable to the common good.

That is a more demanding definition than the ordinary one, in which capital counts as productive whenever it increases output or return. Moral economy presses further: productive of what, for whom, and at what cost? A factory can produce goods while degrading its workers and its land. A platform can produce convenience while destroying local capacity and capturing dependency. A fund can produce returns while weakening the companies it owns. A company can produce growth by training its users into addiction. A housing portfolio can produce yield while shrinking the supply of homes. Production alone settles nothing.

Capital serves life when the productive activity itself is rightly ordered — which is why the common good remains essential. Capital cannot be evaluated from the investor’s chair alone; it has to be judged within the whole web of relationships it touches. Does it strengthen or weaken the community of production? Does it build durable capacity or take temporary extraction? Does it preserve future agency or consume it? Does it help households, workers, users, and institutions flourish? Does it honor limits?
The Franciscan tradition does not answer these questions in modern terms. But it presses us toward them, and it refuses — flatly — to let capital be judged by return alone.

sections.title: From Charity to Institution
paragraphs: Perhaps the most important movement in this chapter is the movement from charity to institution.

Charity is indispensable. There is no Christian moral economy without love of neighbor, care for the poor, almsgiving, mercy, and generosity. But charity alone cannot keep up with structures that produce recurring harm. If usurious lenders trap the poor, almsgiving arrives after the damage is done; a better lending institution prevents some of the damage in the first place. If unjust contracts exploit workers, charity relieves the suffering; better labor institutions change the terms of work. If founders face only extractive capital, philanthropy sustains a few alternatives; better financing structures change the options themselves. If open-source maintainers burn out while corporations harvest the value of their work, donations help; but it takes governance, procurement, funding, and licensing structures to preserve the commons.

The Monti embody the shift. They did not merely tell rich people to be generous. They built a financial mechanism that changed the credit options available to the poor. Moral concern took institutional form.

This is the heart of moral economy. A society ordered toward life cannot rely on heroic virtue. It must build structures that make justice ordinary — where the easier path is not reliably the extractive one — and it must make stewardship durable beyond the moods and personalities of individual stewards. That is likewise the promise of Steward Ownership, mission locks, evergreen funds, repayment caps, and non-extractive capital: they are attempts to institutionalize moral intention so that it does not evaporate under pressure.
Charity responds to need. Institution changes the conditions under which need arises. Both are necessary. But without institution, charity is forever repairing what extraction keeps breaking.

sections.title: The Franciscan Ambiguity
paragraphs: The Franciscan story is powerful, but it is not pure. No tradition enters the world of money and comes out untouched. The very act of designing morally disciplined finance creates temptations. Exceptions become loopholes. Sustainability becomes expansion. Administrative costs become margins. Low-interest lending edges toward the practices it was founded to resist. Moral language ends up legitimizing the drift it was meant to prevent.

The right response is not cynicism but vigilance. The lesson is not that ethical finance is impossible; it is that ethical finance must expect drift and design against it. It must assume every justification can be abused, and build the counter-measures in advance: audits, governance checks, transparency, caps, mission locks, practices of renewal. And it must keep returning to its founding questions. Whom are we serving? What are we preserving? What are we charging, and why? Are we protecting the vulnerable, or monetizing them? Are we preserving the fund for the mission, or preserving the mission as a story that protects the fund? Are we still an alternative to extraction, or a gentler form of it?

The Franciscans earn the right to pose such questions because their own tradition lived in tension: poverty and finance, renunciation and institutional design, charity and sustainability, moral purity and practical compromise, service to the poor and preservation of the fund. The tension is not a flaw in the story. It is the story. Moral economy lives in tension because human beings need institutions, and institutions are always corruptible. The answer is not to avoid institutions but to build institutions that know they are morally vulnerable.

sections.title: The Birth of Capital as a Moral Category
paragraphs: The title of this chapter speaks of the birth of capital, and the phrase needs care. Capital as a material fact is ancient. Tools, land, livestock, money, buildings, ships, roads, stored grain — accumulated capacity is as old as agriculture. What begins to emerge in Franciscan and late Scholastic thought is something different: capital as a moral category.

Capital is wealth placed into time — wealth ordered toward future activity. It can preserve, produce, circulate, and serve. It can also dominate, extract, enclose, and enslave. That is the breakthrough. Once capital is understood morally, we can ask not merely how much return it generates but what kind of relationship it creates. Does it participate in production, or claim tribute from it? Does it share risk, or impose it? Does it strengthen enterprise, or make enterprise dependent? Is its claim limited by contribution, or expanded by power? Does it serve the common good, or feed on vulnerability?

Everything that follows in this book stands on that foundation. Without a moral category of capital, finance goes opaque: money moves, returns are calculated, contracts are enforced, institutions grow — and the deeper question disappears. Is this capital serving life, or feeding on it?
The Franciscans would not let the question disappear. Neither will we.

sections.title: Toward Salamanca
paragraphs: The story now moves to a Spanish university town. The School of Salamanca will broaden the discussion: subtler accounts of subjective value, market price, supply and demand, money, inflation, and international trade. Its thinkers will see, more clearly than anyone before them, that value is not simply inherent in objects but shaped by human need, estimation, scarcity, and context — a long step toward what later economics will recognize as market coordination.

They will also introduce a deeper ambiguity. Salamanca reflected on markets from inside the age of empire. Questions of trade, conquest, indigenous rights, money, and global exchange could not be pulled apart, and moral economy would have to judge not only individual transactions but whole systems expanding across continents.

The Franciscans have taught us that capital can be productive when it is ordered toward service, risk, and real need. Salamanca will ask how markets coordinate value across a wider world — and whether market reasoning can remain morally truthful when it is embedded in conquest, extraction, and imperial power. The tension matters, because productive and extractive capital do not always appear as opposites. Sometimes they travel together. The same trade route carries provision and domination. The same financial instrument enables enterprise and exploitation. The same language of freedom conceals unequal power.

Moral economy must therefore become more than a theory of fair exchange. It must learn to discern whole systems. The Franciscans brought capital under moral scrutiny. Salamanca will bring the market world into view.

chapters.title: Chapter 6: Salamanca: Market Coordination and Moral Ambiguity

paragraphs: If the Franciscans sharpened moral distinctions around productive capital, the School of Salamanca broadened the horizon of economic reasoning — and it did so from inside one of the strangest economic experiments the world had yet run.

Sixteenth-century Spain was the receiving end of history’s first great commodity flood. Every year the fleets came up the Guadalquivir to Seville carrying American silver, and every year, mysteriously, life in Spain grew more expensive. Prices across the century rose as they had never risen in living memory — the episode historians call the Price Revolution — and nobody could say quite why. Meanwhile Spain’s theologians were being asked questions no confessor’s manual covered: about exchange dealings between Seville and Antwerp, about the legitimacy of conquest, about the status of peoples across an ocean whom Aristotle had never heard of. At the University of Salamanca — already three centuries old when the silver started arriving — a remarkable succession of Dominican and Jesuit thinkers took these questions on: Francisco de Vitoria, who held the prime chair of theology from 1526; his student Domingo de Soto; the jurist Diego de Covarrubias; the canon lawyer Martín de Azpilcueta, known as Doctor Navarrus; the merchant-manual writer Tomás de Mercado; the Jesuit Luis de Molina, and others in their orbit.

Where earlier Scholastics and Franciscans had focused on the ethics of individual transactions — this loan, this price, this risk — Salamanca brought attention to markets as systems of coordination: a way of understanding not only whether a single exchange is just, but how a whole network of exchanges organizes resources across space and time.

Salamanca did not invent modern economics. It assumed no perfect competition, no atomized agents, no mathematical abstraction. But it introduced concepts that anticipate subjective value, supply and demand, monetary theory, and the interplay of trade and uncertainty — and, more importantly for our story, it kept all of them embedded in moral reasoning, connecting price, fairness, and human welfare in a single frame. It also thought inside an empire, and that fact will matter enormously before this chapter is done.

sections.title: Subjective Value and Market Price
paragraphs: The Scholastics had long debated the just price; Aquinas had anchored it in proportionality and fairness. Salamanca refined the concept by taking seriously something the tradition had been circling for centuries: value is, in large part, subjective. The same good has different value to different people, at different times, in different places. Value is not a property sealed inside the object like a pit in a plum; it arises from human judgment, desire, scarcity, and need.

The puzzle was ancient. Augustine had noticed, with some perplexity, that the market’s valuations track nothing in the order of nature: a horse could fetch more than a slave, a jewel more than a servant girl — prices that would be incomprehensible if value lived in the intrinsic dignity of things. A living mouse outranks gold in the hierarchy of being; nobody trades their gold for mice. For centuries the observation sat there as an oddity. The friars inherited a useful triad from Olivi and Bernardino for making sense of it — a good’s value reflects its usefulness, its scarcity, and its pleasingness, how much it is esteemed — and the Salamancans pressed the point to its logical conclusion. Covarrubias put it with lawyerly bluntness: the value of a thing depends on the estimation of men, even when that estimation is foolish. Wheat is not “worth” some fixed amount by nature. It is worth what the common estimation of the market — the communis aestimatio — judges it to be worth, here, now, among these people with these needs. The old puzzle dissolved: prices had never been measuring the cosmic order. They measure human valuation, which is exactly why they can coordinate human affairs — and exactly why they cannot, by themselves, judge them.

This insight carries two crucial implications, and the stub of moral economy’s whole later history is folded inside them. First, price is relational: it reflects the intersection of human valuation, utility, and circumstance. A shoe is worth more to a traveler in winter than to a cobbler with a stockpile; grain is worth more in a famine than in a surplus. Second, justice is contextual: a just price must account for relative need, opportunity, and social conditions. A merchant who profits from foresight and risk-bearing supply may be acting justly. A merchant who profits by exploiting desperation is not — even if he charges the same number.

Notice what this is and is not. Recognizing subjective value is a major step toward understanding markets as moral systems of dispersed coordination — thousands of private judgments aggregating into public prices. It is not a license for whatever the market will bear. The same thinkers who described the communis aestimatio also condemned the manipulation of it: cornering supplies, colluding on prices, engineering the scarcity you then charge for. The estimation of the community can be genuine, or it can be rigged, and the moral difference between the two was, for Salamanca, the whole point.

sections.title: Supply, Demand, and Market Dynamics
paragraphs: From subjective value, the Salamancans worked their way toward what we would now call supply-and-demand reasoning. Scarcity drives price. Desire and need shape willingness to pay. The availability of substitutes, the risk of spoilage, the pressure of time — all of it moves exchange. They observed these regularities not as laws of physics but as patterns of human estimation, grounded in watching actual markets: the grain exchanges, the wool trade, the fairs, the money markets that Mercado described for the merchants of Seville in plain Castilian.

And because they were moral theologians, the observation immediately raised questions the modern textbook declines to ask. If demand creates extreme pressure, how should the just price respond? May merchants exploit scarcity to maximize gain? Should price reflect effort and risk? Should profit be tempered to prevent harm? Their answers ran along the line already drawn: price may move with genuine scarcity and genuine cost, but necessity is not a revenue opportunity, and the seller who bids up bread because the buyer is starving has left the realm of exchange for the realm of extortion.

Here the Salmantine tradition extends the Franciscan one. The moral problem is no longer only the intent of the individual merchant or the fairness of a single transaction. It is coordination: how the system of exchanges — the whole humming network — distributes goods, risks, and opportunities across a society, and who ends up carrying what.

sections.title: Money, Inflation, and the Global Economy
paragraphs: It was money, though, that drew Salamanca’s most original work — understandably, given that Spain was conducting an uncontrolled monetary experiment on itself.

The Salamancans saw clearly that money has no intrinsic value: it is a conventional measure, valuable because a community treats it as such. From there they reached a genuinely new insight. Azpilcueta, writing in 1556 — a dozen years before the French jurist Jean Bodin, who usually gets the credit — observed that the purchasing power of money depends on its abundance. Where money is plentiful, as in silver-soaked Spain, everything priced in it grows dear; where money is scarce, it buys more. This is the quantity theory of money in recognizable form, derived not from equations but from watching prices climb as the fleets came in. And it explained the century’s great riddle: Spain was not being punished by fate; it was being repriced by its own treasure.

The same principle illuminated the foreign-exchange business. If money is worth more where it is scarce, then exchanging currencies across places is not sleight of hand; real differences of value are being traded, and the banker’s margin may correspond to something genuine. With that, a whole category of commerce that blanket usury-talk had shadowed became morally analyzable — and, in the right conditions, defensible.

But the analysis never floated free of ethics. A rise in prices caused by coin debasement was not, to Salamanca, a merely technical event. When a king clipped his coinage or a windfall of silver silently shrank the value of wages and savings, the losses landed on people — disproportionately the poor, whose livelihoods were denominated in the debased coin and who held no assets that rose with the tide. An unjust price could be imposed retail, one bargain at a time, or wholesale, through the currency itself. Money, they understood, is a tool; mismanaged or manipulated, it becomes an instrument of extraction operating on everyone at once.

The Salamancans also watched money-morality play out at sovereign scale.
