Economics became less comfortable asking what economic life is for, and more comfortable asking how systems behave, how preferences are satisfied, how resources are allocated, how growth occurs, how markets reach equilibrium. Real questions, all of them. Narrower questions, too.

The result was the great separation: the gradual uncoupling of economic analysis from the older language of virtue, teleology, theology, justice, stewardship, and the common good. It was not total, not simple, and not the work of any one thinker, school, or conspiracy. It happened through many developments at once — the rise of political economy as a discipline, the success of mathematical modeling, the hunger for scientific status, the growth of industrial capitalism, the influence of utilitarianism, professionalization, the secularization of public reasoning, and the honest desire to separate what is from what ought to be.
The separation brought gains. It also brought losses. This chapter is about both.

sections.title: Economics Breaks from Theology
paragraphs: For most of the tradition we have followed, economic reasoning lived inside a wider moral order. Aristotle placed household management under ethics and politics; Christian moralists placed wealth under charity, mercy, sin, and salvation; the Scholastics judged exchange by commutative justice; the Franciscans asked whether capital served production or exploited need; Salamanca studied markets without releasing them from conscience; Protestant thinkers brought vocation, stewardship, and Sabbath into economic life; Smith held political economy and moral psychology in one mind. Economics was not yet autonomous.

That did not make the older reasoning automatically better. The old frameworks could be rigid, partial, hierarchical, blind to complexity. Theological condemnation sometimes outran institutional understanding; moralists denounced markets without grasping coordination; rulers dressed paternalism and privilege in moral language; religious authorities misread commercial practices that genuinely served human need. So the move toward an independent economic science had honest motives. Economic systems are complex. Prices move for reasons no single moralist can see. Regulations backfire; good intentions produce scarcity, black markets, and dependency; incentives matter even when motives are noble; institutions must be judged by their effects, not their aspirations. Economics became powerful precisely because it saw some things more clearly once it loosened the inherited categories.

But the loosening became a detachment. In a plural, secularizing world, theologians lost shared public authority; philosophy itself was contested; economics went looking for firmer ground in observation, logic, mathematics, and prediction, and increasingly split positive questions from normative ones — what is, rather than what ought to be.

The distinction has real uses. You must know what is happening before deciding what to do; a physician who misdiagnoses cannot heal; an economist who misunderstands incentives cannot design just institutions. Moral economy needs empirical seriousness. But the positive/normative divide can also mislead, because economic description is never wholly innocent. What we choose to measure, model, assume, and ignore already encodes judgments about what matters. A model that takes preferences as given has bracketed the formation of desire. A framework that books labor as a cost has chosen a standpoint from which to see work. A theory that treats nature as an input has made a claim about creation. A theory of the firm that installs shareholder value as the organizing purpose has not avoided teleology — it has picked one.

When economics broke from theology, it did not become free of ultimate questions. It became less explicit about them. The danger was never that economics became analytical. The danger is that it forgot that analysis always happens inside a moral horizon.

sections.title: Utilitarianism and the New Moral Arithmetic
paragraphs: One of the great engines of the separation was utilitarianism — the moral system Jeremy Bentham launched with the cheerful confidence of a man who believed ethics could be done with a calculator. Bentham even sketched the method, a “felicific calculus” for summing pleasures and pains. Instead of grounding judgment in virtue, natural law, divine command, or the common good, utilitarianism proposed judging actions and institutions by their consequences for happiness or utility. Moral reasoning became calculable, public, and apparently democratic: no contested theology required, just the greatest good for the greatest number.

The strengths were real. Utilitarianism challenged inherited privilege; it asked whether institutions actually improved human well-being rather than merely preserving tradition; it exposed cruelty hidden behind custom; it powered reforms in law, prisons, public health, and poverty relief. It forced moral reasoning to care about consequences at scale — a discipline the older traditions sometimes lacked.

But it also flattened the moral world. Once the good becomes a quantity to aggregate, other questions grow harder to ask. What may never be done, even for aggregate benefit? Which dignities cannot be traded off? Which goods are incommensurable? What is owed to particular persons and communities, not populations? What happens when the pleasures being counted were themselves formed by unjust systems?

The flattening matters for economics because utility quietly replaced richer accounts of flourishing. Human beings became bearers of preferences; the economy became a machine for satisfying them; and if a choice raises measured welfare, the question of whether the desire behind it is well ordered becomes almost unaskable. This is not because utilitarians were unserious — many were profoundly serious. It is that the framework converts moral reasoning into calculation while losing formation, virtue, domination, sacred limits, and the qualitative differences between goods. A society can maximize preference satisfaction while training people to prefer what harms them; it can raise welfare while normalizing dependency; it can justify extraction whenever total output comes out higher; it can process persons as units in a sum.

And utilitarianism has particular trouble with the category this book keeps returning to: enough. If more utility is always better, limits must justify themselves by their consequences. Sabbath, restraint, and sufficiency lose their intrinsic force — defensible, perhaps, as long-run welfare strategies, but no longer standing witnesses that some things must not be subordinated to production and gain. The result is rarely cruelty; more often it is humane managerialism. But humane managerialism cannot answer the founding question. It can tell us how to increase welfare as the system defines it. It cannot tell us what kind of creatures we are, what goods deserve love, or which forms of economic life deform the soul even while satisfying preferences.

sections.title: Industrial Capitalism and the Abstraction of Labor
paragraphs: The great separation was not only an affair of ideas. It was built in brick, iron, and steam.

Industrial capitalism transformed the scale and organization of economic life. Production moved into factories; labor became specialized, disciplined, measured, and supervised; workers were separated from their tools, their land, and household production; time became wage time; cities swelled; capital requirements soared; ownership and labor split into opposing camps; and the firm became a site of coordination, hierarchy, and conflict.

Economic life turned abstract because economic relations turned mediated. The worker no longer saw the final product; the owner no longer knew the workers; the consumer never met the maker; the lender never met the borrower; the shareholder held a claim on an enterprise without any relationship to its place, craft, people, or purpose. The economy became larger, more productive — and more anonymous.

The material gains were immense: output, cheaper goods, infrastructure, and eventually vast improvements in health, mobility, literacy, and security. So were the miseries: exploitation, urban squalor, dangerous and deadening labor, child labor, ecological damage, class war.

And the older moral vocabulary strained to keep up. Aquinas could reason about a buyer and a seller; the Franciscans about a lender and a borrower; Smith about a workshop and a town. Industrial capitalism created systems in which responsibility itself went missing. When wages are too low to live on, who exactly is the sinner? The employer? The market? The consumer, the investor, the law, the worker’s lack of alternatives, the structure of property, the monetary system, the whole industrial order? Harm no longer lived inside a single unjust transaction. It arose from the structure of production itself.

The abstraction of labor made extraction easier to hide. Treat labor as a cost, and the worker appears as an input. Measure productivity as output per unit of labor, and the person disappears behind the ratio. View the firm as a production function, and vocation, dignity, and formation drop out of the equation. Turn ownership into a tradable financial claim, and the enterprise stops being a community of persons and becomes an asset.

The moral economy tradition had always insisted that economic life consists of human acts in relation to other humans. Industrial capitalism made the relations hard to see — and modernity has kept perfecting the trick. A supply chain delivers cheap goods while hiding coerced labor. A financial instrument generates yield while hiding household distress. A platform delivers convenience while hiding surveillance and invisible work; a fund posts returns while hiding the fragility imposed on its portfolio; a consumer enjoys low prices without ever glimpsing their conditions. Systems become morally opaque as they become technically powerful. A moral economy must learn to re-personalize what industrial and financial systems depersonalize.

sections.title: The Abstraction of Value
paragraphs: Alongside the abstraction of labor came the abstraction of value.

Older moral economy thought about value in relation to use, need, justice, custom, contribution, and common estimation. Salamanca recognized subjective valuation, but inside a moral world; Smith distinguished use value from exchange value while holding economics and moral philosophy together. Modern economics — especially after the marginal revolution of the 1870s, when Jevons, Menger, and Walras rebuilt value theory on marginal utility — sought ever more formal and general accounts. Understandably so: explaining prices across countless goods and conditions requires abstraction, and value clearly involves subjective judgment, scarcity, margins, and opportunity costs.

But abstraction becomes dangerous when it forgets what it abstracts from. In practice, value in economic analysis becomes what shows up as willingness to pay, market price, utility, productivity, or return. That enables analysis — and narrows attention. Goods that resist pricing become hard to see; externalized harms sit outside the model; forms of value requiring time, relationship, or common life get discounted; the old distinction between price and worth becomes almost impossible to maintain.

The consequences are everywhere. A forest holds value as timber, carbon, recreation, biodiversity, water regulation, sacred meaning, Indigenous stewardship, and the inheritance of future generations; a model can price a few of these, but something essential is lost when all value must enter as monetized preference. A home is shelter, memory, neighborhood, stability, continuity, place — and a housing market values it as an asset, and when asset value wins, the home’s meaning is deformed. A company is a community of work, a provider, a craft tradition, a public dependency, sometimes a mission-bearing institution — and the financial market values it as discounted future cash flows, a measure that is not meaningless but that, made sovereign, renders every other kind of value defenseless. A human being appears as worker, consumer, data source, debtor, user, patient, risk profile. Each category captures something. None captures the person.

The abstraction of value makes coordination easier — and domination easier. Once value is translated into a single metric, whatever resists the metric can be reorganized, traded, optimized, or sacrificed. Capital prefers commensurability, because commensurable things are comparable, liquid, and investable. But not all goods should be liquid, and not all worth can be faithfully translated into price. This is how capital turns extractive while remaining perfectly legal and efficient: it sees what its metrics let it see. Count cash flows and ignore mission drift, and mission becomes invisible. Count engagement and ignore addiction, and addiction becomes growth. Count rental yield and ignore displacement, and displacement becomes someone else’s problem. The great separation is partly the story of metrics becoming masters.

sections.title: The Rise of Homo Economicus
paragraphs: The most famous mascot of modern economic abstraction is homo economicus — the rational, self-interested maximizer of utility under constraints. He is often attacked, sometimes unfairly. As a simplification he can be useful: people do respond to incentives, do make tradeoffs, do pursue their interests, and no model can include everything. In 1932 Lionel Robbins gave the discipline its enduring self-definition — the science that studies human behavior as a relationship between given ends and scarce means — and the word doing the quiet work in that sentence is “given.” The ends are handed in at the door. Economics studies the means.

The trouble starts when the model becomes an anthropology. Human beings are not preference-maximizers; they are lovers, worshippers, parents, children, neighbors, citizens, workers, debtors, founders, stewards, sinners, saints, and fools. They seek meaning, honor, belonging, beauty, truth, forgiveness, and God. They act from habit, fear, love, shame, duty, loyalty, resentment, hope, and imitation. They frequently do not know what they want; they prefer what harms them; they sacrifice for others; they deceive themselves; they can be trained into virtue or vice. Smith knew all of this. So did Aristotle, the Christian moralists, and the theologians of vocation. Homo economicus brackets nearly all of it.

Bracketing has its place — it buys analytical focus. But bracket too much for too long and you begin to miseducate the imagination. People trained to see economic actors as self-interested maximizers design institutions that assume and reward maximizing — and those institutions then form people who behave more like the model. The simplification becomes performative. Assume investors want only maximum return, and funds are engineered around that appetite. Assume managers act opportunistically, and compensation and control systems follow. Assume workers shirk, and surveillance grows; assume consumers are preference-satisfiers, and preference manipulation becomes a business plan; assume founders seek exit, and venture structures push toward exit; assume the firm exists for shareholder value, and every other purpose becomes structurally fragile. The model becomes the world.

A moral economy must break this feedback loop — not with fantasies of angelic agency (human beings are self-interested, and institutions must reckon with sin, weakness, and temptation), but with a richer account of the person and institutions that call forth better agency. The older traditions were wrong about many things. They never forgot that human beings require formation. Modern economics bought predictive clarity by abstracting from formation — and thereby risked manufacturing the very actors its abstractions described.

sections.title: The Case for Separation
paragraphs: Before judging the great separation too harshly, honesty requires making its case — because the case is strong.

The older moral economies had real failures. They could be paternalistic, static, hierarchical, anti-commercial, suspicious of innovation, blind to the benefits of coordination. They protected insiders and guilds in the language of justice; they condemned practices they did not understand; they confused custom with morality; they sacralized unjust social orders. Economic analysis needed room to breathe — to observe how prices actually move, to study incentives without first condemning self-interest, to show that restrictions justified by moral language could starve the poor they claimed to protect, to expose the unintended consequences of well-meaning intervention.

The separation also eased public reasoning in plural societies. When citizens disagree about theology, virtue, and the highest good, policy cannot wait for metaphysical consensus. Economics offered a shared language of incentives, costs, benefits, tradeoffs, and outcomes — useful then, useful now.

So let it be said plainly: a recovered moral economy should not discard modern economics. Scholastic categories alone will not run central banking, antitrust, climate transition, startup finance, or global trade. Righteous sentiment is not analysis. The great separation taught disciplines that moral seriousness alone never could: that intentions are not outcomes, that scarcity is real, that incentives matter, that coordination is hard, that decentralized knowledge counts, that prices carry information, that tradeoffs cannot be wished away, that policy must be judged by results. These are genuine gains, and a moral economy that ignores them will fail.

sections.title: Why the Gains Were Not Worth the Losses
paragraphs: And yet the price was too high — because the gains came bundled with a forgetting.

The problem was never that economics became analytical; it was that analysis came detached from purpose. Not that economists studied incentives, but that incentive analysis bracketed the formation of desire. Not that prices were understood as information, but that price became a substitute for judgment; not that self-interest was recognized, but that it was promoted to anthropology; not that growth was studied, but that growth became telos.

The older question did not disappear because it was answered. It disappeared because the discipline stopped being willing to ask it. What is an economy for? What kinds of people does it form, what desires does it cultivate, what limits should it honor? Which forms of value must not be made liquid, which profits are dishonorable, what duties ride with property? When does return become extraction, when does voluntary exchange conceal domination, when does capital stop serving enterprise and begin ruling it?

These questions were exiled to ethics, politics, religion, sociology, personal opinion. But economic life never stopped answering them. It answered them through institutions, metrics, and capital structures. If shareholder value governs the firm, that is an answer. If GDP governs public success, that is an answer. If exit value governs startup finance, engagement governs platforms, liquidity governs investment, and willingness to pay governs access to essentials — those are answers, every one. The danger is that they present themselves as technical when they are moral.

That is why the losses were so serious. The great separation let capital present its claims as neutral, inevitable, scientific. It made it easier to hide extraction behind efficiency, to treat economic power as a market outcome rather than a moral relation, to file institutional design under engineering rather than ethics, to forget that the economy is a human order and not a natural force. The gains of economics should have been integrated into moral economy. Instead, too often, they displaced it.

sections.title: The Hidden Normativity of Neutrality
paragraphs: Modern economics likes to present itself as positive rather than normative: it analyzes, predicts, evaluates tradeoffs, and leaves judgment to others. There is wisdom in the humility — economists should not mistake technical expertise for authority over the good life, and they should say clearly when they are describing and when prescribing.

But neutrality can become a mask. Assumptions are not neutral; metrics are not neutral; the decision to treat some things as variables and others as externalities is not neutral. Modeling the firm as a nexus of contracts rather than a community of work is a choice. Treating nature as input, labor as cost, and capital as claimant is a choice. Measuring welfare as preference satisfaction is a choice. Even the refusal to speak morally has moral consequences: decline to judge whether an outcome is just, and the outcome stands; decline to ask whether preferences were manipulated, and preference remains the basis of analysis; decline to ask whether bargaining conditions were coercive, and consent stays formal; decline to ask whether capital’s claim is proportionate, and the return books itself as a market fact.

Neutrality, in practice, stabilizes the existing order. This does not make economists villains or economics propaganda. It means any discipline that studies human action must be honest about its moral boundaries. Economics can say a great deal about means. But when it refuses to discuss ends, the ends do not evaporate — they default to whoever has the power to build them into institutions. In modern capitalism, that power usually belongs to capital. Once moral language is expelled from the core of economic reasoning, capital defines success in its own dialect: return becomes performance, liquidity becomes efficiency, growth becomes health, exit becomes success, scale becomes importance, market price becomes truth. The question of whether capital serves life becomes almost impossible to ask in authoritative language. Moral economy must expose the hidden normativity — without surrendering the analytical rigor.

sections.title: What Was Lost: Teleology
paragraphs: The deepest loss was teleology.

The older thought, for all its faults, asked what economic activity was ordered toward. The household was for life; the city for the good life; property for use and stewardship; exchange for mutual need; lending for assistance or productive participation; work for vocation and service; wealth for the support of virtue, charity, household, and common good. Modern economics avoids such claims. It asks what individuals want, not whether wants are worthy; how firms maximize, not what firms are for; how markets clear, not whether the goods traded should be governed by market logic at all; whether the economy grows, not whether the growth serves flourishing.

The loss of teleology produces a specific confusion: confusion about limits. If you do not know what a thing is for, you cannot know when it has enough. A hospital ordered toward healing can tell when financial pressure is undermining care. A school ordered toward education can tell when metrics are distorting learning; a farm ordered toward stewardship can tell when yield is destroying land; a company ordered toward mission can tell when growth endangers purpose; a fund ordered toward non-extractive finance can tell when returns have become disproportionate. But if the end is simply more — more growth, more return, more engagement, more scale — then every limit looks irrational.

Teleology gives us the category of enough. Without it, capital tends toward infinity: always more return to seek, more data to capture, more attention to monetize, more assets to sweat, more costs to cut, more dependencies to exploit — and the system goes on calling this progress, because it has no language left for disorder.

A recovered moral economy must therefore recover ends — not simplistically, not authoritarianly. Plural societies will not agree on ultimate questions. But they can still ask what institutions are for; they can still tell shelter from speculation, healthcare from extraction, education from credential markets, infrastructure from enclosure, enterprise from liquidation, stewardship from domination. The question of ends is unavoidable. The only choice is whether we ask it openly.

sections.title: What Was Lost: Moral Limits on Capital
paragraphs: The second loss was a moral language of limits on capital.

The older traditions disagreed about much, but they shared a suspicion of gain without service, accumulation without limit, and lending that profits from need. They knew money was dangerous because it could detach from life; they feared usury, avarice, hoarding, monopoly, unjust price, and wealth without responsibility.

Modern finance, meanwhile, became history’s most ingenious manufacturer of claims. Debt, equity, derivatives, securitization, leverage, management fees, carried interest, preferred shares, liquidation preferences, convertibles, platform rents, IP portfolios, data extraction — all of them claims on future value. Some serve life: they fund production, distribute risk, support innovation, coordinate capital across time. But without a moral account of limits, claims multiply past all contribution. Capital demands return long after its risk has been compensated; converts temporary support into permanent control; uses legal form to shed responsibility while keeping upside; treats every future cash flow as something to be owned today. It colonizes the future.

The old usury debates were not technically adequate to modern finance. But they preserved the question we still need: when does a financial claim become morally excessive? Modern economics can answer only in terms of market failure, monopoly, information asymmetry, externality, or illegality — useful categories, and insufficient. A claim can be legal, competitive, informed, and formally voluntary, and still be extractive, if it arises from structural necessity or imposes disproportionate control.

What moral economy needs is the concept the whole tradition has been circling since Aquinas: proportionality. Return should correspond to contribution, risk, time, service, and responsibility — not run to infinity merely because a contract permits it. Financial claims should remain subordinate to the life of the enterprise and the common good. This is why repayment caps, steward ownership, evergreen finance, and mission-locked governance are more than alternative financial products. They are attempts to restore moral limits to capital. They say: capital may serve, but it may not become sovereign.

sections.title: What Was Lost: The Moral Meaning of the Firm
paragraphs: The third loss was the moral meaning of the firm.

Older moral economy understood economic activity through households, crafts, guilds, estates, and communities. The modern firm is something new: a legal, financial, managerial, and productive institution coordinating labor, capital, knowledge, contracts, and strategy across time — and one of the most powerful moral structures in modern life. It shapes work, technology, land, wages, supply chains, politics, culture, and ecology. It forms habits, distributes power, and decides what gets built, who is rewarded, who is exposed, and what counts as success.

Yet modern economic theory struggles to give the firm any rich moral meaning. Seen as a nexus of contracts, its relational and communal character fades. Seen as an asset owned by shareholders, its workers, customers, suppliers, communities, and mission become secondary. Seen as a profit-maximizing production unit, its formative and civic roles disappear; seen as a vehicle for investor return, enterprise is simply subordinated to capital.

But a firm is not a contract bundle. It is a community of production under governance. Not a family, not a church, not a state — it has its own limited purpose — but within that purpose it is a real human institution. People give it years of their lives; communities depend on it; customers trust it; knowledge accumulates inside it; and its governance decides whether its mission survives pressure. So the moral question of the firm cannot be dodged: what is this enterprise for, and who has the authority to decide when financial return conflicts with that purpose?

The great separation filed this under ownership and contract — technical matters. It is moral architecture. Shareholder primacy is not merely a governance theory; it is a teleology of the firm. It answers “what is the company for?” with “shareholder value,” and even when softened by stakeholder rhetoric, the answer stays welded into law, compensation, fiduciary culture, capital markets, and exit expectations. A recovered moral economy must contest that answer. Not every firm should share one purpose — but no firm is morally neutral, and if firms shape human life, their governance must be judged by more than efficiency and return. The firm must become a site of stewardship again; the later chapters on the corporation, steward ownership, and non-extractive capital take up exactly that task.

sections.title: What Was Lost: The Commons
paragraphs: The fourth loss was the commons.

Older societies maintained many forms of shared use, customary right, communal obligation, and common good. These arrangements were not always just or efficient, but they preserved an idea: not everything necessary for life should be treated as alienable private property or state-administered resource. Modern economic thought simplified the landscape into market and state, private and public — and the commons became invisible.

The invisibility matters, because many of the goods most necessary for flourishing are commons or commons-like: language, knowledge, culture, ecological systems, public trust, scientific research, civic institutions, open-source software, digital protocols, public health, a stable climate, shared infrastructure. None of these is well understood as private property or state possession. They require stewardship.

The great separation stripped away the language needed to defend them. Where value is recognized only when priced, owned, or traded, a commons registers as inefficiency, free resource, or under-monetized asset — at which point capital sees opportunity: enclose, meter, license, extract, securitize, platformize, monetize. Not always maliciously; sometimes private enterprise maintains shared resources well, sometimes state management fails, sometimes commons genuinely need governance discipline. But the loss of commons language makes enclosure look natural and stewardship look naive.

The urgency is now digital. Open-source software, public data, protocols, research, and shared infrastructure are forms of common productive capacity. Governed as commons, they serve life. Left undefended, they are captured — by platforms, cloud providers, AI companies, proprietary ecosystems. The commons becomes training data; community labor becomes product input; public knowledge becomes a private moat. Against this pattern the whole tradition we have traced stands ready: Aristotle asking whether shared goods serve life or accumulation, Christian thought holding ownership accountable to use and need, the Scholastics judging exchange by justice, the Franciscans designing institutions to protect the vulnerable, Protestant stewardship preserving entrusted goods, Smith warning against concentrated power. A recovered moral economy must bring the commons back into view.

sections.title: From Separation to Sovereignty
paragraphs: The great separation did not just produce an academic discipline. It produced the conditions under which capital could become sovereign.

When economics no longer asks what capital is for, capital answers for itself. When firms are defined by shareholder value, capital acquires governing priority. When prices are treated as sufficient signals of value, capital can buy what should be stewarded; when human beings are modeled as preference-maximizers, capital can manufacture preferences and call the result demand; when moral limits appear external to economic logic, capital treats them as constraints to optimize around. Capital becomes sovereign not by declaring itself king but by becoming the default measure of institutional success.

Look around at the sovereignty. Universities reorganize around funding, rankings, and market relevance; hospitals around billing systems and throughput; cities around real-estate value and tourism; media around attention monetization; technology around data capture and platform control; startups around valuation and exit; public infrastructure grows dependent on private capital and proprietary systems; politics answers to donors, growth narratives, and investor confidence. Money is not the only force in modern life — culture, politics, religion, law, and personal virtue still matter. But capital has become uniquely powerful because so many institutions must now justify themselves in its language.

The older tradition had labored to keep capital subordinate — to household, city, virtue, charity, justice, vocation, common good, and God. The great separation made subordination hard even to articulate, and capital could then appear as neutral resource, rational allocator, objective judgment, necessary discipline. Part IV of this book examines what happens as the process deepens: what extraction means, how capital detaches from productive service, how corporations became sovereign, and why financialized capitalism produces crises that growth alone cannot solve.

But the central result of the great separation should be named before we go there. Modern economics became better at explaining how economic systems work, and worse at asking whether they are rightly ordered.

sections.title: The Task After Separation
paragraphs: We cannot undo the great separation by pretending it never happened. There is no way back behind modern economics, industrial capitalism, monetary theory, statistical analysis, central banking, corporate law, digital technology, or pluralist politics — and no reason to want one. The analytical gains are real, and moral economy needs them.

The task is not reversal. It is reintegration. Economic analysis disciplined by moral purpose; moral theology and philosophy informed by economic reality; institutional design that understands incentives without worshipping them; markets embedded in law and culture without being smothered; finance that supports risk and production without converting everything into extractive claim; firms that coordinate complex work without being reduced to shareholder instruments; metrics that serve judgment rather than replace it. What we need, in short, is a moral economy after economics — not before it, and not against it.

Such a moral economy must be bilingual. It must speak capital structures, incentives, governance, prices, risk, and coordination — and it must speak purpose, justice, vocation, limits, stewardship, and the common good. Speaking only the first language, it will be captured by the system it hopes to reform. Speaking only the second, it will remain beautiful and ineffective.

The great separation divided what must now be brought back into conversation. That conversation begins by naming what extraction is — for if capital is to serve life again, we must first learn to recognize when it no longer does.
We have arrived at the hinge of the book.

Everything to this point has been recovery work — the reassembly of a language. Aristotle gave us the distinction between an economy ordered toward life and accumulation without limit. Christianity added that wealth turns spiritually dangerous the moment it comes loose from charity, mercy, and love of neighbor. The Scholastics gave us commutative justice — proportionality and right relation as the tests of exchange. The Franciscans showed that capital can be productive when it participates in real risk, foresight, and service, and that finance must be designed so it does not prey upon the vulnerable. Salamanca showed markets coordinating value across time and place — and market reasoning traveling in the company of empire and domination. The Protestants dignified work as vocation and wealth as stewardship, warning that discipline without Sabbath curdles into accumulation without rest. Adam Smith demonstrated that commercial society runs on moral sentiments and that wealth corrupts when it draws admiration without virtue. And the great separation taught us what economics gained by becoming more analytical — and what it lost by becoming less able to ask what economic life is for.
Now we have to name the thing all these traditions were circling.
Extraction.

The word gets heavy use, and not always careful use. It can slide into a slogan — a name for whatever the speaker dislikes: profit, capitalism, markets, growth, finance, technology, corporations, inequality, ecological destruction, colonialism, rent, debt, exploitation. Some of these are connected to extraction. None is identical with it in every case.

A concept stretched that wide stops doing work. If everything is extraction, nothing can be distinguished: commerce becomes extraction, then profit, investment, rent, interest, and growth, and the word has dwindled into a mood. Moral economy depends on distinction. It must be able to say not merely “this is wrong” but why this kind of gain differs from legitimate service, risk-bearing, production, stewardship, or exchange.
Begin with what extraction is not.

It is not profit. Profit is legitimate when it corresponds to real contribution — service, risk, labor, skill, coordination, stewardship. Extraction begins when gain detaches from proportionate contribution and is obtained instead through dependency, domination, enclosure, scarcity, opacity, or the sheer asymmetry of power.

Nor is it markets. Markets serve life when they coordinate useful activity, reward real service, reveal need, and let people cooperate freely. Extraction begins when the market form conceals coercion — when choice is structured by necessity, competition gives way to capture, or exchange becomes a machine for converting vulnerability into gain.

It is not growth, either. Growth serves life when it means more food, shelter, health, knowledge, resilience, beauty, and shared capacity. Extraction begins when growth is purchased by depleting people, communities, ecosystems, institutions, or future possibility.

And it is not finance. Finance serves life when it enables productive activity across time, shares risk, preserves mission, and accepts limits. Extraction begins when the financial claims come to dominate the very activity they were meant to support.

The task of this chapter is to give extraction a working definition — not a final metaphysical definition, not a formula that settles every case, but a moral-economic diagnostic: a way of recognizing when capital has stopped serving life and begun feeding on it.

sections.title: A Working Definition
paragraphs: Here it is. Extraction is the appropriation of value — from persons, communities, institutions, ecosystems, or productive enterprises — in ways that are disproportionate to contribution and sustained by dependency, domination, enclosure, scarcity, opacity, or structural power.
That sentence does a lot of work, so take it apart piece by piece.

First, extraction involves appropriation. Something is taken — captured, claimed, redirected, siphoned away. Extraction is not mere inequality of outcome; two parties can end up unequal without either extracting anything. It is a relation: one party gains by drawing value out of another source.

Second, the value comes in many currencies: money, but just as easily labor, land value, data, attention, knowledge, ecological capacity, institutional trust, public subsidy, future cash flow, cultural meaning, or plain social cooperation. Much of modern extraction consists in converting non-financial forms of value into financial claims.

Third — the heart of the matter — extraction is disproportionate to contribution. A person or institution may legitimately receive value for real service, labor, risk, coordination, creativity, stewardship, or investment. Extraction begins when the claim outgrows the contribution, especially when the excess is sustained by power rather than service.

Fourth, extraction is relational, operating through dependency, domination, enclosure, scarcity, opacity, or structural power. The point is not simply that one actor gains while another loses; the gain is made possible by a structure that limits the other party’s freedom, visibility, alternatives, or bargaining power.

Fifth, extraction can be perfectly legal — this is essential. Many extractive arrangements are lawful, contractually agreed, institutionally normalized, professionally managed, and publicly celebrated. The legal form of consent does not settle the moral question.

Sixth, extraction can occur inside productive systems. A company can produce useful goods and still be governed extractively; a platform can provide real services and still extract from dependency; a fund can support real enterprises and still impose disproportionate financial claims; a technology can be useful and still enclose a commons.

All of which means extraction requires judgment. You cannot identify it by asking whether an activity is profitable, legal, voluntary, innovative, scalable, or efficient — it can be all six and extractive anyway. You have to ask how the gain is produced, who bears the cost, who controls the relationship, whether alternatives are real, whether the claim is proportionate, and whether the activity strengthens or depletes the life from which value is drawn.
Or, compressed to a single line: extraction is gain without right relation.

sections.title: Productive and Extractive Capital
paragraphs: The distinction between productive and extractive capital is not a distinction between good people and bad people, nor between virtuous sectors and vicious ones. Manufacturing can be extractive. Finance can be productive. Technology can serve life; agriculture can exploit it. A nonprofit can drift toward extraction while a commercial business quietly practices stewardship. The distinction concerns the relation between capital, contribution, power, and purpose.

Productive capital participates in the creation, preservation, or circulation of real goods. It supports useful work, shares risk in proportionate ways, and strengthens the productive capacity of the enterprise or community it touches. It accepts limits. It remains accountable to the purpose of the activity it finances or governs, and it receives its return in relation to service, risk, time, and contribution.

Extractive capital captures value without proportionate contribution. Its gains come through control, dependency, scarcity, legal privilege, informational advantage, monopoly, distress, or the forced financialization of goods whose value exceeds their price. It may well support productive activity at the start. But over time its claims dominate, weaken, or redirect that activity toward the needs of capital itself.

A simple example shows the difference. A lender funds a small manufacturer’s equipment purchase. The loan is repayable from future cash flow, carries a reasonable rate, flexes in hardship, and gives the lender no control beyond what protecting repayment requires. If the equipment improves productivity and repayment stays proportionate, that capital is serving life.

Now change the structure. The lender offers terms that look manageable but contain covenants almost certain to be breached under ordinary stress. Upon breach come fees, seized assets, forced restructuring, control. The manufacturer’s vulnerability has become the lender’s opportunity: the claim no longer merely supports production; it is positioned to extract from distress. Both arrangements are loans. Only one is life-serving.

Investment tells the same story. Picture an investor in a mission-driven company who accepts a capped return, limited control, patient repayment from durable surplus, and governance protections that preserve the company’s purpose. The company grows slowly but resiliently; workers are paid fairly; customers are served honestly; the investor receives a fair return without ever owning the company’s future. That is productive capital.

Now picture the investor whose terms require rapid growth and exit. The company must scale before it is ready, chase customers it cannot serve well, cut the costs that protect quality, and groom itself for acquisition by a firm whose governance is incompatible with the mission. The original investment may have enabled production, but its structure redirects the enterprise toward liquidity, and capital ends up master rather than servant.

The difference is not sentiment. It is architecture. Who controls the enterprise? Who bears the risk? Who receives the surplus? Who decides when enough has been reached? Who can force an exit? Who protects the mission? Who remains responsible after the value has been taken out? Those questions sort productive from extractive capital more reliably than sector labels or moral branding.

sections.title: The Genealogy of the Distinction
paragraphs: The distinction is modern in its language and ancient in its bones.

Aristotle supplies the first grammar. Oikonomia is acquisition ordered toward life; chrematistics is wealth-getting without limit. The moral danger he saw was an inversion of means and ends — money, useful as a tool, promoted to purpose — and once accumulation loses its limit, economic activity loses its proper order.

Christianity deepened the concern through its language of avarice, usury, mercy, and charity. Wealth is dangerous, on this account, not merely because it can destabilize the city but because it can deform the soul and violate love of neighbor. Debt becomes morally charged, since it can bind the vulnerable to the powerful. Property becomes answerable, since the poor have claims upon the conscience of the rich.

The Scholastics formalized the matter through commutative justice and the just price, asking whether an exchange preserved proportionality and right relation. Profit had to correspond to service. Contract did not exhaust justice, and a perfectly voluntary transaction could still be morally disordered if it exploited necessity, ignorance, or unequal power.

The Franciscans clarified that capital can be productive in enterprise, risk, and foresight — that money as sterile possession is not morally identical to capital participating in real activity. But the clarification cut both ways: once capital can be productive, someone must distinguish productive return from usury wearing a disguise.

Salamanca expanded market reasoning to subjective value, price dynamics, money, and trade — and revealed in the process that market coordination can coexist with empire. A system can be economically sophisticated and morally compromised at once. Coordination is not the same as justice.

Protestant vocation and stewardship brought economic activity under calling. Work became service, property entrusted responsibility — though discipline and reinvestment, detached from Sabbath and grace, could turn into restless accumulation.

Smith then contributed the moral psychology: how wealth corrupts admiration, how self-interest requires justice, how markets coordinate but also concentrate power, and how rent-seekers bend law to private gain.

And modern economics gave us formidable analytical power while weakening the moral vocabulary of limits. As teleology receded, the distinction between productive and extractive capital became harder even to state. Extraction remained visible in suffering; it became harder to name in authoritative economic language.

The genealogy matters because it shows that extraction is not a new concern invented by modern critics of capitalism. It is the old problem of disordered wealth in contemporary form, and every generation caught the disorder in its own idiom: money detached from use, gain from service, return from risk, ownership from responsibility, control from accountability, growth from flourishing — capital detached from life. The pattern is detachment.

sections.title: From Chrematistics to Veblen
paragraphs: To connect the ancient version of the distinction to the modern one, no guide is more useful than Thorstein Veblen (1857–1929), born on a Wisconsin farm to Norwegian immigrant parents and grown into the most corrosively observant economist America had yet produced. He is best remembered for “The Theory of the Leisure Class” (1899), which gave the world “conspicuous consumption.” The book that matters here is “The Theory of Business Enterprise” (1904), where he drew a line between industry and business that echoes, across twenty-three centuries, Aristotle’s line between economy and chrematistics.

Industry, for Veblen, is the productive process itself: making goods, applying knowledge, solving technical problems, coordinating labor, meeting material needs. Business — in his critical sense — is the pursuit of pecuniary gain: the strategic hunt for profit through control, restriction, sabotage, monopoly, and the manipulation of ownership claims.

The point is not that all business is bad or all industry good. The point is that the logic of production and the logic of pecuniary gain can come apart. A factory may be capable of producing useful goods while its owners restrict output to keep prices up. A technology may be capable of serving millions while access is enclosed to maximize revenue. A company may have productive workers, knowledge, and customers while its financial owners pull out cash, cut capacity, and sell assets to raise returns. A platform may solve a real coordination problem, then collect rents from everyone who has come to depend on it.

In Veblen’s language, business can sabotage industry — and he meant the word precisely. Sabotage, he wrote in “The Engineers and the Price System” (1921), is “a conscientious withdrawal of efficiency”: deliberately throttling what could be produced in the interest of what can be charged.

This is a thoroughly modern articulation of extraction. Capital that should serve production instead interferes with production to enlarge its financial claim. Value is not created by making more, serving better, or stewarding longer; it is captured by controlling the conditions under which others must produce, exchange, or gain access to what they need.

The contrast sharpens our distinction. Productive capital asks how to create, preserve, or circulate real goods. Extractive capital asks what position would let it capture value from the activity of others. And position-hunting has predictable tastes: it will prefer scarcity to abundance if scarcity raises the price, dependency to resilience if dependency improves retention, enclosure to commons if enclosure creates monetizable access, complexity to transparency if complexity protects the margin, debt to equity if debt confers control, exit to stewardship if exit maximizes liquidity — and it will prefer metrics that justify extraction to measures that reveal depletion. That is the grammar of extraction.

Veblen also explains why extraction can be perfectly rational inside the wrong system. If the governing goal is pecuniary gain, then restricting output, manipulating scarcity, lobbying for advantage, and acquiring competitors may all be intelligent moves. The problem is not irrationality. The problem is the flawlessly rational pursuit of a disordered end.

Which is why modern extraction looks sophisticated rather than primitive. It is modeled in spreadsheets, written into contracts, justified by fiduciary duty, celebrated as strategic positioning, optimized through data, financed by respectable institutions, and defended as market discipline. This is why we need moral economy: without it, we are left to admire extraction as intelligence.

sections.title: Rent, Usury, and the Grammar of Extraction
paragraphs: Three older categories map the territory: rent, usury, and enclosure. Each names a different way of gaining from control rather than contribution.

Rent, in its problematic sense, is income from controlling access to something people need, rather than from ongoing productive contribution. Not all rent is illegitimate — a person who maintains a building, stewards land, or provides useful access may fairly be compensated. Rent turns extractive when it flows from control over scarcity, monopoly, or exclusion without proportionate service.

Usury, in the older moral sense, is gain from lending that exploits need, or that treats money as able to generate a claim without real participation in risk, labor, or production. Not all interest is usury in this broader sense. The word names a permanent danger: financial return detaching from contribution and sustaining itself on the borrower’s vulnerability.

Enclosure is the conversion of shared or common goods into controlled assets from which access can be monetized. Not every boundary is unjust — stewardship sometimes requires defined rights and responsibilities. Enclosure becomes extractive when shared capacity, public knowledge, ecological systems, community value, or digital commons are privatized so that one actor profits from what many created or need.

The three patterns usually run together. A platform encloses a network, then charges rent for access. A lender uses debt to gain control, then extracts through fees and collateral. A landlord captures, through rent, land value the surrounding community created. A private equity firm acquires a productive company, loads it with debt, extracts fees, and sells the assets. A technology company trains its models on shared knowledge, encloses the resulting infrastructure, and charges access to the very people whose labor helped create the value. A patent strategy restricts access to essential medicine or technology, converting legal exclusivity into rent. A dominant marketplace controls sellers’ access to buyers, then raises its fees once the dependency is established.

Rent, usury, and enclosure are not relics. They are living forms of extraction — the standing ways capital gains by controlling the conditions of life and production. And the playbook follows a recognizable sequence. First identify a dependency. Then gain control over access to it, and limit the alternatives. Make the dependency appear voluntary. Convert access into a recurring claim, and justify the claim by invoking risk, innovation, efficiency, legality, or scale. Finally, treat anyone who resists as irrational, anti-market, or naive.

The sequence can run in nearly any sector — housing, healthcare, education, finance, technology, agriculture, infrastructure, media, public services — any of which becomes a site of extraction the moment access to a necessary good is converted into disproportionate claim. The moral issue is not that access is priced. Prices can be legitimate. The issue is whether control over access becomes a means of domination.

sections.title: Extraction and Power
paragraphs: Extraction is inseparable from power. That sounds obvious, but the language of voluntary exchange hides it with remarkable efficiency. A contract appears equal because both parties signed. A market appears free because buyers and sellers transact. A platform appears consensual because users clicked. An investment appears chosen because the founder accepted the terms.

But power shapes the field on which choices get made. A starving person’s choice is not morally equivalent to the choice of someone with many alternatives. A founder with three months of runway negotiates differently from a fund with diversified capital. A worker who depends on employer-sponsored benefits bargains differently from a corporation with lawyers on retainer. A tenant facing eviction bargains differently from a landlord with multiple properties. A small supplier dependent on one platform bargains differently from the platform that controls its access to customers. A municipality running on proprietary software bargains differently from the vendor that owns the system.
Extraction occurs when power turns another’s lack of alternatives into a revenue stream.

This is why formal consent cannot be the only moral test. Consent matters, but it has to be interpreted within the conditions of necessity, knowledge, bargaining power, and exit. The question is never just “did they agree?” It is “what made this agreement their best available option — and who benefits from the absence of better ones?”

None of this means every unequal exchange is extractive. Inequalities of skill, information, capital, and urgency are everywhere in economic life. A seller may know more than a buyer; an investor may have more money than a founder; a landlord may own what a tenant needs; a company may employ people who need wages. Such asymmetries do not automatically make exchange unjust.

What they create is moral responsibility, and it scales. The more power one party holds, the more careful it must be not to convert that power into disproportionate gain. The more dependent the other party is, the less formal consent settles. The more essential the good, the more dangerous the opportunity for extraction. The fewer the alternatives, the more demanding the standard of justice. Power does not make profit wrong; it makes profit answerable.

sections.title: Extraction and Dependency
paragraphs: Dependency, by itself, is not a vice. Human beings are dependent creatures — on parents, neighbors, workers, farmers, teachers, doctors, institutions, ecosystems, and God. Societies are webs of interdependence, and markets themselves are one way of organizing it. The problem is never dependency as such. The problem is dependency without reciprocity, accountability, or exit.

Life-serving interdependence strengthens everyone in it: people cooperate, specialize, trust, and flourish; mutual obligation forms; dignity is preserved; each party ends more capable. Extractive dependency weakens one party to secure the other’s claim. It makes leaving difficult, monetizes lock-in, cultivates recurring need, and profits precisely when the dependent party cannot leave, cannot understand, cannot substitute, cannot bargain, and cannot refuse.

The distinction is crucial in the digital economy. A software platform typically begins by solving a real problem. Users join because it is useful; developers build around it; businesses integrate it; whole communities come to depend on it. Over time, switching grows costly: data accumulates, workflows harden, network effects deepen. The platform can then change terms, raise prices, alter visibility, impose fees, capture data, or privilege its own services — and its users can do little about any of it. The service may still be real. But the dependency has become monetizable. This is extraction through lock-in.

The same pattern appears in finance. Debt can help a borrower survive or invest; but when repayment terms hold the borrower in constant dependency, the lender’s revenue comes to depend on the borrower never quite recovering — extractive not because repayment exists but because the dependency is prolonged and monetized. It appears in labor: employment is necessary and can be dignified, but workers kept precarious, deskilled, surveilled, or tethered to employer-controlled benefits hand their employer a power drawn from their fragility. And it appears in housing: renting can be a useful arrangement, but when scarcity leaves tenants without real alternatives, landlords can extract from necessity rather than provide proportionate service.

A moral economy must be able to tell interdependence from dependency capture. Capital serves life when it increases the real agency and resilience of those it touches. It feeds on life when its profits depend on keeping them dependent.

sections.title: Extraction and Enclosure
paragraphs: Enclosure is one of the most powerful forms of extraction because it changes the moral status of a good. Something that was shared, open, common, relational, ecological, or public becomes a controlled asset. Once enclosed, access can be priced, use monitored, participation conditioned — and value created by many captured by whoever owns the gate.

Historically the word belongs to land. Common fields, forests, grazing rights, and shared rural practices were converted into private property regimes — in England alone, more than 5,200 enclosure acts passed by Parliament between 1604 and 1914 enclosed some 6.8 million acres, roughly a fifth of the country. The history is complex. Some enclosures increased agricultural productivity. Others destroyed ways of life, dispossessed communities, and turned settled people into wage laborers. The moral question was never whether boundaries or property rights can be legitimate — they can — but what is enclosed, by whom, for whose benefit, under what conditions, and with what loss of common capacity.

Modern enclosure mostly concerns intangible goods. Knowledge is enclosed through intellectual property regimes; data through platforms; software through proprietary infrastructure; attention through addictive interfaces; culture through licensing and branding; scientific research behind paywalls; public services through privatized systems; community value through land speculation. Enclosure becomes extraction when the enclosing party captures value it did not create alone and restricts access to goods that should remain shared, stewarded, or broadly available.

This is not an argument against property. Property can protect stewardship, and an open commons without governance can be depleted, captured, or neglected. Some exclusion is necessary — for care, responsibility, privacy, safety, or long-term investment. The question is whether property serves the good or converts the good into tribute. A mission-locked firm may use ownership precisely to protect purpose; a cooperative, membership boundaries to preserve shared governance; an open-source license, conditions to prevent enclosure; a land trust may hold property expressly to remove it from speculation. These are forms of bounded stewardship, not extraction, and the difference lies in purpose, governance, access, and accountability. Extraction encloses in order to capture, where stewardship draws boundaries in order to preserve.

The distinction will matter greatly when we reach the digital commons, because the digital world treats openness as raw material for enclosure. Shared code, public data, user content, academic knowledge, community moderation, and creative culture can all become inputs to private systems — which then charge the public for access to what the public helped build. That is chrematistics in digital form.

sections.title: Extraction and Opacity
paragraphs: Extraction thrives in the dark. If people cannot understand the terms, they cannot judge them. If consequences are hidden, moral imagination has nothing to work on. If pricing is complex, comparison becomes difficult; if ownership structures are layered, responsibility diffuses; if algorithms govern visibility, the people affected may never learn why they succeed or fail. And if financial engineering obscures where the risk sits, extraction can take its bow as innovation.

Opacity is not always a scheme. Complex systems are often hard to understand because the world is complex — modern finance, software, supply chains, regulation, and global trade all demand specialized knowledge, and some difficulty is unavoidable. Opacity becomes extractive when it is used to preserve advantage. A lender who hides the true cost of borrowing extracts through confusion. A platform that obscures how visibility is allocated extracts through dependency. A fund that buries its fee structure extracts through complexity. A company that conceals its supply-chain harms extracts through distance. A technology provider that makes exit technically difficult extracts through lock-in. A contract that tucks its control rights into legal language extracts through asymmetry.

Nor is transparency alone the cure. A predatory term disclosed in unreadable form is not made just by disclosure. A user cannot meaningfully consent to a system she can neither understand nor avoid; a founder cannot fully judge an investment structure without access to expertise; a worker cannot negotiate fairly when compensation, surveillance, scheduling, and termination criteria are opaque. Transparency still matters — it is a precondition for accountability — but moral economy requires something stronger: intelligibility. People must be able to know what claims are made upon them, what risks they bear, who controls the relationship, how value is allocated, and what alternatives exist.

This is also why accounting matters: whatever goes unaccounted for becomes easier to extract. A company that accounts for revenue but not worker burnout can extract labor while reporting growth. A fund that accounts for distributions but not portfolio fragility can extract financial return while reporting performance. A platform that accounts for engagement but not addiction can extract attention while reporting success. A city that accounts for property values but not displacement can extract land value while reporting prosperity.
Opacity, in the end, is not merely a lack of information. It is misdirected visibility — extraction arranging the lights so that what life-serving judgment needs to see stays outside the beam.

sections.title: How to Recognize Extraction
paragraphs: Extraction rarely walks in wearing its own name. It arrives under respectable ones: efficiency, innovation, liquidity, growth, optimization, fiduciary duty, market pricing, risk management, user engagement, asset utilization, value creation. So how do we recognize it?

A practical diagnostic begins with a handful of questions. Ask first what the source of the gain is: real service, production, risk-bearing, coordination, stewardship, invention — or control over access, scarcity, dependency, distress, opacity, legal privilege? Ask whether the claim is proportionate to the contribution: does the actor receiving value still provide service, bear risk, or preserve the good, or has the claim outgrown the contribution? Ask who bears the downside risk. Are risks shared fairly, or are workers, borrowers, founders, communities, ecosystems, and public institutions absorbing the fragility while capital captures the upside?

Ask whether the alternatives are real — whether the other party can refuse, exit, substitute, bargain, or even understand, or whether the consent is formal but structurally constrained. Ask whether the arrangement increases or reduces agency: do the people and communities affected become more capable, resilient, and free, or more dependent, fragile, and controllable? Ask what happens to mission — does the capital structure protect the purpose of the enterprise, or subordinate purpose to return, liquidity, and exit? Ask what is invisible in the accounting: which costs are being externalized onto workers, ecosystems, households, public institutions, open-source communities, or future generations? And ask, finally, whether the system can recognize enough. Is there a limit to the claim? Can repayment end? Can growth stop before it damages the good? Can surplus be stewarded rather than endlessly extracted?

These questions do not produce automatic answers. They require judgment. What they do is shift the analysis from surface form to moral structure. A loan is not extractive because it is a loan, nor a rent because it is rent, nor a profit because it is profit. A platform is not extractive because it is large, a fund because it seeks return, or a company because it grows. The question is always what kind of relationship is being created — and what kind of life it produces.

sections.title: The Suppression of the Distinction
paragraphs: Earlier in this book we were careful not to say too quickly that moral economy was “suppressed.” Mostly it was not — not in any deliberate, conspiratorial sense. It was narrowed, displaced, professionalized, abstracted, secularized, and forgotten. But there is a further truth. The distinction between productive and extractive capital also meets resistance because it threatens powerful interests.

Consider what follows once the distinction can be drawn. Not all profit receives the same honor. Not all investment appears equally legitimate, not all growth healthy, not all innovation life-serving. Not every legal contract looks just, not every shareholder return morally deserved, not every market outcome beyond judgment. For anyone who benefits from ambiguity, this is dangerous.

It is useful, if you are an extractive actor, to keep the line between enterprise and extraction blurry — to appear as an entrepreneur, an innovator, an investor, a job creator, an efficiency expert, a capital allocator, a platform builder, a market participant. Sometimes that is exactly what such people are. But sometimes the words are camouflage for rent-seeking, enclosure, domination, or disproportionate claim.

The language of value creation is especially easy to bend. A private equity firm may claim to create value when it raises financial returns, even when the returns come from layoffs, underinvestment, debt loading, asset sales, and reduced resilience. A platform may claim to create value when it captures transactions that previously flowed through open networks. A real estate investor may claim it when rising land prices merely register public investment and community life. A technology company may claim it when it encloses public knowledge in proprietary systems. In each case the same question waits: value for whom, created how, and at whose expense?

A moral economy must reclaim the language of value creation from financial abstraction. Real value creation strengthens life — genuine capacity, usefulness, resilience, knowledge, beauty, health, justice, freedom. Extraction can raise financial value while shrinking every one of those goods.
This is why the distinction keeps getting sidelined. It is too morally revealing. A society that can name extraction can no longer honor all accumulation equally.

sections.title: Extraction and Externalization
paragraphs: One of extraction’s most common mechanisms is externalization: shifting costs onto others while keeping the benefits. A factory pollutes a river and never pays the full cost of restoration. A platform runs on unpaid moderation, public infrastructure, and open-source software without contributing proportionately to any of them. A company burns out its workers and lets households, healthcare systems, and communities absorb the consequences. A fund extracts cash from a company and leaves the fragility to employees, creditors, and the public. A fossil fuel company books its profits now and leaves the climate costs to future generations, who have no seat at the table.

Externalization lets extraction masquerade as efficiency. Costs disappear from the balance sheet, so the firm appears more productive than it is. Harms move elsewhere, so profit appears higher than it should. The market price does not reflect the full cost, so consumption appears cheaper than it truly is. And investors receive returns that depend on unpaid debts to people, places, and the future.

By the standards this book has been assembling, externalization fails on every count. It violates commutative justice, because the exchange no longer reflects right relation; stewardship, because an actor entrusted with power refuses responsibility for its effects; the common good, because private gain is purchased with public cost. And it is, besides, a failure of truth. A business model that depends on hidden harm is lying about its profitability. Its accounts may be legally accurate and morally incomplete at once — reporting surplus while concealing depletion, calling itself efficient because someone else is paying the real cost.

So a moral economy must insist on fuller accounting. Not infinite accounting — no institution can measure everything. But morally relevant costs must not be systematically hidden: worker harm, ecological depletion, community displacement, dependency creation, mission drift, institutional fragility, and the enclosure of commons must all be visible enough to govern. Otherwise extraction will keep showing up in the annual report, dressed as performance.

sections.title: Extraction and Time
paragraphs: Extraction has a characteristic relationship with time: it distorts it.

Productive activity is mostly slow. Soil fertility takes years to build, and so do trust, craft, research, open-source infrastructure, institutional culture, mission fidelity, and the formation of human beings. Extractive capital, by contrast, accelerates its claims on value. It wants liquidity before maturity. It pulls future cash flows into present valuations, pressures companies to grow before they are stable, discounts long-term harms, rewards exit over stewardship, treats future resilience as underutilized capital, and prefers whatever can be monetized now to whatever must be cultivated slowly.

This is why the time horizon of capital is a moral fact and not just a portfolio preference. A patient investor can carry a company through the slow work of becoming durable; an impatient one can force growth that weakens it. A lender can structure repayment around real surplus, or demand payment on a schedule that manufactures distress. A steward can preserve land for future generations, or liquidate it for immediate gain. A technology company can build infrastructure for long-term public benefit, or optimize for short-term user capture.

Extraction compresses time in the claimant’s favor. It asks the future to pay for the present: workers absorb the pressure now so investors can receive returns sooner; ecosystems bear the depletion now so profits can be reported this quarter; companies sacrifice resilience for valuation; communities accept displacement in the name of development.

A moral economy must therefore recover patience as a financial virtue. Evergreen finance, repayment from durable surplus, mission-locked ownership, long-term stewardship, and commons governance are all attempts to repair distorted time. Each says, in its own way, that not every good can be forced onto the timetable of liquidity — that some goods must be cultivated, preserved, and passed on. Capital that serves life respects the time life actually takes. Capital that feeds on life is forever trying to collect early.

sections.title: Extraction and the Category of Enough
paragraphs: Extraction cannot recognize enough.

This may be its most Aristotelian feature. Natural acquisition, he taught, is limited by the needs of life; chrematistics is unlimited because its end is accumulation itself. Modern extraction runs on the same logic: when the governing aim is return, growth, liquidity, or market dominance, every limit presents itself as an inefficiency to be engineered away.

Watch how reliably more overrides enough. A company has revenue enough to sustain itself, its workers, its customers, and its mission — but investors demand growth. A platform serves its users well — but must increase engagement. A landlord receives fair compensation — but market scarcity allows more. A fund has collected a reasonable return — but uncapped upside permits the claim to continue. A technology is useful as a commons — but enclosure could monetize it. A community has stable housing — but redevelopment promises higher asset values.

This is why limits must be designed rather than left to sentiment. A person may intend restraint while the structures around her reward expansion. A founder may want mission while capital demands exit; a board may want stewardship while fiduciary culture demands maximization; a platform may begin with community while its metrics demand engagement; a fund may begin with impact while investor expectations demand return. Good intentions, unassisted, lose these arguments.

So the category of enough needs institutional form. Repayment caps define when capital has received enough. Asset locks prevent mission assets from becoming private gain. Steward ownership separates control from speculative ownership. Commons licenses set the conditions under which shared goods may be used without being enclosed. Sabbath does the same work in theological form, placing a limit on production and mastery. And Jubilee — the fiftieth year of Leviticus 25, when land returned to its families and liberty was proclaimed — interrupts debt’s claim to perpetuity.
Without such limits, extraction will rationalize itself endlessly. Enough is not an anti-economic idea. It is the condition under which economic life stays ordered at all.

sections.title: Extraction as Anti-Stewardship
paragraphs: Extraction is the opposite of stewardship, and setting them side by side shows what is at stake.

A steward receives a good in trust. The good is not merely possessed; it is entrusted for a purpose, and the steward must preserve, cultivate, and use it rightly — accountable to the giver, to the purpose of the good, to the community affected, and to the future. Extraction breaks that whole web of relationship. It treats entrusted goods as opportunities for claim. It consumes inheritance, monetizes dependency, liquidates mission, converts common goods into private assets, captures surplus without the responsibility that goes with it, and uses legal ownership to fend off moral accountability.

This is why extraction is not merely an economic problem but a spiritual and institutional disorder. It is a sustained refusal of several truths at once: that ownership is limited by purpose, that power creates obligation, that future generations have claims on the present, that the vulnerable are not raw material for return, and that not all value should be liquid.

Extraction, at bottom, is capital refusing to be a steward. Where stewardship asks what has been entrusted to us and how it must be preserved and used, extraction asks what can be claimed, monetized, and controlled. Stewardship accepts accountability; extraction engineers insulation. Stewardship honors limits; extraction files them under inefficiency. Stewardship preserves mission; extraction converts mission into an asset. Stewardship strengthens the life of the other; extraction feeds on it.

This is why any recovery of stewardship must be structural. It is not enough to ask capital, politely, to behave; it must be placed under forms of governance, ownership, accounting, and law that make stewardship durable and extraction difficult.

sections.title: Extraction in the Firm
paragraphs: The firm is one of the main sites where productive and extractive logics collide, because a firm can be either thing.

At its best, a firm is a community of production. It gathers people around a useful purpose and coordinates labor, knowledge, capital, tools, relationships, and trust across time. It serves customers, sustains workers, supports suppliers, and contributes to the life of its community. It can be, in the older language, a vessel of vocation and stewardship.

But a firm can also become a vehicle for extraction, and the change happens when its productive life is subordinated to financial claim. Workers become costs to minimize. Customers become revenue streams to lock in. Suppliers become pressure points.
