Community becomes branding; mission becomes marketing; assets become collateral; future cash flows become instruments of leverage. The company itself becomes something to be bought, optimized, indebted, stripped, and sold. The firm still produces — for a time, perhaps more efficiently. But its purpose has quietly changed hands.

The shift is easiest to see where ownership has detached from responsibility. Shareholders can demand return without knowing the workers, customers, or communities their demands land on. Private equity owners can control strategy while legal structures insulate them from downside. Venture investors can push growth and exit while founders and employees bear the lived consequences. Public markets can punish long-term investment the moment it dents short-term earnings.

Extraction in the firm usually begins with a change in the answer to one question: what is the company for? If the answer is “to serve a real mission through useful work,” capital must be ordered around that purpose. If it is “to maximize financial return to owners,” mission survives only as long as it stays compatible with return — and no longer.

This is why governance matters. The purpose of a firm cannot be protected by rhetoric alone; it must be embedded in ownership rights, board duties, investor agreements, exit restrictions, surplus allocation, and accountability structures. Chapter 12 will examine the corporation more fully. For now, the key point is that extraction often enters productive enterprise through governance. Capital does not need to destroy production right away. It only needs the authority to redefine what production is for.

sections.title: Extraction in Finance
paragraphs: Finance is morally ambivalent because time is morally ambivalent. Human beings need ways to move resources across time — we save, lend, borrow, invest, insure, pool risk, and fund projects whose benefits arrive years later. Done well, finance makes long-term work possible. It can support enterprise, infrastructure, households, research, transition, resilience, and repair.

But finance is also uniquely vulnerable to extraction, because its whole business is creating claims on the future. A financial claim is legitimate when it corresponds to real contribution, risk, time, and service. It becomes extractive when the claim exceeds the contribution, avoids responsibility, exploits need, or comes to dominate the productive activity out of which repayment must come.

The mechanisms are worth listing, because each is a respectable tool with an extractive setting. Interest can extract, when repayment burdens exceed the borrower’s real capacity or profit from distress. So can fees, when complexity hides disproportionate compensation, and control rights, when capital takes authority beyond what protection requires. Liquidation preferences extract when investors protect their upside by shifting loss onto others; leverage, when debt raises the owners’ returns while the company carries the fragility; securitization, when originators sell the risk onward and shed responsibility for the consequences. Exit pressure extracts when companies are shaped for sale rather than stewardship, and valuation extracts when imagined future growth becomes the basis for present control.

None of these mechanisms is automatically immoral in every form. Interest, fees, control rights, liquidation preferences, leverage, securitization, exits, and valuations all have legitimate uses — which is exactly why moral economy must learn to distinguish. The question, in every case, is whether finance remains servant to productive life. Does it provide patient capital and share risk? Does it preserve mission, allow repayment from durable surplus, and accept a limit to its claim? Does it strengthen the enterprise, and leave people, institutions, and commons more resilient than it found them? Or does it use productive life as material for return?
That is the central question for Non-Extractive Capital.

sections.title: Extraction in the Digital Commons
paragraphs: The digital economy has created new forms of extraction for an instructive reason: it has created new forms of shared value. Open-source software, public research, user-generated content, community moderation, behavioral data, language, culture, and the sheer participation of millions in a network all generate value that is collective, distributed, and often unpaid. Digital platforms have proved extraordinarily skilled at capturing that value and converting it into proprietary systems, advertising revenue, subscription models, data assets, AI training sets, and market power.

This does not mean digital companies create no value. Many create a great deal: search, communication, cloud infrastructure, open-source tools, AI systems, marketplaces, and collaborative platforms can serve real needs, coordinate knowledge at enormous scale, reduce transaction costs, expand access, and enable creativity. But the digital economy also makes enclosure unusually easy. The raw material may be shared while the resulting infrastructure is private; the users may create the network while the platform owns the data; the community may build the value while the company captures the return. The commons may train the model — and the model may then charge the commons for access.

This is extraction through the asymmetrical capture of shared value. The moral question is not whether digital infrastructure should be free in every case. Infrastructure costs money; developers need livelihoods; maintenance, security, scaling, and governance all matter. But when the value is created by many, depended upon by many, and essential to public life, its governance cannot be left entirely to private extraction.

Digital sovereignty, in other words, is not only geopolitical. It is moral-economic. Who controls the infrastructure on which public institutions depend? Who owns the code, the data, the models? Who can change the terms, and who can exclude? Who captures the value of shared knowledge, who bears the cost of maintenance, and who remains accountable to users, citizens, maintainers, and future generations?

Chapter 17 will return to these questions. For now, notice that the digital age has not escaped the old problem; it has intensified it. Money detached from life has become platforms detached from commons. Usury has become the recurring claim on dependency. Enclosure has become proprietary control over shared knowledge; rent has become the access fee at a digital chokepoint; chrematistics has become the optimization of attention, data, and infrastructure for unlimited accumulation.
The forms are new. The disorder is old.

sections.title: Why Extraction Matters
paragraphs: Extraction matters because it destroys the conditions of life while appearing to create value — and the tally of what it weakens is long. It weakens households by converting need into debt, and workers by treating labor as disposable cost. Firms are weakened as productive purpose is subordinated to financial claim, communities as place turns into an asset, ecosystems as creation is treated as input and sink. Public institutions grow weaker as they become dependent on private infrastructure, markets as competition gives way to capture, entrepreneurship as founders are herded toward exit, the commons as shared capacity is enclosed. And moral imagination weakens most of all, in a society that honors accumulation without asking how it was obtained.

Nor is extraction merely an unfair division of the spoils after production. It changes production itself — what gets built, how work is organized, what risks are taken, what goods are neglected, what forms of dependency are cultivated, what futures become possible.

This is why growth alone cannot solve extraction. An extractive economy can grow by deepening extraction. It can increase GDP through debt, speculation, addiction, surveillance, ecological depletion, healthcare costs, housing inflation, and defensive expenditures. It can report productivity while hollowing out resilience, and produce financial returns while destroying the institutions that make future production possible. So the question is never only whether the economy grows, but what kind of growth, generated how, governed by whom, and ordered toward what. A moral economy judges growth by whether it serves life.

sections.title: The Need for Reconstruction
paragraphs: Critique, by itself, is not enough. It is entirely possible to name extraction and remain trapped by it — to condemn financialized capitalism while depending on its tools, to denounce shareholder primacy while building companies that require exit, to praise the commons while relying on business models that enclose it, to invoke stewardship while using ownership structures that make stewardship fragile.

The task is reconstruction. If extraction is architectural, then moral economy must become architectural too. It must redesign ownership, governance, finance, accounting, contracts, and institutional purpose. It must build forms in which capital can receive fair return without becoming sovereign; protect mission beyond founder intention; allow enterprise to grow without forcing exit; make repayment depend on durable surplus rather than domination; define enough; make externalities visible; preserve commons; and give stewardship legal and financial teeth.

That is why the later chapters of this book turn to steward ownership, mission-locked governance, evergreen finance, repayment caps, and Non-Extractive Capital. None of these is a perfect solution. All of them can drift, be captured, be poorly designed, or dwindle into the merely symbolic. But they represent the right kind of response — not moral appeal alone, but institutional form. A society that wants capital to serve life must structure capital so that service is possible, durable, and protected.

sections.title: The Diagnostic We Carry Forward
paragraphs: This chapter gives us the diagnostic the rest of the book will use. Whenever we encounter a firm, a fund, a platform, a market, a contract, or an institution, we will put the same questions to it. Is value being created, or merely captured? Is gain proportionate to contribution? Is risk genuinely shared, or shifted downward? Are people becoming more capable or more dependent? Are commons being strengthened or enclosed? Is mission protected or monetized? Can the system recognize enough? Does capital remain servant, or has it become master?

Questions like these do not replace economic analysis. They deepen it. We still need to understand incentives, costs, prices, competition, productivity, risk, and coordination — but we must also ask what those mechanisms are ordered toward. The market can tell us what can be charged; it cannot tell us whether the charge is just. Finance can tell us what return can be achieved; it cannot tell us whether the return is proportionate. Corporate law can tell us what owners can do; it cannot tell us what ownership is for. Extraction is what happens when the second half of each sentence stops being asked.

The next chapters examine how that abandonment took institutional form. Austrian economics will show us the beauty and the limits of spontaneous order. The corporation will show us how legal architecture can make capital sovereign. Financialized capitalism will show us what happens when extraction becomes systemic.

But we now have the central distinction in hand. Capital serves life when it participates in real goods, accepts limits, shares risk, preserves mission, and remains accountable to the communities and futures it affects. Capital feeds on life when it captures value through dependency, domination, enclosure, opacity, scarcity, or disproportionate claim.
The rest of the book asks how to build an economy around the first, while resisting the second.

chapters.title: Chapter 11: Austrian Economics and Entrepreneurial Discovery

paragraphs: Chapter 10 named extraction thoroughly enough that a reader might come away wanting to burn the whole apparatus down. If profit can be extractive, perhaps profit itself is suspect. If price can conceal power, perhaps price is merely ideology. If capital can dominate, perhaps decentralized exchange is only a mask for domination. And if markets can be captured, perhaps planning is the moral alternative. Each step feels like rigor. The temptation must be resisted, and resisting it is this chapter’s job: to protect moral economy from becoming anti-market.

Markets are not morally self-sufficient, but they are not morally meaningless either. They are one of the ways human beings coordinate knowledge, labor, need, risk, creativity, and time without a single center of command. They can reveal things no planner knows. They allow ordinary people to respond to one another’s needs through enterprise, trade, and adaptation. They make room for discovery.

The tradition that saw this most clearly is the Austrian school. It is not the only tradition that understands markets, nor a complete moral economy in itself — it runs thin on power, virtue, institutional formation, commons, and the moral ends of economic life. But it preserves insights any serious reconstruction must receive.

Carl Menger helps us see that value is not embedded in objects but emerges from human need, judgment, and context. Friedrich Hayek helps us see that knowledge is dispersed, tacit, local, and often impossible to centralize. And the Austrian account of entrepreneurship helps us see the market not as a static allocation mechanism but as a process of discovery under uncertainty.

These insights matter because a moral economy must not confuse moral seriousness with central control. The economy is not a machine to be morally engineered from above; it is a living field of action, judgment, error, adaptation, and discovery. Nobody knows in advance all the goods that need creating, the needs that will emerge, the uses a technology will find, the capacities hiding in a community, or the ways people might serve one another through enterprise. Moral economy needs markets because it needs discovery.

But discovery must be ordered. That markets discover opportunities does not tell us the opportunities are good. That a price signals scarcity does not tell us whether the scarcity is natural, artificial, just, or manufactured. That entrepreneurs respond to incentives does not tell us whether they serve need or monetize vulnerability. That order emerges spontaneously does not tell us whether it is humane.
Here is the beauty and the limit of Austrian economics. It teaches that markets are epistemically powerful — that markets know things. What markets are for, it cannot by itself say.

sections.title: Menger and Subjective Value
paragraphs: Carl Menger came to economics through journalism. Covering markets for Vienna’s official Wiener Zeitung, he noticed that the merchants and brokers he interviewed explained prices in ways his textbooks could not: the books said value came from labor or costs of production; the traders talked about what buyers needed, how urgently, and what else was on offer. Menger sided with the traders. His Principles of Economics appeared in 1871, the same year William Stanley Jevons reached the same essential idea in England, with Léon Walras arriving independently in Lausanne three years later — the marginal revolution, begun in Vienna by a newspaperman who trusted his sources.

Menger’s account of value begins from human need. Goods have value because they can satisfy human wants; value is not a property sitting inside the object but arises from the relationship between a person, a need, and a good capable of meeting it. The same object therefore carries different value in different circumstances. A cup of water is worth little beside a clean river and nearly everything in a desert. A tool useless to one person is indispensable to another. A medicine is irrelevant to the healthy and priceless to the sick.

The theologians of Salamanca had begun circling the same insight centuries earlier — value as partly subjective, contextual, and relational — and the Austrians, as we saw in that chapter, were pleased to claim them as ancestors.

Menger’s marginalism refined the point. The value of a good depends not on the total usefulness of all goods of its type, but on the significance of the particular need that would go unmet if one unit were lost. That is why diamonds command high prices while water, without which nobody lasts a week, often costs almost nothing. Price and existential importance are not the same thing; scarcity, marginal use, and context decide.

As analysis this was a major advance. It moved economics past crude theories of value, explained how prices actually behave, and took seriously the perspective of the acting person. Economic value, it turned out, is not imposed from above by moralists, rulers, or labor arithmetic alone. People value goods in relation to their own purposes, circumstances, and plans.

A moral economy should welcome all of this. Human beings are not interchangeable units with identical needs, and value is not fully knowable from outside. What a good is worth may depend on local knowledge, personal situation, timing, culture, vocation, and practical context. A centralized authority cannot know what a particular tool means to a craftsperson, what a loan means to a household, what a piece of software means to its maintainer, what a small grant means to a community, or what an unglamorous technology makes possible for the entrepreneur staring at it.

Subjective value protects humility. It reminds us that the people closest to a situation often know something outsiders do not. It warns against paternalism and makes room for decentralized judgment. And it explains the quiet miracle of exchange: even at one and the same price, both parties can walk away better off, because each values what they received more than what they gave.

But subjective value is not enough. That a person values something does not settle whether the desire is well ordered. Human beings can value what harms them. Addiction creates value in the strict economic sense. Manipulation creates demand. Artificial scarcity raises willingness to pay. Desperation raises the subjective value of access, and a person may value relief from distress so highly that they accept terms that deepen the dependence producing it.
Subjective value explains prices. Justifying them is a different job entirely.

So the Austrian insight must be supplemented. Value does emerge from human judgment and context — but judgment is formed, constrained, manipulated, and sometimes desperate. A market that treats every willingness to pay as morally equivalent cannot tell medicine from addiction, shelter from speculation, education from credential anxiety, or useful digital infrastructure from dependency capture.
Menger taught us that value is relational. Moral economy must ask what kind of relation forms the value.

sections.title: Prices as Knowledge
paragraphs: One of the great Austrian insights is that prices communicate knowledge. A price is not merely a number. It condenses dispersed information about scarcity, demand, opportunity, cost, urgency, substitution, and expectation for people who never see the underlying facts.

Hayek made the case with tin, in “The Use of Knowledge in Society,” published in the American Economic Review in 1945. Suppose tin becomes scarcer — a mine floods, or a profitable new use appears. No single mind needs to know which. The price rises, and the rise is instruction enough: users economize, producers hunt for alternatives, consumers substitute, entrepreneurs notice opportunities. A vast field of coordination occurs without central command.

The economic problem, for Hayek, was never merely how to allocate given resources among given ends. The deeper problem is how to use knowledge that exists nowhere in one piece — dispersed among many people, much of it tacit, local, and tied to the moment. The farmer knows the soil. The engineer knows the failure mode. The shopkeeper knows the customer, the nurse the workflow, the maintainer the software dependency, the founder the user’s pain, the family its own needs, the craftsman the material. Markets coordinate all this scattered knowing through prices, profit, loss, and entrepreneurial action.

This is a profound insight, and moral economy ignores it at great cost. A morally serious economy that cannot coordinate knowledge will fail the people it wants to serve. It will set prices that create shortages, protect jobs while destroying the conditions for future work, subsidize what should change while neglecting what should be built, impose uniform solutions on diverse contexts, mistake visible intention for actual service, and concentrate power in institutions that cannot learn quickly enough. Hayek teaches humility before complexity. No moral theologian, planner, regulator, investor, or activist knows enough to design economic life from the top down. People must be allowed to discover, test, adapt, fail, and respond; the economy contains more knowledge than any committee can collect.

But prices are not omniscient. They communicate certain kinds of knowledge and quietly omit others — powerful and partial at once. They can reveal scarcity but not always justice, communicate willingness to pay but not human dignity, signal demand without saying whether it was manipulated. They reflect the costs the seller bears, not the costs pushed onto workers, ecosystems, public institutions, or generations not yet born. They reward innovation — and, with equal enthusiasm, addiction, enclosure, speculation, and dependency.

A price can only see what has been priced; the limitation is not fine print, it is central. If a river can be polluted at no cost, the price of the product lies. If open-source maintainers go unpaid, the price of the proprietary software stacked on their work lies. If workers absorb burnout, the price of the service lies. If tenants have nowhere else to go, the rent reveals scarcity and power, not necessarily justice. If a platform controls access to users, its fees measure dependency as much as value.

The price system is a knowledge system, but not a complete moral knowledge system. Moral economy must neither dismiss prices nor worship them; it must read them critically. Prices are signals, not sacraments. They tell us something real. They do not tell us everything we need to know.

sections.title: Hayek and Dispersed Knowledge
paragraphs: Hayek’s critique of central planning is one of the strongest arguments ever mounted against moral arrogance in economic life, and he did not build it alone. His Vienna mentor Ludwig von Mises had opened the fight in 1920, arguing that a socialist commonwealth, having abolished market prices for the means of production, could not calculate — could not tell which of a thousand possible uses of steel and labor was worth its cost. The socialist calculation debate ran for decades; Hayek’s contribution was to shift its ground from calculation to knowledge.

The planner imagines the economy as a problem in arithmetic: gather the data, define the ends, allocate the resources, collect the outcome. Hayek’s reply is that the relevant knowledge is not given to anyone. It is dispersed, changing, tacit, and often discovered only through action. The information needed for economic coordination exists in fragments across society, and much of it cannot be written down in advance even by the people who hold it.

This is not only an economic claim. It is a claim about human finitude. No person sees the whole; no institution, no state, and no market actor either. We depend on forms of coordination that allow partial knowledge to become socially useful.

Markets, for Hayek, are such a form. They let people act on knowledge they possess without first explaining it to a central authority, and the result can be a spontaneous order: a pattern of coordination designed by no single mind, emerging from many actions under rules.

The world conceded the point in 1974, splitting the economics Nobel — with impeccable diplomatic instinct — between Hayek and Gunnar Myrdal, a Swedish champion of planning with whom he agreed about almost nothing. Hayek’s Stockholm lecture was titled “The Pretence of Knowledge.”

Moral economy needs the concept of spontaneous order, because not all order comes from command. Language is a spontaneous order. So are custom, common law, markets, scientific communities, open-source projects, and much of civil society. Replacing such orders with central design can destroy knowledge embedded in practice — knowledge nobody knew was there until it was gone.

The point presses hardest on the digital commons. Open-source ecosystems are spontaneous orders of a purity Hayek never lived to see. No single planner fully commands them. Developers contribute for every reason imaginable — need, reputation, curiosity, employment, ideology, frustration, joy, service, plain necessity — and dependencies form, standards emerge, tools improve, communities coordinate, and value accumulates through distributed knowledge. A moral economy of the digital world cannot impose top-down control without damaging the generativity it hopes to preserve. Stewardship of commons must respect dispersed knowledge.

But Hayek’s insight has limits, and they matter as much as the insight. Spontaneous order is not automatically just. Harmful orders also emerge without anyone designing them. Segregation can be spontaneous. So can discrimination, financial bubbles, extractive platform dependency, race-to-the-bottom labor markets, ecological destruction, misinformation dynamics, and markets in addiction. Nobody plans them, and an order nobody planned is not thereby morally good.

Hayek arms us against the arrogance of planners. Moral economy must also guard against the innocence of spontaneous order. The question is not whether an order was planned or emerged. It is whether the order serves life.

sections.title: Entrepreneurship as Discovery
paragraphs: Austrian economics gives the entrepreneur a role no other tradition quite grants her. She does not merely manage known resources. She discovers possibilities: a mismatch between present arrangements and future opportunity, an unmet need, an underused resource, an emerging technology, a price difference, a new combination, a hidden demand. She acts under uncertainty, before others see what she sees. Israel Kirzner, Mises’s student, built a whole theory of the market on this moment in Competition and Entrepreneurship (1973), and gave the crucial faculty a name that stuck: alertness — the knack of noticing what everyone else has walked past.

This connects directly to the Franciscans of chapter 5. Olivi and the moral-economic thinkers after him recognized that commercial foresight can be a genuine contribution: a merchant who anticipates need, bears risk, and moves goods to where they are useful may legitimately profit. Austrian economics develops that intuition into a broader account of market discovery. Profit is not merely a reward for possession. It can be a reward for seeing and acting before others do.

This is an important defense of entrepreneurship, and moral economy should accept it gratefully. Discovery must not be crushed under suspicion; many life-serving goods begin as someone noticing what others missed — the new tool, the better process, the neglected customer group, the overlooked technology, the new organizational form, the cheaper method, the more humane service, the idle resources waiting for coordination. Entrepreneurship can serve life by revealing possibilities hidden inside the present.

But discovery is morally ambivalent — and here the Austrian account goes quiet. An entrepreneur can discover a way to provide clean water more cheaply. She can also discover a way to addict children to a platform. He can discover a new medical device, or a regulatory loophole. They can discover an underserved community and build it dignified services, or discover a vulnerable population and monetize its desperation. A company can discover a better way to coordinate open-source maintenance, or a way to enclose that community’s work. Discovery, in short, is not the same as service.

Austrian economics tends to treat entrepreneurial profit as evidence that the entrepreneur saw something valuable. Moral economy asks what kind of value was seen. Was the entrepreneur alert to real need, or to exploitable weakness? Did the discovery increase agency or dependency, build capacity or capture it? Did it solve a problem, or create a problem it would be profitable to keep? Did it serve users, or manipulate them? The entrepreneur as discoverer must become the entrepreneur as steward.

Startup culture needs this distinction more than most. The language of innovation praises discovery and rarely pauses to interpret it. Founders are rewarded for finding scalable opportunities, and not all scalable opportunities are good. Some scale because they serve genuine need. Others scale because they exploit psychological vulnerability, regulatory gaps, labor precarity, network lock-in, or public subsidy — and from a distance the pitch decks look identical.
A moral economy does not ask entrepreneurs to stop discovering. It asks them to discern what it is they have found.

sections.title: Profit and Loss as Discipline
paragraphs: Austrian economists prize profit and loss as mechanisms of learning. Profit signals that resources may have been combined in a way that creates value for others; loss signals that they might be better used elsewhere. Markets, on this view, discipline error. Entrepreneurs test hypotheses, consumers respond, prices move, some ventures survive while others fold, and knowledge emerges from the feedback.

There is real power here. A moral economy that ignores feedback will waste resources and harm the people it hopes to help. An institution can have noble intentions and still produce goods no one needs, services no one uses, and systems that cannot sustain themselves. Loss can reveal reality. Profit can reveal usefulness. Financial discipline can prevent fantasy.

The Monti di Pietà taught this lesson in chapter 5: a charitable fund that depletes itself cannot serve the poor. Moral intention must meet accounting reality, and an institution that cannot cover its costs, manage its defaults, preserve its capital, and govern abuse may collapse onto the people leaning on it. Sustainability matters because service takes place over time.

But profit and loss are not morally complete signals. Profit may flow from genuine service; it may equally flow from extraction. Loss may indicate waste; it may also indicate mission fidelity in a market that fails to reward real goods. A company that protects its workers, stewards its land, maintains open infrastructure, or serves poor communities may earn less than one that externalizes every cost it can. A platform that manipulates users will generally out-earn one that respects their attention. A landlord who exploits scarcity does better than one who practices restraint. The ledger records all of it without blushing.
Profit is feedback. It is not absolution. Loss, likewise, is feedback — and not always condemnation.

For non-extractive finance the distinction is essential. A portfolio company that generates durable surplus through real service is a different creature from one that generates cash by underpaying workers, locking in customers, neglecting maintenance, or externalizing costs. A repayment formula tied to free cash flow has to ask whether the cash flow is morally healthy. A profitable enterprise may still be extractive; an unprofitable one may still be producing goods the market undervalues.

None of this means abandoning financial discipline. It means the discipline must be morally interpreted. The question is not simply whether the market rewarded a venture, but what, exactly, the market was rewarding.

sections.title: The Beauty of Spontaneous Order
paragraphs: There is beauty in spontaneous order. A market square, a language, a footpath worn across a field, an open-source ecosystem, a craft tradition, a city neighborhood, a scientific community, a body of customary practice — each can emerge from countless actions that no one designed. Order appears without command. People adjust to one another, patterns form, knowledge circulates, practices stabilize, innovation happens at the edges, and participants discover uses and possibilities no central designer would have imagined.

Moral economy should not sniff at this. There is humility in allowing order to emerge, and a respect for creaturely plurality — a recognition that human beings participate in patterns larger than their own plans. There is a kind of subsidiarity in it: decisions made by those closest to the knowledge and the need, with room for vocation, initiative, and local intelligence. Rightly understood, the moral economy tradition should find all this naturally sympathetic.

Subsidiarity does not mean that every problem should be solved by markets. It means that higher levels of authority should not absorb the proper agency of lower ones — and Hayek’s knowledge problem supplies an economic reason for a principle Christian social thought had reached on other grounds: human action is situated, local, and plural. Sphere sovereignty, the Reformed tradition’s kindred idea, adds that the spheres of life — family, church, school, firm, market, state, science, civil society — each have their own integrity and should not be collapsed into one master sphere. Spontaneous orders help preserve that plurality, if only by keeping all coordination from becoming state command.

Open-source communities show the beauty at full strength. Contributors self-organize around shared problems. Governance may be formal or informal. Reputation, usefulness, technical excellence, institutional backing, and community norms interact; no single actor controls the whole; value emerges from distributed stewardship.

But beauty is not innocence. Spontaneous orders can exclude. They can reproduce hidden hierarchies, be captured by better-resourced actors, neglect their own maintenance, create dependencies without accountability, and fail to protect the vulnerable. They can be enclosed by precisely the people who understand the system well enough to monetize it.

So the beauty of spontaneous order must be joined to the ethics of stewardship. A garden may grow organically, but somebody still has to weed it. A commons may emerge spontaneously and still need governance; a market may coordinate knowledge and still need moral limits; an open-source project born in perfect freedom still needs support, and protection from those who would fence it in. Spontaneous order is not the opposite of stewardship. It is one of the things stewardship must learn how to protect.

sections.title: The Limits of Spontaneous Order
paragraphs: The limits of spontaneous order become visible wherever the conditions of just coordination are absent. Markets require background institutions: law, trust, property rules, contract enforcement, norms against fraud, limits on coercion, access to information, some real measure of competition. They require goods that can be meaningfully exchanged without being destroyed by the exchanging. And even then, not everything is governed well by market signals. Not every need becomes effective demand, not every harm gets priced, and not every affected party has a seat at the transaction.

Spontaneous order fails morally when the background conditions are unjust. If land was acquired through dispossession, market exchange in land begins from injustice, however voluntary each later sale appears. If workers lack real alternatives, labor markets reflect necessity as much as freedom. If housing is scarce — by law, by geography, by speculation — rent reflects power as much as service. If users cannot leave a platform, consent reflects lock-in. If ecological harms are externalized, prices mislead. If public infrastructure is privately controlled, market dependency can quietly undermine sovereignty. And if capital markets require exit, startup choices have been shaped before any founder appears to choose.

Austrian economics can understate this problem, because it tends to focus on coordination through voluntary exchange while paying less attention to how the field of exchange was structured in the first place — by law, history, ownership, and power. Moral economy cannot afford that inattention. A market is not just a process. It is an institutionally constituted process. The rules of property, contract, liability, money, corporate governance, bankruptcy, intellectual property, labor law, taxation, and public investment determine what market actors can do; change the rules and the spontaneous order changes with them.

Which means that appeals to “the market” usually conceal a prior act of design. There is no market without law, no price without property, no voluntary exchange without rules of contract, no entrepreneurship without institutions that define ownership, risk, and reward, no finance without enforceable claims — and no digital market without protocols, platforms, standards, permissions, and governance.
The question is not whether markets should be designed. They already are. The live question is whether they are designed to serve life or extraction.

sections.title: The Problem of Power in Austrian Thought
paragraphs: The Austrian tradition is powerful on knowledge and often strangely quiet on power. It understands that central planners lack knowledge, that markets coordinate dispersed information, that entrepreneurs discover opportunities, that prices transmit signals. It attends less to the way power decides which signals exist at all — whose knowledge counts, who can act on an opportunity, who bears the consequences of failure.

Power affects knowledge at every joint. A worker may know a process is unsafe and lack the authority to change it. A nurse may know what patients need and be overruled by the billing system. A maintainer may know the software infrastructure is fragile and have no funding to fix it. A tenant may know the housing market is exploitative and have no alternative to it. A founder may know what the company needs while the investors control the board. A community may know what the land means while the legal title sits elsewhere. Dispersed knowledge, in short, does not automatically become effective action.

Markets transmit knowledge through willingness to pay — but also through ability to pay, and the two are not the same. Those with more purchasing power speak louder. Those with urgent needs and little money are nearly silent. Future generations do not bid. Ecosystems do not bid. The poor bid desperately but faintly. Open-source maintainers create enormous value and capture almost none of it through price, so the price system barely hears them.

Power also shapes exit. The Austrian and liberal traditions lean heavily on exit as the market’s great discipline: if a seller mistreats buyers, they go elsewhere; if a platform fails its users, competitors emerge; if an employer mistreats workers, the workers leave; if a supplier overcharges, customers substitute. But exit is not always real. Network effects, switching costs, debt, immigration status, geography, professional specialization, medical need, housing scarcity, monopoly, data lock-in, and plain lack of capital can make exit formally possible and practically unavailable — and when exit weakens, the discipline of competition weakens with it. Dependency becomes extractable.

A moral economy must therefore join Hayek’s knowledge problem to a power problem. Alongside who knows?, it has to ask who can act on what they know, who controls the rules, who can leave and who cannot, who bears the losses and who captures the gains — and whose knowledge is ignored because it arrives without purchasing power. Without those questions, spontaneous order becomes a language that protects existing power.

sections.title: Entrepreneurial Discovery and Moral Discernment
paragraphs: Entrepreneurial discovery needs moral discernment for a blunt reason: opportunities are not morally equal. Austrian economics rightly sees that entrepreneurs discover mismatches, inefficiencies, unmet needs, and future possibilities. But a market opportunity can grow from many soils, and some are poisoned. There is opportunity in healing disease, and in exploiting addiction; in reducing waste, and in manufacturing obsolescence; in making housing more available, and in restricting its supply; in supporting open-source maintainers, and in enclosing their work; in helping founders build durable companies, and in pushing them toward exit; in reducing transaction costs, and in becoming the tollbooth through which every transaction must pass. If the structure permits it, the market will reward every one of these without distinction.

So the entrepreneur must ask not only what opportunity she sees but what good the opportunity serves. The investor must ask not only whether the thing can scale but what scaling it will do to people, communities, institutions, and the future. The policymaker must ask not only whether a measure promotes innovation but what kind of innovation it promotes, under what ownership, and toward what dependencies.

This is where the language of vocation returns. Entrepreneurship can be a vocation when it serves real need through responsible risk, creativity, and stewardship. It turns chrematistic — accumulation for accumulation’s sake — when discovery is aimed at gain without regard for the good being created or destroyed. The question is not whether entrepreneurs should seek profit; profit can discipline fantasy and sustain useful work. The question is whether the profit is the sign of service or the reward for extraction.

A moral economy needs entrepreneurs — but entrepreneurs formed by the virtues: prudence, justice, courage, temperance, humility, truthfulness, patience, and care for the common good. And it needs structures that support those virtues rather than punish them. A founder cannot remain a steward if every available capital path demands extraction. An investor cannot be patient if the fund structure demands liquidity. A platform cannot preserve a commons if its ownership requires enclosure. A worker cannot practice vocation in a firm that treats labor as expendable. A market cannot reward stewardship if its rules reward rent-seeking.
Which is why moral discernment cannot remain private. It must become institutional design.

sections.title: Market Discovery and the Commons
paragraphs: The Austrian tradition keeps its gaze on private property and price signals. But some of the most important discovery happens in commons. Scientific communities discover through shared inquiry; open-source developers through collaborative experimentation; cultural traditions through imitation and adaptation; local communities through practices of mutual aid, land use, and care; professional communities through standards of excellence tested by peer judgment. These are not markets, or not exactly. They are decentralized knowledge systems all the same.

Which suggests that Hayek’s insight about dispersed knowledge is bigger than the conclusion he drew from it. Prices are one way of coordinating knowledge, not the only way. Reputation, reciprocity, vocation, professional norms, gift exchange, peer review, shared mission, liturgy, custom, and commons governance coordinate knowledge too — and if we reduce every form of discovery to market entrepreneurship, we will misunderstand some of the most life-serving systems we have.

Open-source software is the clearest modern example. Much of the digital world runs on code created, maintained, reviewed, and improved through non-market or mixed-motive collaboration. Some contributors are paid; some volunteer; motives run from need to reputation to joy to ideology to ordinary employment. The value created is immense, and it is not always captured by the people who create it.

Then the market arrives. Companies build proprietary services on top of the open code. Cloud providers monetize the infrastructure. AI companies train on the public knowledge. Platforms capture user contributions. Investors go looking for scalable returns on shared foundations. Sometimes this supports the commons. Often it extracts from it.

A moral economy informed by Austrian insight should recognize the generativity of decentralized discovery — and refuse the assumption that private capture is discovery’s natural reward. The commons needs stewardship precisely because it creates value that markets may not sustain.

A richer picture of spontaneous order emerges: some spontaneous orders are markets, some are commons, and many are hybrids, each requiring governance appropriate to its nature. Market mechanisms can help; they must not be allowed to enclose everything they touch. Discovery is broader than entrepreneurship, coordination broader than price, value broader than whatever gets captured.

sections.title: Competition and Its Corruption
paragraphs: Austrian economics treats competition as a process of discovery rather than a static condition — another gift. Competition is not merely many firms selling identical goods at a given price — the bloodless textbook picture. It is rivalry, experimentation, learning, adaptation, entrepreneurial alertness. Competitors test different ways of serving customers; some succeed, others fail; the process reveals information nobody had. Real competition is dynamic — innovation, differentiation, timing, quality, reputation, imagination. It lets outsiders challenge incumbents, punishes complacency, and can discipline rent-seeking.

But competition can be corrupted, because firms will compete at whatever the rules make profitable. They may compete to serve customers better — or to manipulate them more effectively. They may compete to reduce waste — or to externalize costs. They may compete through innovation — or through regulatory arbitrage, union avoidance, tax avoidance, surveillance, planned obsolescence, dark patterns, and lobbying. Competition does not automatically produce moral outcomes. It depends on the rules of the game.

If the rules allow free pollution, competition may reward pollution. If they allow addictive design, competition may reward addiction. If capital markets reward rapid growth and exit, competition may reward unsustainable scaling. If labor law permits precarity, competition may reward worker insecurity. If data extraction is legal and profitable, competition may reward surveillance. In such cases the market has not failed for lack of competition. It fails because the competition is ordered toward the wrong ends.

This is why moral economy must shape the field on which competition occurs. Rules do not eliminate market discovery; good rules aim it at life. Safety standards, labor protections, environmental limits, antitrust, data rights, fiduciary duties, open standards, and mission-protecting corporate forms can all help ensure that firms compete by serving rather than extracting. The question is not competition or regulation. It is what kind of competition, under what moral architecture.

A race can be perfectly fair — but only if the rules prevent poisoning your rivals, bribing the judges, tearing up the track, or charging the spectators tribute on their way out. Markets are no different.

sections.title: Hayek, Tradition, and Moral Culture
paragraphs: Hayek was never simply a champion of individual choice. He also understood tradition. Many social rules evolve without being fully designed or rationally articulated. Customs, norms, legal principles, and moral habits can carry inherited wisdom no single mind could reconstruct from first principles, and a society that bulldozes such traditions in the name of rational planning may destroy knowledge it never knew it had.

This resonates deeply with moral economy. Traditions can preserve limits. They can encode hard-won knowledge about trust, obligation, craft, restraint, property, family, worship, and common life. They can protect goods that are difficult to justify in purely utilitarian or market terms. They can train desire itself, making certain forms of extraction unthinkable long before anyone has to make them illegal.

But traditions are morally ambivalent too. Some preserve wisdom; others preserve domination. Some protect the vulnerable; others exclude them. Some restrain greed; others rationalize hierarchy or sanctify inherited privilege. Tradition is not self-justifying, and Hayek’s respect for evolved order must be joined to moral judgment. A tradition deserves respect because it may contain wisdom, not because it is immune from critique — and we must keep asking what it serves, whom it protects, whom it harms, what goods it preserves, and what injustices it conceals.

Still, the insight is vital, because a market cannot function on law alone. It needs moral culture: people who do not exploit every loophole, monetize every vulnerability, abandon every obligation, or liquidate every inheritance. It needs trust, promise-keeping, craftsmanship, restraint, truthfulness, and concern for reputation in the deeper sense. It needs families and communities that form people capable of self-command, and religious and philosophical traditions that keep reminding everyone that not everything profitable is honorable.

When moral culture collapses, markets lean harder on regulation. When regulation is captured, markets grow more extractive. When extraction becomes normal, trust collapses further — and the system demands more surveillance, more enforcement, more contracts, more compliance, more defensive behavior. A free market without moral culture does not stay free. It becomes predatory, or it becomes bureaucratically overmanaged.
Moral culture, in other words, is not decoration on the market. It is part of the market’s infrastructure.

sections.title: Why Markets Still Require Moral Culture
paragraphs: Give the Austrians their due in one place: knowledge is dispersed; central planning is dangerous; prices carry information; entrepreneurs discover; spontaneous order can be beautiful; and economic life cannot be morally improved by ignoring incentives, scarcity, or unintended consequences. All of it stands.
And markets still require moral culture, for at least five reasons.
First, markets require trust. Contracts cannot specify everything, enforcement is costly, and if people lie whenever lying pays, exchange itself becomes fragile.
Second, markets require restraint. Not every vulnerability should be monetized, not every legal opportunity should be taken, and not every form of demand should be stimulated.
Third, markets require truthful valuation. Prices mean something only against background conditions — competition, transparency, internalized costs, real alternatives — that make them meaningful.

Fourth, markets require limits on power. Without antitrust, labor protections, data rights, property rules, and governance constraints, market actors can grow powerful enough to deform the market itself.
Fifth, markets require a vision of the good. They can coordinate means; they cannot rank ends. They can reveal what people are willing to pay for, but not what they ought to love.

This is the core argument against market idolatry. Markets are good servants and dangerous masters — powerful instruments of discovery and coordination that lose the moral conditions of their usefulness the moment they are promoted to highest authority. A market ordered toward life must be embedded in institutions that ask the questions the market cannot answer. What should not be sold? What should not be enclosed? Which forms of dependency should never be monetized, and which claims should be capped? Which goods require commons governance, and which work deserves protection even when its bargaining power is weak? Which forms of enterprise should be preserved from exit? Which forms of value cannot be reduced to price at all?
Without those questions, market discovery becomes the discovery of new forms of extraction.

sections.title: Austrian Economics and Non-Extractive Capital
paragraphs: What, then, should Non-Extractive Capital take from the Austrians?

Humility, first. A fund cannot know in advance which enterprises will flourish, which founders will discover real opportunities, which technologies will matter, or which markets will emerge. It must allow experimentation and must not overplan the life out of its portfolio.

Second, the weight of local knowledge. Founders often know their customers, their technologies, and their communities better than their investors do. Capital should support that judgment rather than dominate it, and governance should protect the mission without smothering entrepreneurial discovery.

Third, that prices and cash flows contain information. A company’s ability to generate durable surplus matters. Revenue, margin, customer retention, and repayment capacity are not morally dirty concepts; they can reveal whether an enterprise is serving real needs.

Fourth, that loss matters. Not every mission-driven enterprise should continue forever. Stewardship includes recognizing failure, reallocating resources, and declining to deplete the fund out of sentiment.

Fifth, that decentralized order is precious. Open-source ecosystems, local enterprise, founder-led companies, cooperatives, and community institutions often contain knowledge that centralized capital does not possess.

But the learning runs both ways. Austrian economics has things to learn from Non-Extractive Capital, starting with this: capital structure shapes discovery. If the only available capital demands exit, the market will duly “discover” exit-shaped companies. If finance rewards dependency, entrepreneurs will discover dependency. If investors seek uncapped upside, firms will be pushed toward scale, monopoly, and capture. The market process is not independent of the capital architecture that funds it.

It must learn, too, that prices can reflect power; that voluntary exchange can conceal necessity; that commons can generate value without being reducible to private property; that moral limits can protect markets from becoming extractive; and that freedom requires more than the absence of planning — it requires protection from domination.

A non-extractive market economy would preserve entrepreneurial discovery while changing the terms on which capital participates. It would ask capital to fund discovery without owning the future without limit. It would let founders build without forcing exit, and let firms respond to customers without making customer dependency the business model. It would reward useful service while capping disproportionate claims, and it would support spontaneous order while protecting commons from enclosure.
None of this is hostility to markets. It is an attempt to retrieve them.

sections.title: The Beauty and Limits of Freedom
paragraphs: Austrian economics is animated, at bottom, by a love of freedom. At its best this is not merely freedom for capital. It is freedom for human beings — to act, discover, exchange, build, associate, and use knowledge no central authority possesses. It is freedom from bureaucratic arrogance, from coercive planning, from the flattening of local difference, from the dead hand of imposed order.

That freedom matters. A moral economy that suffocates initiative will fail. People need room to create, communities to adapt, entrepreneurs to test, workers to move, families to choose, civil society to organize. The future needs room to surprise us.

But freedom without moral architecture becomes domination by the powerful, and the word “free” starts doing very strange work. A worker who is “free” to accept unsafe labor or starve has a freedom that is merely formal. A tenant “free” to accept unaffordable rent or leave the city has a freedom that is thin. A founder “free” to accept extractive capital or shut down is free in a heavily constrained sense, and a user “free” to leave a platform that has captured her network and her data is free only in performance. A community “free” to accept infrastructure dependency because no public alternative exists enjoys what is best called dependency in liberal dress.

True freedom requires conditions: alternatives, limits on domination, access to the goods that make agency possible, institutions that keep one person’s liberty from becoming another person’s enclosure — and, once again, moral culture.

This is where Austrian freedom must meet Christian and classical moral economy. Freedom is not merely choice. It is the capacity to act toward the good without domination, and markets can support that freedom only when they are ordered within a moral and institutional framework that protects persons, communities, and commons from extraction. The goal is not less freedom; it is freedom without domination.

sections.title: The Transition to the Corporation
paragraphs: Austrian economics helps us see the market as a process of discovery. But modern economic life is not made only of markets. It is made of firms.

The corporation is not simply a participant in the market. It is a legal and governance structure that shapes how capital, labor, ownership, risk, and purpose are organized. It can enable productive enterprise at scale, coordinate complex work, accumulate knowledge, steward assets, and serve customers across generations. It can also become a sovereign institution. When corporate law, shareholder primacy, limited liability, capital markets, and managerial incentives align around financial return, the firm becomes less a community of production and more a vehicle for capital extraction. Markets may still churn away around it; inside it, power runs on hierarchy, ownership, contract, and law.

Austrian economics spent a century warning about the dangers of state planning. But the modern corporation can also plan, command, allocate, surveil, and dominate. A platform corporation may coordinate millions of market participants while itself functioning as a private regulator. A multinational may shape law, labor markets, infrastructure, and public dependency across borders. A financialized corporation may reorganize productive life around shareholder return.

That is why the next chapter turns to the corporation. To understand extraction in modern capitalism, it is not enough to understand markets. We must understand the legal architecture of the firm — how ownership becomes control, how control becomes sovereignty, how sovereignty becomes extraction, and how enterprise might be governed otherwise.
The Austrian tradition reminds us that centralized power is dangerous. The corporation is about to show us that the state never held a monopoly on it.

For a civilization that describes itself as market-based, we spend remarkably little of our lives in anything resembling a market. Most of the working world does not show up each morning to haggle; it shows up to be managed. Modern economic life happens overwhelmingly inside firms — through hierarchy and management, employment contracts and ownership rights, boards, financing agreements, intellectual property, procurement systems, supply chains, platforms, and governance structures. The market, in short, is only one institution of modern capitalism.

This matters because a market and a corporation are different kinds of thing. A market coordinates many independent actors through exchange. A corporation coordinates people, assets, capital, knowledge, contracts, and strategy under a single legal form — and that form can do things no human being can. It can act across time; own property; sue and be sued; borrow, employ, acquire, and lobby. It can outlive its founders and every worker who ever passed through it, and it can concentrate decision-making power on a scale no individual could ever wield alone.

The corporation is one of the most powerful institutional inventions in history. It can serve life: gathering resources for productive purposes no household or small partnership could undertake alone, building infrastructure, coordinating research, employing thousands, producing complex goods, carrying institutional knowledge across generations. Hospitals, factories, software systems, energy grids, laboratories, logistics networks — whole categories of enterprise exist only because some institution can persist beyond one person’s working life. But the same form can become the vehicle through which capital escapes ordinary human limits.

That is the ambiguity at the heart of the corporate form. A corporation can be a community of production ordered toward a useful purpose, or an asset — owned by capital, governed for capital, optimized for capital, disciplined by capital. A vessel of enterprise, or a machine for extraction. The useful question is therefore not whether corporations are good or bad in themselves, but what they are ordered toward, who controls them, whose claims govern them, what limits restrain them, and whether their legal architecture makes stewardship durable or fragile.
Inside the corporation, moral economy stops being an argument and becomes legal architecture.

sections.title: The Firm as Moral Architecture
paragraphs: A firm is not merely a bundle of contracts, though you can see why people say so. Contracts are everywhere in business life: employment contracts, supplier agreements, customer terms, leases, licenses, loan agreements, shareholder agreements, investor rights, procurement contracts, insurance policies, service-level agreements. A firm is indeed partly made of paper. But it is more than the sum of its signatures.

A firm is an organized human institution. People give years of their lives to it. Knowledge accumulates inside it, relationships form around it, customers come to depend on it, suppliers organize their own production around its orders. A community may be shaped by its presence or its absence. Workers develop skills, identities, habits, and expectations through it, and its decisions reach into land, housing, public infrastructure, political life, and technological dependency.

And a firm has a purpose, whether it admits one or not. It may exist to make bread, build software, provide healthcare, produce energy, manage housing, run a school, manufacture tools, conduct research, host a platform, or finance other firms. It may serve that purpose well or badly, preserve it across time, or trade it away for growth, liquidity, or control.

This is why corporate governance is morally serious, however dry the paperwork looks. Governance determines who has authority to decide what the firm is for — who can redirect surplus, force a sale, change strategy, replace management, issue shares, take on debt, sell assets, alter the mission, merge, liquidate, or distribute profits — and who gets to speak when purpose and profit collide. Every governance structure is an answer to a moral question: who is entrusted with the enterprise, and to what end?

Sometimes the answer is explicit. A cooperative says the enterprise exists for the benefit of its members; a foundation-owned company, to serve a mission protected beyond investor control; a family business, as an inheritance and responsibility across generations. A public company answers, in practice if not always in law: to increase shareholder value.

The danger comes when that last answer gets treated as technical rather than moral. Shareholder primacy is not merely a financial theory. It is a doctrine of institutional purpose — it tells managers, boards, investors, analysts, lawyers, and employees what the company is finally for. Other goods matter, on this view, insofar as they feed shareholder value or because law, reputation, or strategy demands attention to them. The mission may be real; workers, customers, and communities may matter. But if shareholder value is the governing end, every other good remains conditional.

That is a teleology, whatever it calls itself. And the corporation becomes sovereign precisely when this teleology hides under the language of neutrality, efficiency, fiduciary duty, or market discipline.

sections.title: The Promise of the Corporate Form
paragraphs: Before criticizing the corporation, we should be honest about why it conquered the world. It solves real problems well.

The corporate form lets enterprise outlive its owners. It pools capital from many investors, funds complex projects that need large money up front, and lets risk be organized, delegated, and limited. It creates a recognizable legal person that can hold assets, enter contracts, borrow, sue, be sued, and keep going while generations of human beings arrive and depart. These are not trivial achievements. Railways, hospitals, energy systems, telecommunications networks, pharmaceutical research, large-scale manufacturing, aviation, semiconductors, complex software infrastructure — all require coordination beyond the reach of an ordinary partnership. The corporation can gather dispersed resources into a durable institution.

It also separates the enterprise from the personal lives of its participants, which can be a mercy. A founder’s death need not destroy the company. Investors can back productive work without running it day to day. Workers can join a stable institution without personally owning its assets. Customers and suppliers can contract with the firm itself rather than renegotiate with a changing cast of individuals.

That continuity can serve life. A good corporation preserves knowledge, provides stable work, invests for the long term, maintains useful infrastructure, and serves its customers for decades. Some companies become institutions of trust, holding craft, memory, relationships, and productive capacity that would vanish if every enterprise dissolved with its founders.

So the moral problem is not corporate continuity; it is continuity without accountability to purpose. A corporation can outlive people — but what, exactly, lives on? A mission? A community of production? A brand? A legal shell? A claim on cash flows? A machine for return? The corporate form gives enterprise a kind of institutional body. Moral economy asks what soul animates it.

sections.title: Limited Liability, Externalized Risk, and Moral Distance
paragraphs: Limited liability is one of the defining features of modern corporate life, and one of its strangest gifts: shareholders can invest in a company without being personally liable for its debts and harms beyond the amount they put in. Lose your investment, and the losing stops there.

This makes investment dramatically easier: it encourages capital formation, lets people back risky ventures without staking their entire personal estate, and enables pooling on a grand scale. It can serve life, too. Innovation requires risk, infrastructure requires investment, and a society that holds every investor personally liable for every possible failure will see many useful enterprises never financed at all. Limited liability lets capital move toward uncertain but socially useful projects.

But it also creates moral distance. It lets investors take the upside while capping the downside. It separates control, benefit, and responsibility. It can encourage risk-taking whose costs land on workers, creditors, communities, ecosystems, or the public — and it lets owners structure enterprises so that harms are penned inside thinly capitalized entities while the profits flow somewhere safer.

The problem is not limited liability as such but limited liability without countervailing responsibility. A shareholder profits from cost-cutting that makes a company fragile; employees bear the job losses when it fails. A parent company profits from risk-taking subsidiaries in particular jurisdictions; legal separation quarantines the liability. Investors profit from environmental harm whose full cost never reaches the books. A private equity sponsor profits from debt-financed distributions; creditors and workers carry the collapse risk. A platform profits from armies of independent contractors while avoiding an employer’s obligations. In each case the architecture does the same quiet work: it lets upside and responsibility part company.

That separation is one of extraction’s favorite conditions. If capital collects the gain while others absorb the fragility, the return becomes morally suspect. If investors can steer strategy while disclaiming its consequences, control becomes extractive. If the corporate form shields owners from the harms generated by their own pursuit of return, the legal form itself has become a moral problem.

The answer, again, is not abolition but embedding: limited liability must sit inside stewardship, paired with duties, capitalization requirements, liability rules, transparency, worker protections, environmental responsibility, governance checks, and limits on distributions that weaken the enterprise. It must not become a license to privatize gains and socialize losses. Limited liability can support productive risk. Abused, it becomes a technology for externalizing moral responsibility.

sections.title: From Chartered Purpose to General Incorporation
paragraphs: Corporations were not always ordinary. For centuries, a corporation typically came into being by charter, for a stated purpose: to build infrastructure, conduct a trade, administer a public function, manage a university, govern a municipality. Corporate privilege was not a general private right but a grant made under conditions, usually with at least a claim of public interest.

That older world was no golden age. Chartered companies could be monopolistic, colonial, violent, corrupt, and deeply extractive; a public charter guaranteed nothing, and sometimes simply created a privileged vehicle for domination. But the idea of chartered purpose preserved something important: corporate existence is not natural. It is legal. The corporation is created by law, receives its privileges from the political community, and therefore owes an answer to some purpose beyond private gain.

General incorporation changed the arrangement. Britain made incorporation a matter of simple registration in 1844 and extended general limited liability in 1855; other jurisdictions followed, and forming a corporation became a routine administrative act rather than a favor from the state. This was progress in many respects — it democratized corporate formation, curbed arbitrary state favoritism, let far more people start companies, and fed real economic dynamism.

But as incorporation became ordinary, the public question of purpose quietly weakened. The corporation came to look like a neutral legal container whose purpose could be whatever its owners wanted, within the boundaries of law. Over time — especially under the influence of capital markets and shareholder-centered governance — the practical purpose of the business corporation became ever more tightly identified with financial return.

This was a major shift in moral imagination. When a legal form created by public authority is treated as a private instrument for capital accumulation, a once-obvious question becomes hard to ask: what does society receive in exchange for granting this form? The corporation keeps its privileges — legal personality, asset ownership, limited liability, perpetual life, entity shielding, access to the courts — while its obligation to the common good goes diffuse.

This is one reason the corporation can become sovereign. It is created by law, yet often carries itself as if it stood above ordinary democratic moral judgment. Ask it about purpose and it invokes private property; about responsibility, contract; about justice, market discipline; about harm, legal compliance.

Moral economy must recover the public nature of corporate privilege. A corporation is not a natural person exercising freedom; it is an institution authorized by law. Its privileges were designed — which means its responsibilities can be designed too.

sections.title: Colonial Corporations and the Fusion of Commerce and Sovereignty
paragraphs: The corporation’s sovereignty is not merely a metaphor. The most powerful early corporations exercised political and military power directly.

The Dutch East India Company, chartered by the States General in 1602, is usually counted the first joint-stock corporation with freely tradable shares — and its charter empowered it to negotiate treaties, wage war, coin money, and establish colonies. The English East India Company, chartered in 1600, began as a trading venture and ended by governing much of the Indian subcontinent, taxing millions and keeping a private army that by the early nineteenth century numbered some 200,000 men, roughly twice the size of Britain’s own; the Crown did not assume direct rule until 1858. Companies like these operated across oceans, governed territories, raised armies, extracted resources, controlled trade, and shaped imperial expansion. Commerce and sovereignty were fused in a single institution.

This history matters because it exposes a deep temptation built into the corporate form: private capital acquiring public power. The colonial corporations were not simply businesses operating in markets. They were instruments of empire. They turned trade routes into systems of domination, converted land, labor, resources, and political authority into commercial gain, and operated in the blurred space between state and market, public authority and private profit.

This is one of the most extreme forms of extraction. It combined enclosure, monopoly, violence, political privilege, racial domination, and financial return. It demonstrates that market language can conceal coercive power when the background conditions are imperial, and that the corporate form lets responsibility disappear into distance: investors in one place drew steady benefit from domination elsewhere, and rarely had to look at it.

The modern corporation is not identical with the colonial chartered company, and it would be careless to treat every corporation as a continuation of empire. But the history stands as a warning: corporate power can become political power, and political power can be used to secure corporate gain.

That pattern has not disappeared. Corporations no longer raise armies in the old way, but large firms today can shape law, infrastructure, speech, public knowledge, labor markets, tax systems, urban development, digital dependency, and geopolitical strategy. Platform companies can govern access to communication; financial institutions can discipline states through capital flows; pharmaceutical companies can shape access to medicines; energy companies can influence climate policy; technology firms can make themselves indispensable to public administration. When a corporation controls infrastructure on which public life depends, it exercises quasi-sovereign power. Who, then, governs the governors?

If corporate power reaches into public life, private ownership alone cannot justify it. Moral economy must ask whether such power is accountable, whether affected communities have a voice, whether essential infrastructure should be governed as commons, whether public dependency has become a source of private extraction, and whether corporate rights have outgrown corporate responsibilities.
The colonial corporation is a warning from history: commerce without moral and political limits can become sovereignty for profit.

sections.title: Shareholder Primacy
paragraphs: Shareholder primacy is the doctrine, explicit or implicit, that the corporation should be governed primarily for the benefit of its shareholders. In its strongest form, managers are agents of the shareholders and should maximize shareholder value within the bounds of law. Other stakeholders — workers, customers, suppliers, communities, ecosystems — matter instrumentally, insofar as their well-being affects long-term profitability, reputation, compliance, or risk. The ultimate claimant is the shareholder. The doctrine even has a classic citation: in Dodge v. Ford (1919), the Michigan Supreme Court informed Henry Ford, who wanted to plow profits into higher wages and cheaper cars, that “a business corporation is organized and carried on primarily for the profit of the stockholders.”

This is not simply a legal doctrine. It is a moral anthropology of the firm: the corporation imagined as something property-like, shareholders as residual claimants whose financial interests define success, the firm’s surplus as belonging in the decisive sense to capital, managerial discretion as dangerous unless disciplined by owners, governance as a mechanism for aligning management with shareholder return.

There are reasons the view became attractive, and they are not silly ones. Managers can become self-serving, firms flabby, capital wasted; shareholders bear risk and need protection from managerial abuse. In The Modern Corporation and Private Property (1932), Adolf Berle and Gardiner Means had already documented how thoroughly ownership had separated from control in the large American firm, and a governance theory that disciplines managers with measurable performance looks practical and rigorous. Milton Friedman gave the doctrine its most famous statement in a 1970 New York Times Magazine essay whose title said it all: the social responsibility of business is to increase its profits.

But shareholder primacy solves one agency problem by creating a moral problem. It asks, with great ingenuity, how shareholders can control managers — and rarely why shareholders should be the ultimate principals in the first place.

Consider who else is in the room. Workers invest years of their lives in a company. Customers may depend on its reliability. Suppliers may be shaped by its practices. Communities provide infrastructure, education, legal order, and trust. Founders may have built its mission and culture. Public institutions may have subsidized its research or absorbed its risks. Ecosystems bear its externalized costs, and future generations will inherit its consequences. Why should capital’s claim govern all of these relationships?

The usual answer is that shareholders own the company. This is imprecise. Shareholders own shares — not the company, and not in the way one owns a bicycle. The corporation owns its assets; what shareholders hold are financial and governance rights defined by law, and rights defined by law could be defined differently. Shareholder primacy is not a fact of nature. It is an institutional choice.

And like every institutional choice, it forms behavior. Managers judged by shareholder return will prioritize shareholder return. Executives whose compensation is tied to stock price will care about the stock price. If investors reward buybacks, cost-cutting, mergers, leverage, and asset-light strategies, corporations will learn those patterns. And if capital markets punish long-term stewardship whenever it lowers short-term returns, stewardship becomes fragile.
This is how capital becomes sovereign inside the firm — not by commanding openly, but by defining the metric of success.

sections.title: The Firm as Asset
paragraphs: When shareholder primacy dominates, the firm comes to be seen as an asset.
