paragraphs: The first principle has the least inviting name. “Subsidiarity” comes from the Latin subsidium, the term for Rome’s reserve troops — the ranks that stood behind the front line and came forward only when the fighting required them. That picture is the principle. Help should stand ready, and it should stand back.

Formally, subsidiarity is a guiding principle for organizing social and economic life: decisions should be made at the lowest level competent to handle them, and higher authorities should intervene only when necessary. Quadragesimo Anno gave it the formulation everyone still cites: “it is an injustice and at the same time a grave evil and disturbance of right order to assign to a greater and higher association what lesser and subordinate organizations can do.” Every social institution, Pius XI went on, exists to furnish help — subsidium — to the members of the social body, “and never destroy and absorb them.” In 1931 the immediate target was the totalitarian state, then busily absorbing everything in sight. But the principle cuts in more than one direction, which is exactly what makes it useful here.

For enterprise, subsidiarity has practical consequences. Local authority matters: families, workers, communities, and enterprises should retain real decision-making power over their own affairs. Centralized oversight is conditional: when higher levels — government, regulators, investors, fund managers — do step in, their job is to support local decision-making, never to dominate it. And accountability requires clarity: an intervention from above must be transparent, justified, and limited to the scope of the actual need. (The European Union liked the idea enough to write it into the Treaty of Maastricht in 1992, which is how a term from Jesuit social theory ended up in Brussels legalese.)

In practice, subsidiarity helps prevent extraction by distributing authority and responsibility. Extraction, as Part IV kept discovering, loves distance. Distant capital, distant managers, and distant policy-makers find it easy to impose solutions that benefit themselves while undermining the local stakeholders who must live with the results; the spreadsheet never has to look anyone in the eye. Subsidiarity shortens the distance. Employees who understand local operations shape the decisions that affect productivity and safety. Neighborhoods and municipalities affected by a corporation’s operations get a genuine voice in planning and oversight. Firms, co-ops, and community initiatives adapt to local conditions instead of being subordinated to rigid, top-down mandates.

What this preserves is worth naming precisely: agency, local knowledge, and moral responsibility embedded in the context of action. Readers of chapter 11 will notice a family resemblance. Hayek argued that the knowledge an economy runs on is dispersed, local, and impossible to centralize without loss. Subsidiarity is the moral form of the same insight. The difference is what each trusts to do the coordinating: Hayek relied on price signals, while CST asks for governance — institutions in which the people who hold the knowledge also hold real authority, and answer for how they use it.

Two cautions belong here. Subsidiarity is no license for local tyranny — a company town, a domineering patriarch, and a homeowners’ association can all be despotisms in miniature — and higher levels of authority exist precisely to correct abuses that exceed the local capacity for resolution. Nor is it decentralization for its own sake; the point is competence and accountability at the right scale, not smallness as an aesthetic. What the principle guarantees is narrower and more valuable: extraction cannot occur simply because authority is centralized and detached.

When this book reaches Steward Ownership and Non-Extractive Capital in the chapters ahead, subsidiarity will be doing much of the quiet work. A company whose control rests with the people responsible for its mission rather than with whoever bought the shares most recently, a fund that finances an enterprise without acquiring the right to run it — these are subsidiarity translated into corporate and financial architecture.

sections.title: Solidarity: The Structural Alternative to Extraction
paragraphs: In August 1980, workers at the Lenin Shipyard in Gdańsk went on strike, locked the gates, and named their movement Solidarność — Solidarity. Within a year it had ten million members, roughly a third of Poland’s working-age population, and within a decade the government it confronted was gone. The men and women at the gates were not quoting encyclicals, but the pope watching from Rome was Polish, and when John Paul II sat down in 1987 to write Sollicitudo Rei Socialis, the word had acquired a track record. Solidarity, he wrote, “is not a feeling of vague compassion or shallow distress at the misfortunes of so many people,” but “a firm and persevering determination to commit oneself to the common good” — to the good of all and of each, because all are really responsible for all. A determination, note, not a sentiment. Something you commit to, and keep committing to, the way the shipyard workers kept showing up at the gates.

That is the second principle. Solidarity complements subsidiarity by insisting on mutual responsibility across society, and the tradition is emphatic that it means more than charity. Charity, however admirable, is occasional, discretionary, and flows downhill at the giver’s pleasure. Solidarity is an ethical framework built on the recognition that we are interdependent whether we like it or not — that risk, in a connected economy, is always shared, and the only question is whether it is shared fairly. Where subsidiarity governs the distribution of authority, solidarity governs the distribution of care and obligation. Under it, wealthy and powerful actors are answerable for their impacts on vulnerable populations; institutions carry a standing duty to prevent the systematic exploitation of labor, environment, and social capital; and communities, enterprises, and governments are expected to act in ways that build one another’s resilience rather than one another’s dependence. This is the soil in which the Latin American church would later grow its “preferential option for the poor,” formalized at the Puebla conference of 1979: when institutions are being designed or judged, the people with the least power get the first claim on attention, precisely because they are the ones structural harm reaches first.

The practical force of the principle is that it moves moral concern out of the realm of one-off good deeds and into ongoing institutional obligation. A company, for instance, does not satisfy solidarity by publishing a corporate social responsibility report with a photograph of a wind turbine on the cover. It satisfies solidarity by embedding the obligation in its governance — by being structured so that workers, users, communities, and ecosystems actually benefit from its operations, and so that its externalities are minimized or equitably shared rather than quietly exported to whoever cannot object.

In a financialized economy, the principle has three sharp applications, each aimed at a pathology Part IV catalogued. Solidarity prevents debt from becoming domination: repayment terms must account for the borrower’s capacity and resilience, a standard under which a loan engineered to trap is not finance but predation with paperwork. It blocks rentier dominance: rent should reward contribution, not scarcity or dependence — collecting because you built something is one thing; collecting because you got there first and locked the door is another. And it forbids platform enclosure: shared infrastructure, data, and knowledge should remain accessible and fairly governed, not fenced and metered by whoever assembled the fence. In each case solidarity operates as a structural tool — an obligation encoded in the organization of institutions, finance, and markets, which is to say generosity with the option to decline removed.

sections.title: Structural Sin and Economic Systems
paragraphs: The vocabulary for this section was forged a hemisphere away from Rome. In 1968 the Latin American bishops met at Medellín, in Colombia, and looked out at a continent of debt peonage, landless laborers, and comfortable elites, and reached for a phrase no papal document had used: the poverty around them was “institutionalized violence.” Violence, that is, with no assailant anywhere in view — built into land tenure, credit terms, and wage structures, doing its damage on schedule while everyone involved remained personally polite. Puebla followed in 1979, and John Paul II — no friend of the Marxist analysis that traveled with some of this theology — nonetheless kept the structural insight and gave it doctrinal form in Sollicitudo Rei Socialis: there exist “structures of sin,” rooted in personal choices, above all in “the all-consuming desire for profit” and “the thirst for power,” which then harden into mechanisms that outlive and outreach any individual’s intentions.

The concept names something moral philosophy had long found awkward: injustice embedded in social, economic, and political systems rather than in anyone’s particular wickedness. A structural sin is an arrangement that systematically disadvantages some while advantaging others — and it needs no villain to run it. Everyone can be following the rules, meeting fiduciary duties, and acting on standard advice, while the arrangement does the harm on their behalf.

Financialized capitalism, as Part IV showed at length, offers a working museum of the type: debt regimes that trap households while enriching lenders; housing markets that privilege investors while excluding residents; private platforms that profit from the dependency of their users; corporate governance structures that prioritize shareholder value at the expense of workers, communities, and the commons. What makes such arrangements durable is that they are largely invisible — the legal framework permits them and frequently encourages them, and it is hard to see a wrong that comes with a compliance certificate. Here CST repeats the point it has been making since Leo XIII, and it bears repeating: legality does not guarantee justice. A system can be entirely lawful and quietly ruinous, and the remedy for it is not better sentiment but moral and institutional reform.

Because the disease is structural, so is the cure. The tradition points to mechanisms, several of which will occupy the rest of this book: mission-locked corporate governance, which ensures that an enterprise serves its purpose before it serves profit; repayment structures tied to durable surplus, which align capital’s returns with the productive health of the enterprises it finances; commons stewardship, which protects shared infrastructure, open-source resources, and ecological systems from enclosure; and labor protections and fair employment practices, which safeguard dignity where power is most unequal. Each of these takes solidarity out of the homily and writes it into the bylaws — durable institutions in which extraction is harder to arrange and stewardship pays.

sections.title: The Common Good in a Global Economy
paragraphs: The third principle is the oldest, and it gives the other two their bearings. The Second Vatican Council supplied the modern definition in Gaudium et Spes (1965): the common good is “the sum of those conditions of social life which allow social groups and their individual members relatively thorough and ready access to their own fulfillment.” As prose, this has the unmistakable aroma of a drafting committee. As a standard, it is quietly radical, and what it declines to say matters most: the common good is not aggregate wealth, and it is not GDP. A country’s output can climb for a decade while the conditions the definition actually names — access to work, housing, healthcare, education, a clean environment, social participation, meaningful opportunity — decay for most of its people, a divergence Part IV documented in some detail.

A moral economy therefore has one recurring question to ask of every institution: does it enhance the common good? Put to the major players, the question generates a job description apiece. Markets should coordinate knowledge, production, and exchange without undermining human dignity — coordination was always their gift, as Salamanca noticed and Hayek proved, and dignity was always their blind spot. Corporations should produce value not only for owners but for workers, communities, and future generations. Finance should fund productive activity without enslaving borrowers or subordinating enterprise to exit pressure. And governments should protect life-sustaining infrastructure and prevent the concentrations of power that make extraction easy.

Francis pushed the principle to its full extent in Laudato Si’: “The climate is a common good, belonging to all and meant for all.” The encyclical’s “integral ecology” makes the same move in general form — the conditions for human flourishing include the biosphere, so the common good cannot be truncated at a national border or at the edge of the present generation. The ledger includes people who have not been born and rainfall that has not yet failed.

What the common good contributes to the triad, then, is a horizon. It measures systems not by efficiency or growth alone but by their contribution to actual human flourishing, and it keeps the means and ends sorted: markets, firms, and finance are instruments. Instruments can be excellent. They are still not the point.

sections.title: Markets Ordered Toward Human Dignity
paragraphs: In 1991, with the Berlin Wall in fragments and command economies expiring across half of Europe, everyone wanted to know whether capitalism had won, and a number of people asked the pope. John Paul II’s answer, in Centesimus Annus, remains the tradition’s most precise statement of where it stands. If “capitalism” means an economic system that recognizes the positive role of business, the market, private property, and free human creativity, then yes — that is the system the Church can affirm. But if it means a system in which economic freedom is not circumscribed within a strong juridical framework placing it at the service of human freedom in its totality, the answer is no. Same word, opposite verdicts; everything hangs on whether the market sits inside a moral and legal order or has climbed on top of it. The same encyclical added the necessary cautions — there are “many human needs which find no place on the market,” and a warning against the “idolatry of the market” — and Benedict XVI later noted the gap the whole tradition works in: globalization has made us neighbors, he observed, but it does not make us brothers. Proximity arrived; fraternity is still on order.

Which returns us, with the whole tradition now in view, to the three principles this chapter set out to examine — and to how they function as a set. Subsidiarity ensures that decision-making sits where knowledge, responsibility, and accountability are greatest. Solidarity ensures that no one suffers harm simply because of unequal power or dependency. The common good supplies the standard for judging whether institutions, markets, and capital serve life rather than domination. Subsidiarity distributes authority. Solidarity distributes care. The common good orders purpose.

Applied to the problems of Part IV, the triad stops being doctrine and starts being a work order. Corporate governance can be restructured to embed stewardship, accountability, and worker participation. Financial systems can be designed to align investor return with productive purpose and durable surplus. Housing and infrastructure can be protected from speculative capture while remaining productive. Digital platforms and open-source commons can be governed to sustain access, knowledge, and community. Labor, ecology, and social well-being can be weighed alongside profitability when institutions decide — weighed as a matter of design, in the ordinary course of business, rather than remembered afterward in the annual report.

Beneath all of it lies the conviction that institutions are never neutral. Rules, governance, ownership, and finance are moral architecture. They encode priorities, allocate rights, and shape behavior as surely as a building’s floor plan shapes where people walk, and they do it whether or not anyone thought about the moral consequences at the drafting stage — which is an argument for thinking about them. Subsidiarity, solidarity, and the common good are design tools for exactly that work: they create durable frameworks for stewardship, they arrange incentives and constraints so that capital serves purpose, they protect people, communities, and commons from domination, and they supply the metrics for asking whether an enterprise contributes to flourishing or merely generates return.

One tradition, however rich, does not make an economy, and CST has never claimed otherwise. In the chapters ahead these principles meet their relatives: Protestant and Neo-Calvinist thought, ordoliberalism, the cooperative and mutualist traditions, and finally Non-Extractive Capital as an institutional response. CST supplies the moral vocabulary; the other traditions supply complementary institutional models, and the abstract critique of Part IV becomes, step by step, the concrete reform program of Part V. Recovery begins here — with the decision to design structures, finance, and governance around human flourishing rather than around the maximization of what can be extracted.

chapters.title: Chapter 15: Sphere Sovereignty, Stewardship, and Moral Markets

paragraphs: In May 1891 Pope Leo XIII issued Rerum Novarum, and Catholic Social Teaching received the charter we examined in the last chapter. Six months later, in Amsterdam, a Calvinist pastor turned newspaper editor turned party leader named Abraham Kuyper stood before the First Christian Social Congress and told the delegates that alms would not be enough — that the social question of the age demanded an “architectonic critique” of society itself. Not more charity: a review of the blueprints. Rome and Amsterdam, with no coordination and a long history of mutual suspicion, had reached the same conclusion in the same year. The troubles of the modern economy were troubles of structure, and structure is something human beings can redesign.

If Chapter 14 established Catholic Social Teaching as a moral framework for guiding institutions, this chapter widens the view to take in Protestant, Neo-Calvinist, ordoliberal, cooperative, and mutualist insights. Their bearers make an unlikely committee — a Dutch theologian-statesman, a cell of German economists and lawyers working under a dictatorship, twenty-eight weavers with a shop, a one-eyed Basque priest — and they would have disagreed with one another enthusiastically. But they converge on this book’s theme with uncanny consistency, and they offer complementary tools for structuring markets, finance, and enterprise so that stewardship becomes an institutional, economic category rather than merely a personal virtue.

This chapter, in other words, is where moral economy gets operationalized — where the principles of subsidiarity, solidarity, and the common good stop being matter for encyclicals and begin to guide ownership, governance, and enterprise design, with multiple historical and theological perspectives supplying the working drawings. The last chapter gave us principles. This one is about plumbing.

sections.title: Neo-Calvinist Sphere Sovereignty
paragraphs: Kuyper is one of those nineteenth-century figures who make the rest of us feel underemployed. Born in 1837 in a Dutch parsonage, he began as a village pastor, founded a daily newspaper in 1872 (De Standaard, which he edited for over four decades), founded the Netherlands’ first modern political party in 1879 (the Anti-Revolutionary Party), founded a university in 1880, led a secession from the national church in 1886, and served as prime minister from 1901 to 1905 — all while writing books and columns at a pace that periodically landed him in nervous collapse, from which he recovered by taking long mountain holidays and writing more books.

The university is the part that matters here. On 20 October 1880, in Amsterdam’s Nieuwe Kerk, Kuyper opened the Free University — “free” meaning free of both state and church control — with an inaugural address titled, precisely, “Sphere Sovereignty.” The new institution had five professors and eight students, a ratio that suggests either great faith or poor arithmetic, and Kuyper used the occasion to compress his whole social vision into one sentence, since become the most quoted thing he ever said: “There is not a square inch in the whole domain of our human existence over which Christ, who is Sovereign over all, does not cry: ‘Mine!’”

The sentence now enjoys a comfortable retirement on posters and coffee mugs in Grand Rapids, where it is usually read as private piety. Its real edge was institutional, and it cuts in a direction the mug-buyers do not always notice. If Christ is sovereign over everything, then nothing else is. No earthly institution may claim the whole of life — not the state, not the church, and not, Kuyper would insist we add, the market.

From that premise, Neo-Calvinist thought, articulated above all by Kuyper, developed the principle of sphere sovereignty — souvereiniteit in eigen kring, sovereignty in one’s own circle. The different spheres of life — family, church, school, civil society, economy, and state — each possess their own authority, their own responsibilities, and their own integrity. None holds its authority as a delegation from another sphere. The family is not a franchise of the state. The school is not a subsidiary of the church. The firm is not a department of either. Each sphere runs on its own kind of authority — parental love, scholarly truth-seeking, commercial service, public justice — and each goes wrong the moment it starts running on somebody else’s.

The doctrine unfolds into three working rules. First, the spheres are distinct but interrelated, and no sphere should dominate the others: families cannot be entirely subordinated to the state, the church cannot govern the market, and enterprises must respect the integrity of the communities, the labor, and the ecological systems they touch. Second, there is moral accountability within each sphere: each has duties to its own participants and to the broader society, so that a business is accountable to workers, customers, suppliers, communities, and the environment, not solely to its owners or investors. Third, there are limits on interference: higher authorities — state, regulators, investors — may rightly intervene only when a sphere can no longer govern itself, or when harm transcends its boundaries and lands on others.

Readers of the last chapter will notice the family resemblance to subsidiarity, and the two principles do complement each other, but they are not the same idea in different vestments. Subsidiarity is a ladder: it tells you which level of a hierarchy should act, and instructs the higher rungs to help rather than absorb the lower ones. Sphere sovereignty is a map: it says the social world is not one hierarchy at all but a set of adjoining territories with different constitutions, and it polices the borders. The ladder keeps power low; the map keeps power plural.

Applied to markets and corporations, sphere sovereignty operationalizes subsidiarity: companies retain autonomy in day-to-day decision-making, while governance structures ensure accountability when local decisions have broader societal impacts. This prevents distant capital or state authority from dominating local enterprise — and every reduction in domination reduces the opportunities for extraction.

What makes Kuyper so useful to moral economy is that his principle aims at two targets at once. The obvious one is statism, the annexation of every sphere by political power; the twentieth century would shortly supply demonstrations of where that leads. The less obvious target is what we might call market totalism — the annexation of every sphere by financial logic. When investor returns set the staffing of a nursing home, when a newspaper’s owner treats the newsroom as a yield-bearing asset, when family housing becomes a portfolio allocation and a university reorganizes itself around revenue capture, no army has crossed any border, but borders have been violated all the same. Kuyper gives moral economy a vocabulary the stale standoff of “market versus state” cannot supply. The question is never simply how much market; it is which spheres the market’s logic may enter, and on what terms.

Sphere sovereignty also supports pluralism in ownership and governance. It recognizes that cooperative enterprises, mutuals, mission-locked firms, and social ventures each inhabit distinct moral and functional spheres. No single model should dominate all markets; diversity preserves resilience, ethical pluralism, and stewardship — for the same reason a forester prefers mixed woodland to a monoculture. An economy in which every firm is built on the same extractive template fails the same way everywhere at once.

The Dutch took all this seriously enough to reorganize national life around it. For the better part of a century the Netherlands ran on confessional “pillars” — Protestant, Catholic, and secular communities each with their own schools, unions, newspapers, and eventually broadcasters — and Kuyper’s long campaign for equal public funding of religious schools ended in constitutional victory in 1917. The idea traveled, too: Kuyper carried it to Princeton in 1898 as the Stone Lectures, announcing Calvinism as a complete “life-system,” and a later Free University philosopher, Herman Dooyeweerd, spent a career elaborating sphere sovereignty into a formal social theory. The record is mixed, as records are — pillarization eventually hardened into parallel worlds that scarcely spoke, and took until the 1960s to soften — but the core insight has kept its value. A free society is not one big sphere. It is many small sovereignties, each with work of its own.

sections.title: Ordoliberalism and the Moral Constitution of Markets
paragraphs: The second tradition was born about as far from a festive church dedication as one can get: in a German university town in the 1930s, while the century’s most catastrophic demonstration of sphere-violation assembled itself outside the seminar-room windows. At Freiburg, the economist Walter Eucken and the jurists Franz Böhm and Hans Großmann-Doerth began asking, with lawyerly method and considerable personal courage, what had gone wrong with the German economy. Their answer founded ordoliberalism. They published a joint manifesto in 1936; after the war, their yearbook ORDO gave the school its nickname, borrowed from the medieval Latin for right order — a deliberate reach back past laissez-faire toward the Scholastic intuition that an economy is a moral order before it is a mechanism.

Their starting diagnosis was blunt. Interwar Germany had been Europe’s showcase of cartels — they existed by the thousand, legally enforceable, in everything from coal to lightbulbs — and the lesson the Freiburgers drew was that markets left entirely to themselves do not stay competitive. The strong combine, capture the rules, and begin collecting rather than creating; concentrated economic power then fuses with political power, and the results were visible from their windows. Böhm gave the problem its name — private power — and made it the central object of a lifetime’s legal scholarship.

Ordoliberalism, emerging from this German tradition, therefore insists that markets require legal and institutional frameworks to function morally and efficiently. Market order is not natural. Spontaneous exchange is insufficient; properly functioning markets depend on rules — competition law, property rights, contract enforcement — much as chess depends on the pieces moving only as the rules allow. Nobody calls chess unfree because the bishop must keep to its diagonals.

Second, competition is a moral instrument, valued not solely for efficiency but for preventing concentration of power and rent extraction. Böhm called competition “the most magnificent and most ingenious instrument of disempowerment in history.” Where later antitrust doctrine drifted toward asking only whether consumers got low prices, the ordoliberals asked whether anyone had accumulated the power to dictate terms — to suppliers, workers, towns, or legislatures. Efficiency was welcome. Disempowerment was the point.

Third, institutions shape incentives: finance, corporate law, labor rules, and property regimes must encourage enterprise that serves real human needs rather than mere financial gain. Eucken worked this up into constitutive principles for a competitive order — stable money, open markets, private property, freedom of contract, steadiness of policy — and, crucially, liability: whoever stands to gain must also bear the loss. An arrangement that lets investors keep the upside while handing the downside to workers, creditors, or taxpayers is not, on Eucken’s principles, a market at all; it is a casino where the house is other people. Readers who lived through 2008 may pause here for a moment of recognition.

It is worth pausing, too, over what this thinking cost. The Freiburg economists were not armchair moralists. Working with members of the Confessing Church in clandestine study circles, they drafted memoranda on the economic order of a Germany after Hitler — an activity for which, after the failed plot of July 1944, some of the circle were arrested, and Eucken himself was twice hauled in by the Gestapo for interrogation. Ordoliberalism is what a moral constitution for markets looks like when it is drafted by people watching, at close range, what a power without limits does to every sphere it touches. Eucken survived the war and died in 1950 in London, partway through a lecture series, still explaining order to anyone who would listen.

Around the Freiburg core stood two humanists of the same persuasion. Wilhelm Röpke left Germany in 1933 rather than teach under the new regime and spent his exile in Istanbul and Geneva; his A Humane Economy (1958) put the moral case in one sustained argument. The market, Röpke held, is a useful servant and a preposterous master: supply and demand can allocate goods, but they cannot generate the honesty, trust, self-restraint, and public spirit on which markets themselves depend. The market economy lives on moral reserves it does not create and can, if left unbounded, deplete. The constitutional scholar Ernst-Wolfgang Böckenförde would later give the thought its lapidary modern form — the liberal order rests on preconditions it cannot itself guarantee. Röpke’s colleague Alexander Rüstow, exiled in Istanbul, coined a word for the missing dimension: Vitalpolitik, a politics of life, attending to the conditions in which families, neighborhoods, and crafts can actually flourish. A book about capital that serves life is in no position to ignore a name like that.

After the war the school received the rarest gift in the history of economic ideas: a country to try them on. Alfred Müller-Armack supplied the label in 1946 — soziale Marktwirtschaft, the social market economy — and Ludwig Erhard ran the experiment. In June 1948, as economics director for the western occupation zones, Erhard paired the new Deutsche Mark with the abolition of most price controls, essentially overnight and essentially without asking. The story goes that General Lucius Clay summoned him: “Herr Erhard, my advisers tell me you are making a terrible mistake.” “Don’t listen to them, General,” Erhard is said to have replied. “My advisers tell me the same thing.” The exchange has doubtless been polished by decades of retelling, but the policy was real, and so were the consequences: goods reappeared in shop windows within weeks, and the recovery that followed acquired a name of its own, the Wirtschaftswunder. Erhard — whose 1957 book was titled Prosperity for All, which is about as ordoliberal as a title can be — spent his career insisting the miracle was no miracle, just order: an independent central bank (the Bundesbank, statutory from 1957), a cartel law the same year with Böhm’s fingerprints on the draft, and, alongside the ordoliberal scaffolding, the codetermination laws of 1951 and 1976 that seated workers on the supervisory boards of large firms. Economists still argue about which ingredient did what. The frame is what matters for us: postwar Germany treated the market as a constitutional creature — powerful, valuable, and legitimate only inside an order deliberately built to keep it serving.

Ordoliberal insights reinforce Catholic Social Teaching and sphere sovereignty by demonstrating how governance structures shape moral outcomes. By embedding norms, limits, and rules into the institutional fabric, ordoliberal frameworks prevent market domination by extractive actors while preserving incentives for entrepreneurship and productive investment. And ordoliberalism underlines a lesson this book keeps meeting from new directions: stewardship requires systemic design. Good intentions alone are insufficient. Markets, firms, and finance must be embedded in institutional rules that align incentives with long-term human flourishing — because rules, unlike intentions, are still on duty when the founders are gone and the pressure arrives.

The ordoliberals wrote the constitution of the market. About the constitution of the firm — who should own enterprises, and how — they said much less. For that we turn from professors to practitioners: to people who, having little theory to speak of, went ahead and built.

sections.title: Cooperative and Mutualist Traditions
paragraphs: On the evening of December 21, 1844, in a rented ground-floor room at 31 Toad Lane in Rochdale, a mill town near Manchester, twenty-eight weavers and artisans opened a shop. Their whole stock was butter, sugar, flour, oatmeal, and a few candles; their capital was twenty-eight pounds, saved at roughly a pound a member over months in which a pound was a serious sum for a weaver; and the local shopkeepers’ boys gathered outside to jeer. The Rochdale Equitable Pioneers, as they called themselves with more confidence than the inventory justified, are now commemorated as the founders of the modern cooperative movement, which counts its members worldwide in the hundreds of millions. Every one of those members is, institutionally speaking, a descendant of that shelf of oatmeal.

What made Rochdale matter was not the shop but the rulebook. The Pioneers had thought hard about why earlier cooperative ventures had failed, and their remedies were precise. Surplus would be returned to members as a dividend on purchases — the beloved “divi,” paid in proportion to how much you had shopped, not how much you had invested. Governance ran on one member, one vote, whatever anyone’s capital. Capital itself received a fixed and limited interest — compensated for its use, like any hired thing, and then excused from further claims. And the goods would be unadulterated and honestly weighed, which in an era of alum in the bread and chalk in the flour was by itself a minor moral revolution. Notice what the rules accomplish between them: capital is put on a salary. It is paid, fairly, for the work it does, and it does not thereby acquire the right to command the enterprise or to collect whatever the enterprise can be squeezed for. The Rochdale rules, elaborated and restated by the International Co-operative Alliance as recently as 1995, are anti-extraction architecture drawn up by working men who had experienced extraction from the receiving end.

The idea found its financial form in Germany. Friedrich Wilhelm Raiffeisen, mayor of a string of poor villages in the Westerwald, organized a bread society at Weyerbusch in the famine winter of 1846–47 and a society at Flammersfeld in 1849 that bought cattle so that farmers could escape the local usurers — men lending at rates that would have made a Renaissance pawnbroker blush. By 1864, at Heddesdorf, he had worked his way to the durable invention: a cooperative bank owned by its borrower-members, whose unlimited joint liability meant that the village’s own knowledge of character served as collateral. A Raiffeisen bank could lend to a farmer no city bank would look at, because his neighbors — who were on the hook for him — knew exactly what he was good for. Raiffeisen spent his later decades organizing such banks while going progressively blind, which slowed him less than one might expect. His urban counterpart Hermann Schulze-Delitzsch built the equivalent for artisans and shopkeepers, the Volksbanken, and between them the two models seeded the world’s credit unions and cooperative banks — among them the Dutch farm lenders that eventually merged into Rabobank, for decades one of the few large banks in the world with a triple-A rating and no shareholders to please.

Britain added the mutual for housing. From 1775, when Richard Ketley, landlord of the Golden Cross inn in Birmingham, organized his customers into a subscription club to build one another houses, the building society became the ordinary Briton’s route to a roof. It is worth recording what happened when that architecture was dismantled, because the experiment was run in public view. In the late 1980s and 1990s, ten British building societies demutualized, converting members’ collective inheritance into windfall shares — a process so lucrative to insiders and speculators that a word was coined for those who joined societies just to force conversions: carpetbaggers. Within about a decade and a half, every single one of the ten had been swallowed by a bank, broken, or destroyed. The most spectacular casualty was Northern Rock, demutualized in 1997, which swapped its members’ deposits for racy wholesale funding, and in September 2007 produced the first run on a British bank since the Victorian era — depositors lining up down the block — followed by nationalization. The mutual rules had not been quaint inefficiencies after all. They had been the brakes.

The most ambitious cooperative experiment belongs to the Basques. In 1941 a twenty-six-year-old priest named José María Arizmendiarrieta was posted to Mondragón, a battered industrial town still raw from the Spanish Civil War — in which he had served on the losing side and narrowly escaped execution. He was, by all accounts, a terrible preacher; he was also, it turned out, a genius of patient institution-building. He started a technical school in 1943. In 1956, five of its graduates founded Ulgor, a workshop making paraffin stoves, on cooperative lines. In 1959 came the pieces that made the system self-sustaining: Caja Laboral, a cooperative bank to finance new cooperatives, and Lagun-Aro, a mutual providing the social insurance that worker-owners, being technically self-employed, could not get from the state. Pay ratios were fixed at three to one from top to bottom. Arizmendiarrieta himself never held an executive post in any of it; he suggested, prodded, and taught, and died in 1976 owning practically nothing. The Mondragon Corporation today employs roughly seventy thousand people, most of them worker-owners, and ranks among Spain’s largest business groups. Its record includes failure — Fagor Electrodomésticos, the descendant of that first stove workshop, collapsed in 2013 — but the failure is itself instructive: instead of mass redundancy, the network absorbed the blow, redeploying most of Fagor’s workers into sister cooperatives. Losses fell on the system that had agreed to share them, not on whoever happened to be standing closest when the music stopped.

Across all these ventures the same design principles recur, and they are the ones the moral traditions of this chapter would predict. Governance resides with the people affected by the enterprise’s outcomes — workers, customers, or communities — so that authority and responsibility travel together. Surplus is allocated to mission: reinvested or distributed to strengthen the enterprise and serve its stakeholders, rather than funneled solely to investors. And shared responsibility builds resilience, a collective commitment to the long term that starves short-termist, extractive behavior of oxygen. The cooperative record demonstrates the chapter’s central claim in brick and ledger: stewardship can be encoded into organizational design, institutionalized through ownership, governance, and financial rules, and still pay its way.

sections.title: Christian Entrepreneurship Revisited
paragraphs: Chapter 7 traced the Protestant idea of vocation — work as calling, enterprise as service rendered before God. The traditions gathered in this chapter all inherit some version of that conviction, and one community turned it into a business method rigorous enough to audit: the Quakers. Barred from the universities, the professions, and Parliament by the Test Acts, English Quakers poured their energies into trade, and their scruples turned out to be assets. They pioneered the fixed price — haggling offended their conviction that there was one truthful price, and it incidentally meant a customer’s child could be sent to a Quaker shop without being fleeced. Their meetings enforced commercial discipline with a severity no modern regulator approaches: a Quaker who went bankrupt faced investigation by his meeting and, if found imprudent, disownment, because failing one’s creditors was a moral failure. The result was that Quaker credit became the best in England, and Quaker enterprises grew into household names — two of their banking partnerships became Barclays and Lloyds, and their chocolate dynasties gave the world Cadbury, whose George Cadbury moved the factory out of Birmingham’s smoke to the garden village of Bournville in 1879, and Rowntree, whose Seebohm Rowntree conducted the landmark 1901 survey of poverty in York, an employer funding social science that might indict employers.

What the Quakers embodied, the traditions of this chapter articulate as a general account of the entrepreneur’s vocation. Enterprise is a form of stewardship — the creating, maintaining, and coordinating of resources for the benefit of community, workers, and customers — and it carries three marks. The first is long-term orientation: decisions weighted toward durable surplus and societal benefit rather than rapid extraction or exit. The second is embedded accountability: the entrepreneur answers to multiple constituencies — employees, communities, regulators, investors, and, for the faith-driven, God or conscience — rather than to the share price alone. The third is ethical risk-bearing: gains and losses should track productive contribution, not exploitation — Eucken’s liability principle again, wearing its theological clothes. Taken together, these turn entrepreneurship into a moral practice. Enterprise becomes a means of serving life rather than a vehicle for accumulation that has forgotten what the money was for.

sections.title: Stewardship as an Economic Category
paragraphs: Run your eye back over this chapter’s cast — Kuyper’s spheres, the Freiburg jurists, the Rochdale rulebook, the Quaker meetinghouse — and a single insight keeps surfacing: stewardship can be institutionally encoded. It is not merely a personal ethic; it is a structural feature of the economy, something that can be written into charters and bylaws and loan agreements and left running after the founders are gone.

The most striking demonstrations come from a handful of founders who deliberately gave their companies away — to the companies themselves. Ernst Abbe, the physicist whose optical formulas built Carl Zeiss into the world’s great lens-maker, could have become its owner outright when old Zeiss died. Instead he created the Carl Zeiss Stiftung in 1889 and transferred the firm to it, then wrote the foundation’s 1896 statute like the constitutional document it was: profits were dedicated to the works, the workforce, science, and the surrounding city of Jena; the eight-hour day arrived in 1900, decades early for German industry; and no manager could earn more than about ten times the wage of a worker with a few years’ service. In Copenhagen, the brewer J. C. Jacobsen had already given Carlsberg to a foundation dedicated to science in 1876 — with the result that when the Carlsberg Laboratory’s Emil Christian Hansen worked out how to propagate pure yeast cultures in 1883, solving the ancient problem of spoiled beer, the foundation gave the method and the cultures away free, to competitors included. Robert Bosch split the two things ownership normally bundles: today the Robert Bosch Stiftung holds roughly ninety-two percent of the company’s shares but essentially none of the votes, which rest with a trust of seasoned stewards — dividends flow to charity, control flows to competence. Denmark repeated the pattern with the Novo Nordisk Foundation, which controls one of the world’s most valuable pharmaceutical companies. And in Britain, John Spedan Lewis — who had calculated as a young man that he, his father, and his brother were drawing more from the family stores than the entire workforce combined, and pronounced the arrangement “perfectly wrong” — transferred the business to its employees in trust settlements of 1929 and 1950, creating the John Lewis Partnership. These firms have outlasted virtually all of their conventionally owned contemporaries, which suggests that stewardship architecture is not a tax on performance. It looks more like a preservative.

Abstract the cases and you get a toolkit with four drawers. Governance structures — boards, cooperative assemblies, mission-locked entities — embed purpose into decision-making. Ownership rules — asset locks, non-tradable shares, foundation ownership — prevent extraction while still admitting investment. Financial design — repayment caps, risk-sharing agreements, financing tied to durable surplus — aligns capital’s incentives with productive outcomes. And regulatory frameworks supply the legal scaffolds that protect all of the above from extractive pressure: Britain’s Industrial and Provident Societies Act of 1852, drafted with the help of Christian Socialist lawyers, first gave cooperatives secure legal personality; Germany codified cooperative law in 1889; the United Kingdom created the community interest company, with its statutory asset lock, in 2004; and the first American benefit-corporation statute passed in 2010. Law, it turns out, can hold a door open just as easily as it can hold one shut. Viewed through this toolkit, stewardship functions as an economic category in the full sense — a way of governing how value is produced, allocated, and preserved — and it slots in beside subsidiarity and solidarity, ensuring that markets, enterprises, and finance operate inside a moral and institutional framework oriented to human flourishing.

sections.title: Plural Institutions for a Moral Economy
paragraphs: We can now lay the whole architecture out on the table. From Catholic Social Teaching: subsidiarity, which keeps authority at the level closest to knowledge and impact, and solidarity, which extends obligation across society so that no one is harmed merely for being dependent, and the common good, which supplies the evaluative horizon for every institutional decision. From the Neo-Calvinists: sphere sovereignty, which protects the integrity and autonomy of distinct social, economic, and community spheres against annexation by state or market. From the ordoliberals: the insight that markets are constitutional creatures, needing structural rules deliberately framed to sustain moral outcomes. From the cooperators and mutualists: working proof that stewardship can be operationalized in concrete enterprise structures and survive contact with competition. Six instruments, developed by traditions that barely spoke to one another, and they harmonize — a blueprint for an economy in which enterprise, finance, and markets can thrive without extracting life, community, or future capacity.

All of it prepares the ground for the institutional innovation this book has been walking toward, which the next chapter examines in detail: Non-Extractive Capital. The reader will recognize every component as it arrives, because each one translates a principle from these last two chapters into economic machinery. Steward ownership aligns governance with mission — sphere sovereignty and subsidiarity written into a shareholders’ agreement. Mission-locked structures prevent extraction through sale or exit — the asset lock doing for purpose what Abbe’s statute did for Zeiss. Evergreen finance and repayment caps let capital support durable enterprise without dominating it — the old usury insight of chapters 4 and 5, returned as term sheets. And the whole incentive design aligns stakeholders, workers, and investors with productive, life-serving outcomes rather than with the next liquidity event. Non-Extractive Capital, in short, is the practical recovery of stewardship at scale.

This chapter has laid the conceptual and historical foundation: principles from Rome and Amsterdam, constitutions from Freiburg, working models from Rochdale, the Westerwald, Mondragón, Jena, and Copenhagen. The next chapter shows the implementation — bridging moral philosophy, theology, and financial architecture to recover an economy ordered toward life.

The chapters behind us have been a recovery operation: the reassembly, piece by piece, of a moral vocabulary. Aristotle gave us the distinction between economy ordered toward life and accumulation without limit. Christianity deepened the concern by placing wealth under the judgment of charity, mercy, and love of neighbor. The Scholastics taught us to ask whether an exchange preserves justice and right relation; the Franciscans asked whether capital can be productive without becoming usurious. Salamanca saw markets as systems of coordination while exposing the ambiguity of trade in a world of empire. The Protestant traditions dignified work as vocation and wealth as stewardship, and warned that discipline without Sabbath becomes accumulation without rest. Smith gave us a moral psychology — sympathy, admiration, self-command, and the danger of rent-seeking. Modern economics gave us analytical power and let the language of ends grow faint. And financialized capitalism showed us what happens when capital becomes sovereign.
Now the question turns practical. How can capital be structured so that it serves life?
This is the question Non-Extractive Capital tries to answer.

Begin with what it is not. Non-Extractive Capital is not merely “ethical investment.” It is not philanthropy with a revenue model, and it is not ordinary venture capital with nicer language. Nor is it ESG, impact branding, or a moral preference layered on top of conventional ownership. It is an attempt to redesign the relationship between capital, enterprise, governance, surplus, and mission.

The central premise is simple. Capital may receive a fair return for real risk, service, and patience, but it must not gain unlimited power over the enterprise, its mission, its workers, its community, or its future.

That premise is ancient in spirit and contemporary in form. It echoes Aristotle’s warning against accumulation without limit and the Christian critique of usury and avarice. It echoes Scholastic proportionality, the Franciscan concern for productive capital and ethical lending, and Protestant stewardship. It echoes Smith’s suspicion of rent-seeking and concentrated commercial power. And it responds directly to the modern crisis of financialized capitalism. What it asks of capital is genuinely difficult: to serve without becoming sovereign.

sections.title: The Problem It Tries to Solve
paragraphs: Many mission-driven companies face a painful choice. They need capital in order to build — to hire, develop technology, reach customers, weather uncertainty, survive long sales cycles, or invest before revenue is stable. Without it they may remain fragile, underdeveloped, or dependent on grants; some fail before they ever get the chance to serve. But the capital available to them usually arrives with expectations that threaten the very mission it is meant to fund.

Conventional venture capital seeks outsized returns through growth and exit. The model can work — for the small number of companies capable of exponential scaling, winner-take-most dynamics, and an acquisition or IPO at the end of the road. It is poorly suited to a great many enterprises that are useful, durable, cash-flowing, mission-driven, infrastructure-like, or oriented toward stewardship rather than domination. A company may be good but not venture-scalable, profitable but not exit-oriented. It may serve a real need without ever producing monopoly returns. It may require patience rather than hypergrowth, may be designed to preserve a commons rather than enclose one, may be mission-critical with no prospect of becoming a unicorn, may generate durable surplus but never a speculative valuation.

When such a company accepts capital built around exit logic anyway, the company is gradually reshaped. Growth begins to matter more than resilience, valuation more than usefulness, market capture more than service, investor rights more than mission, exit more than continuity — until the company’s whole future is organized around liquidity.

This is not always because investors are malicious. They may be sincere, intelligent, even genuinely mission-aligned. But the structure speaks louder than intention. A fund designed to return capital through large exits must seek large exits. A term sheet designed to protect investor control will create investor control. A governance structure that gives capital the final word will, sooner or later, make mission conditional on capital’s consent. The result is structural drift.

Non-Extractive Capital tries to solve the problem by changing the underlying architecture. Suppose capital were repaid from durable surplus rather than exit value, and investor return were capped. Suppose control remained with stewards bound to mission, and ownership could not be bought and sold as a speculative asset. Suppose, in short, that an enterprise could take on capital without setting foot on a path that bends toward extraction. Part IV of this book was the diagnosis. This is the constructive answer.

sections.title: Steward Ownership
paragraphs: Steward Ownership begins from an idea that is simple to state and radical in effect: companies should be controlled by people who are responsible for their purpose, not by whoever can buy financial rights.

Conventional ownership bundles control and economic upside together. Those who own shares can influence or control the company, and the same shares may rise in value, be sold, or confer rights to future profits. The bundle creates a powerful incentive: control exists for the benefit of financial ownership.

Steward Ownership separates the elements. Control is held by stewards who are bound to the company’s mission. Economic rights are limited. The company is not treated as a speculative object to be bought, sold, and liquidated for maximum gain. Notice how much survives the arrangement: profits can still exist, capital can still be repaid, workers can still be paid, founders can still be compensated, investors can still receive return. What changes is that the governance of the company is protected from becoming an instrument of extraction.

None of this is anti-business. It is a different answer to the question of what a company is for. A steward-owned company exists to fulfill its purpose over time. It is not ownerless, not unmanaged, not allergic to profit. But profit is placed in service of mission, not mission in service of profit.

The steward, meanwhile, is not an absolute owner but someone entrusted with governance — which is why the language of stewardship fits so well. A steward does not treat the entrusted good as private loot. A steward cares for it, develops it, protects it, and passes it on. The steward has authority, and the authority is bounded by purpose. Steward Ownership thus gives institutional form to a theological and moral insight this book has met many times: ownership carries obligation.

In a steward-owned company, the obligation is not left to conscience alone. It is embedded in legal architecture. Transfer restrictions, mission locks, golden shares, foundation ownership, perpetual purpose trusts, veto rights, asset locks, and governance rules can all be used to prevent sale, mission drift, or speculative control. The exact legal form varies by jurisdiction. The moral principle does not: the enterprise is not a commodity. It is an institution entrusted to stewards for a purpose.

Nor is any of this hypothetical. After Carl Zeiss died in 1888, his partner Ernst Abbe created a foundation — the Carl Zeiss Stiftung, 1889 — and within two years made it the sole owner of the famous optical works; the Zeiss firms are foundation-owned still. Robert Bosch’s will pointed the same way: today a charitable foundation holds most of the share capital of Robert Bosch GmbH, while the votes rest with a separate industrial trust and the dividends flow largely into public-benefit work. Ownership without domination has been running large industrial companies for more than a century.

The design resists extraction directly. If capital cannot buy control, it cannot easily force exit; if mission is locked, it cannot easily be sold away; if economic rights are limited, ownership stops being a path to unlimited claim. And a company whose governance is entrusted to stewards can make long-term decisions that ordinary capital markets might punish.

Steward Ownership does not guarantee virtue. Stewards can fail — they can become complacent, self-serving, ideological, incompetent, or captured. Mission locks can be poorly designed. Governance can become rigid. The absence of speculative ownership does not automatically create good leadership. What Steward Ownership changes is the default. It makes extraction harder and stewardship more structurally plausible.

sections.title: Mission-Locked Governance
paragraphs: Mission is fragile when it depends only on founder intention. Founders may begin with clarity and courage. They may want to serve users, workers, communities, open-source ecosystems, ecological repair, or public infrastructure, and they may resist extraction personally, for years. But founders age, burn out, disagree, die, sell, or get outvoted. Investors enter. Boards change. Markets shift. Crises arrive. Competitors pressure margins, advisors recommend “professionalization,” employees want liquidity, and success attracts acquisition offers. A mission that lives only in the founder’s heart is vulnerable to everything that can happen to a heart.
Mission-locked governance turns purpose into structure. It asks what must be legally, financially, and institutionally true for the company’s mission to survive pressure.

The tools are concrete. A mission lock may define the company’s purpose in its governing documents. It may restrict sale to buyers who would violate the mission, require consent from a guardian entity before major changes, or prevent distribution of assets beyond defined limits. It may give veto rights to a foundation or purpose trust, require surplus to be reinvested in mission, protect worker participation or commons commitments, and define what cannot be sacrificed for financial return.

The point is not to freeze the company forever. A mission-locked company still needs adaptation — markets change, technologies change, customer needs change, strategies fail, and good governance must allow learning. But adaptation is different from liquidation. A mission lock says that the company may change how it serves its purpose, but it may not abandon the purpose in order to maximize financial return.

This matters most at the moment capital enters. Investors often ask for protections: information rights, repayment rights, covenants, board seats, liquidation preferences, veto rights, anti-dilution protections, drag-along rights, exit provisions. Some protections are reasonable — investors should not be deceived or recklessly disregarded. But investor protections become mission threats when they allow capital to override purpose.

So mission-locked governance asks for balance. Capital should be protected against fraud, negligence, and arbitrary misuse; mission should be protected against capital’s impatience, control, and hunger for liquidity. The company must be governable. The investor must be treated fairly. And the mission must remain sovereign over both. This is governance as moral architecture, a phrase we will need again before the chapter is out.

sections.title: Evergreen Finance
paragraphs: Conventional venture capital often relies on exit. Investors put money into a fund; the fund invests in companies; the companies are expected to grow rapidly; a small number of large exits returns the fund. The time horizon is limited, liquidity is essential, and the portfolio is shaped by the need for winners large enough to compensate for the losses.

The model produces a particular kind of company. It favors rapid growth, large addressable markets, scalable technology, defensible moats, high margins, and exit potential. It can support genuine innovation. It also pressures companies toward monopoly dynamics, enclosure, aggressive scaling, and financial narratives that may have little to do with their real mission.

Evergreen finance follows a different logic. An evergreen fund is designed to continue over time. It does not depend on liquidating all its investments within a short fund cycle. Returns can be recycled, capital can remain patient, and the fund can support companies that generate durable surplus rather than spectacular exits. It can think in terms of continuity rather than harvest.

This matters because time horizon is moral architecture. A short horizon pushes toward extraction; its question is how value can be increased and realized soon. A long horizon asks instead how the enterprise can become durable, resilient, and capable of producing surplus without harming its mission.

One caution before the enthusiasm runs away: evergreen finance is not automatically non-extractive. A permanent fund can still extract indefinitely if its return expectations are uncapped or its governance is not mission-aligned. Permanence can become another form of domination. But joined to repayment caps, mission locks, and Steward Ownership, evergreen finance can support patient enterprise. It can let a company grow at the speed of trust. It can let repayment come from real surplus and let investors receive return without anyone forcing a sale; it can let capital revolve into new enterprises once repaid, preserve relationships across time, and reduce the pressure to manufacture liquidity.

All of which aligns deeply with moral economy — and inherits one of its oldest problems. The Monti di Pietà faced it in another form: the fund must survive in order to serve. If capital is depleted, service ends. If preservation of capital becomes the highest good, mission drifts. Evergreen finance must navigate the same tension. A non-extractive evergreen fund preserves itself for the mission; it does not preserve the mission as a story that justifies the fund.

That takes discipline of a distinctly unromantic kind. The fund must track losses, cover its costs, avoid sentimental investing, protect its capital from depletion, price risk honestly, avoid becoming dependent on heroic fundraising, and generate enough return to continue serving. And then it must know when enough is enough.
Which is where repayment caps become essential.

sections.title: Repayment Caps and Anti-Usury Logic
paragraphs: A repayment cap defines the maximum return capital can receive. It is one of the simplest mechanisms in non-extractive finance, and one of the most morally significant. It says that capital may be compensated, but its claim is not infinite: once the agreed return has been reached, the financial claim ends or changes form. Capital does not own the enterprise’s future without limit.

This is the ancient problem of usury, addressed in contemporary form. The old usury debates asked when return on money becomes morally excessive — especially when it profits from need, or generates gain without real participation in production. The Scholastics and Franciscans developed distinctions around risk, loss, opportunity, productive use, and partnership. They did not all agree. But they shared the conviction that financial return requires moral justification. A repayment cap gives that conviction structural form.

Start with what the cap concedes. Investors may deserve return. They may have taken real risk, provided capital when the company needed it, waited, offered support, helped make productive activity possible. A fair return is not extraction.

Then comes what the cap insists: return must remain proportionate. The investor’s claim cannot expand forever simply because the company succeeds. It cannot become a permanent tax on the enterprise. It cannot convert temporary risk into perpetual ownership, or use early contribution as justification for unlimited future control.

For mission-driven companies the stakes are particular. Conventional equity gives investors uncapped upside if a company becomes highly successful, and in some contexts that may be acceptable. But in mission-driven or steward-owned contexts, uncapped upside pulls the company toward exit, speculation, and investor-centered governance — and the larger the possible gain, the stronger the pressure to realize it. A repayment cap reduces the temptation. It aligns capital with durable success while limiting capital’s claim upon that success: the company repays its investors from surplus, then continues serving its mission without being permanently governed by the capital that helped it grow.

This is not charity. It is disciplined proportionality — and the discipline is real, because the cap must be set with care. Too low, and investors may not participate, or may not be fairly compensated for risk. Too high, and the cap becomes symbolic while extraction remains possible. If repayment is too rigid, the company may be harmed; too loose, and the fund may not survive. If the calculation is too complex, governance becomes opaque; too simple, and it misses real differences in companies’ circumstances. The moral task is to design a cap that reflects risk, time, contribution, and mission.
A repayment cap, then, is not merely a number. It is a statement about the moral limits of capital.

sections.title: Durable Surplus Instead of Exit Value
paragraphs: The most important shift in Non-Extractive Capital is the shift from exit value to durable surplus. Conventional venture capital asks what a company will be worth to a future buyer or public market. Non-Extractive Capital asks whether the company can generate durable surplus while preserving its mission. These are profoundly different questions.

Exit value depends on what someone else will pay for the company. It is shaped by growth narratives, market comparables, strategic acquisition value, intellectual property, user base, revenue multiples, data assets, and future expectations. It can reward real value creation. It can also reward monopoly potential, dependency, enclosure, and speculative hype.

Durable surplus depends on the company’s own ability to generate more income than it needs for healthy operation, resilience, reinvestment, fair compensation, and mission integrity. It asks whether the company can live — not whether it can be sold, or dominate, or attract a speculative valuation, or promise exponential growth, but whether it can produce enough recurring surplus from real service to repay capital without harming itself.

The shift changes the meaning of success. A company that grows steadily, pays its workers well, serves customers honestly, maintains reserves, contributes to commons, and repays its investors over time may be highly successful even if it never exits. A company that raises at high valuations, burns capital, captures users, underpays maintainers, and sells itself to a monopolist may be financially celebrated and morally questionable at once.

And durable surplus is not mere profit. It must be surplus after the company has met its obligations to mission, workers, resilience, maintenance, customers, and future capacity. If repayment to investors comes only by underpaying workers, neglecting infrastructure, extracting from users, externalizing costs, or weakening the company, the surplus is not durable. It is disguised depletion.

This is why moral accounting matters. A repayment formula must know what counts as available surplus. It must distinguish cash that can rightly be distributed from cash needed for resilience. It must protect reserves, avoid forcing repayment during fragile periods, and prevent investors from being repaid through hidden extraction. The question is never simply whether there is cash. The question is whether there is morally distributable surplus.

The concept links straight back to the Monti. A charitable fund had to preserve itself without exploiting borrowers; a non-extractive fund must be repaid without extracting from its portfolio companies. Both require moral accounting. Durable surplus is the financial expression of capital serving life.

sections.title: Productive vs. Extractive Capital in Practice
paragraphs: The distinction between productive and extractive capital becomes practical in the investment process, where a non-extractive investor must ask different questions from a conventional one — not fewer, and not softer, but a second column beside the first. How large is the market, and what real need is being served? Can this scale, and what happens if it does? What is the revenue model, and does it depend on dependency, lock-in, addiction, opacity, scarcity, or enclosure? What is the growth rate, and is growth strengthening the enterprise or weakening it? What is the exit path, and how could capital be repaid without one? What is the valuation, and what level of claim is proportionate to risk and contribution? How defensible is the moat, and is the moat a legitimate protection of mission or an extractive chokepoint? What is the founder’s ambition, and does the founder understand stewardship? What are the investor rights, and do those rights preserve mission or threaten it?

Questions like these change portfolio construction. Non-Extractive Capital may favor companies with real revenue potential, durable customer relationships, moderate growth, useful products, open-source or infrastructure value, founders committed to mission, and capacity for repayment from surplus. It may avoid companies whose economics depend on monopoly capture, user manipulation, labor arbitrage, speculative exit, regulatory evasion, or enclosure of commons.

None of this means avoiding ambition. A non-extractive company can be ambitious — it can grow, innovate, hire, compete, and build at scale. But its ambition is ordered. It does not seek scale for its own sake, does not treat domination as success, does not assume every market should be captured. It asks how growth can serve mission.

The practical challenge is that non-extractive investing must be financially literate and morally literate at the same time. Only morally literate, and it funds beautiful but unsustainable projects. Only financially literate, and it reproduces extraction with better language. The discipline lies in holding both together.

sections.title: Governance as Moral Architecture
paragraphs: Governance determines whether non-extractive intention can survive pressure. Many companies begin with good intentions; so do many funds. But pressure reveals architecture. When revenue slows, when investors become impatient, when founders disagree, when acquisition offers arrive, when competitors behave aggressively, when workers demand more, when reserves run low, when mission becomes costly — governance decides what happens. Governance is where moral claims become operational authority.

So the questions must be settled in advance, and they are blunt. Who has the right to say no to an acquisition, and who decides whether surplus is distributed or reinvested? Who protects mission when founders change, and who determines whether investor repayment would harm resilience? Who can amend the mission? Who speaks for the commons, and who represents workers, users, or affected communities? Who audits extraction risk — and who can intervene if the stewards themselves fail? None of this can be left vague.

A non-extractive company needs governance that protects mission without creating paralysis: stewardship without unaccountable steward power, investor protection without investor sovereignty, flexibility without mission drift, accountability without domination. This is difficult, and the difficulties are specific. Mission locks can become rigid. Steward bodies can become self-perpetuating. Investor rights can be too weak to attract capital or too strong to protect mission. Worker voice can be meaningful or symbolic, commons commitments clear or vague, purpose clauses enforceable or decorative. Governance can grow so complex that no one understands it.

Non-extractive governance must therefore be designed with clarity and restraint. It should define the mission in operational terms and specify which kinds of decisions require mission review. It should define investor repayment rights transparently and set limits on sale, asset transfer, and changes of control. It should establish who holds stewardship authority, how stewards are appointed, removed, and held accountable, how conflicts are resolved, how mission impact and extraction risk are reported, and how the company can adapt without abandoning its purpose.
The goal is not maximum complexity. The goal is credible protection. Good governance should make the right thing easier and the extractive thing harder.

sections.title: Moral Accounting and the Repayment Formula
paragraphs: A non-extractive fund needs accounting that can distinguish healthy repayment from disguised extraction — and it needs that accounting to be usable by real companies. Ordinary accounting is already demanding; startups and small firms often struggle just to maintain reliable bookkeeping, cash-flow forecasts, tax compliance, payroll, invoicing, and financial reporting. A moral accounting overlay must therefore be practical. It cannot become a burdensome pseudo-monastic ledger of every ethical nuance; if it is too complex, it will fail. The goal is not to measure everything. The goal is to make visible the few moral distinctions that matter most to the financing relationship.

For Non-Extractive Capital, the central accounting question is this: can the company repay capital from durable surplus without weakening its mission, resilience, workers, users, commons, or future capacity?

The question translates into a disciplined repayment formula, and the order of operations is the whole point. A company should first meet its operating needs; then fair wages and essential obligations; then maintenance and security; then taxes; then necessary reinvestment; then resilience reserves; then mission-critical commitments. Only then should investor repayment come, from the surplus that genuinely remains. This is not a rigid universal formula. A software company, a services firm, a hardware company, a cooperative, and an infrastructure provider have different cost structures and different resilience needs, and their thresholds will differ. The principle underneath does not vary: capital should be repaid from surplus, not from depletion.

The moral ledger supporting the formula might track revenue quality, free cash flow, resilience reserves, debt burden, worker compensation and stability, maintenance and technical debt, customer dependency risk, mission commitments, open-source or commons obligations, founder and steward control, investor repayment progress, the cap remaining, and any signs of distress-based repayment. It should be simple enough to use and serious enough to govern.

The key is to prevent false surplus. False surplus appears when a company seems able to repay investors only because it is underpaying workers, ignoring maintenance, accumulating technical debt, delaying taxes, exploiting customers, externalizing costs, or depleting its founder’s health. Such repayment is extractive even when the spreadsheet looks perfectly healthy. A non-extractive fund must refuse repayment that comes from hidden harm.

This is the Monti problem in modern dress. The fund must be preserved, but not by consuming the borrower. The company must repay, but not by betraying the mission. Accounting must reveal the moment when preservation of capital begins to compete with service to life. Moral accounting is the practice of keeping capital honest.

sections.title: The Investor’s Conversion
paragraphs: Non-Extractive Capital asks investors to undergo a conversion of imagination. The trained question is: how much upside can I capture? The better question is: what return is fair for the risk and service I provide, and what should remain with the enterprise, its workers, its community, and its mission?

This is not an easy shift. Investors are trained to seek return. They compare opportunities; they think in risk-adjusted performance, liquidity, portfolio construction, benchmarks, and opportunity cost. Many carry fiduciary duties. Many manage other people’s money. They may worry, reasonably, that accepting capped returns means accepting inferior discipline or settling for symbolic impact. These concerns cannot be dismissed.

A non-extractive fund owes investors honesty in exchange. It should not pretend to be conventional venture capital, or promise unicorn-like upside while rejecting the very structures that produce such upside, or blur philanthropic capital, concessionary capital, and risk-adjusted investment into one warm haze. It should be clear about expected returns, risks, time horizons, liquidity, the possibility of default, and mission constraints.

But it can also ask investors to see differently. A capped return is not necessarily a failed return; it may be a morally disciplined one. Illiquidity is not necessarily weakness; it may be patience. Lack of exit is not necessarily lack of success; it may be continuity. A company that remains independent and cash-flowing may be a better steward of capital than one sold into extraction, and a portfolio that preserves mission and repays steadily may be more aligned with the real economy than one dependent on speculative exits.

The conversion, at bottom, is from ownership as domination to capital as participation. Capital participates in the enterprise for a time. It takes risk. It supports growth. It receives return. Then it releases. It does not remain forever as master, and it does not insist that the company’s entire future be organized around the investor’s liquidity.
This is deeply countercultural. It asks investors to accept enough.

sections.title: Founder Freedom and Founder Responsibility
