paragraphs: Non-Extractive Capital changes the founder’s role as well. Many founders fear losing control to investors, and not without reason. Non-extractive structures can protect them from exit pressure, speculative governance, and mission drift. Steward Ownership can let founders build companies that remain faithful to purpose. Repayment-capped finance can provide capital without requiring sale.

But the freedom comes with responsibility attached. A founder protected from extractive investors must not become an unaccountable ruler. Steward Ownership is not founder absolutism. A mission lock is not a shield for incompetence. Patient capital is not permission to avoid discipline, and capped investor return is not license to disregard repayment.

The founder must become a steward, and the word carries weight. It means accepting accountability to mission, workers, customers, investors, commons, and future generations; building a company capable of surviving beyond the founder’s personality; financial seriousness; transparent reporting; a willingness to be challenged. It means protecting the company from investor extraction and founder ego alike.

Founder control can be life-serving when it protects mission from capital markets. It can be dangerous when it becomes personal empire. The solution is not to hand control to capital by default. The solution is governance that holds founders accountable to purpose.

A steward-founder learns to ask a particular set of questions. What has been entrusted to me? Who depends on this company? What must not be sacrificed? What return is fair to capital? What surplus belongs to reinvestment, to workers, to community, to commons? How can this mission outlive me? And, hardest of all: how do I prevent my own ambition from becoming extraction?
Non-extractive finance requires moral formation on both sides of the table. Investors must accept limits. Founders must accept accountability.

sections.title: Workers, Users, and Communities
paragraphs: If Non-Extractive Capital changed only the relationship between investors and founders, it would be incomplete — and it is worth spelling out why. Capital can be non-extractive toward founders while the company remains extractive toward workers, users, suppliers, communities, or commons. A founder can retain mission control and still underpay workers. A steward-owned firm can still exploit its users. A repayment cap can still be serviced through customer lock-in. A company can avoid exit entirely while creating dependency.

Non-extraction must therefore reach beyond financing, to how the enterprise treats all those from whom value is drawn. Workers should not be treated merely as costs to make repayment possible. Users should not be locked into dependency so that revenue becomes predictable. Customers should not be manipulated, suppliers should not be squeezed to preserve margins, communities should not bear externalized costs. Open-source ecosystems should not be mined without contribution, and public infrastructure should not be privatized through stealth.

The repayment formula must accordingly be linked to broader company health. A company that repays investors while increasing worker precarity is not truly non-extractive. Neither is one that repays by enclosing a commons, or through addictive design, or by externalizing maintenance, security, or ecological costs. This does not mean every company must solve every social problem. It means no company may build its surplus through hidden depletion.

The practical question is how to govern this without creating impossible reporting burdens, and the answer is to identify each company’s material extraction risks — the places where it, specifically, will be tempted. For a software company these may include user lock-in, data extraction, open-source dependency, technical debt, security, and worker burnout. For a manufacturing firm: labor conditions, supply-chain pressure, ecological impact, and maintenance. For a housing enterprise: rent burden, displacement, maintenance, and tenant rights. Non-extractive finance must be contextual. It should ask where this company is most tempted to extract, and how we will know if it does.

sections.title: Open Source and Non-Extractive Finance
paragraphs: Open source is one of the most important test cases for Non-Extractive Capital. Open-source software creates enormous shared value; it supports public infrastructure, private companies, governments, universities, civil society, and daily digital life. Much of this value is generated through distributed collaboration, community norms, and thoroughly mixed motives. Some maintainers are paid. Many are not adequately supported. And corporations often depend heavily on open-source infrastructure while contributing less than they extract.

This creates a financing problem. Open-source projects need sustainability: maintainers need livelihoods, security needs funding, and documentation, governance, testing, and community management all require time someone must pay for. But conventional venture capital often pushes open-source companies toward enclosure — proprietary features, hosted services, cloud lock-in, dual licensing, data capture, enterprise dependency, or eventual acquisition.

The result can be tragic, and the arc is by now familiar. A project begins as a commons. It attracts users. A company forms around it. Capital enters, growth expectations rise, and the company needs defensibility. The commons becomes a funnel into proprietary services; the community becomes lead generation. Eventually the company may close features, change licenses, sell, or simply find itself governed by investor logic.

Non-Extractive Capital offers another path. It can fund open-source companies in ways that require neither enclosure nor exit. Repayment can come from durable surplus generated by services, support, hosting, training, integration, procurement, or enterprise contracts. Returns can be capped. Governance can protect the open-source mission, Steward Ownership can prevent sale to a proprietary acquirer, and commons obligations can be written into mission reporting.

This does not solve every open-source business problem. Some projects may never have viable revenue models. Some infrastructure should simply be publicly funded. Some commons need foundation or cooperative governance rather than a company structure at all, and some companies will still need to protect certain commercial offerings. But the principle matters: shared digital infrastructure should not have to choose between starvation and enclosure. Non-extractive finance can help create a third path — sustainable, commons-aligned enterprise.

sections.title: The Risk of Moral Branding
paragraphs: Every moral innovation can become branding, and Non-Extractive Capital is not exempt. “Non-extractive” is a powerful phrase. It can attract founders, investors, religious audiences, activists, policymakers, and institutions seeking alternatives to conventional finance — and precisely because the phrase is attractive, it can be diluted.

The dilutions are easy to picture. A fund calls itself non-extractive while demanding high returns. A company calls itself steward-owned while leaving its mission weakly protected. An investor accepts a repayment cap set so high that it functions like uncapped extraction. A founder uses mission language to avoid accountability. A platform calls itself commons-oriented while capturing user dependency. An impact fund rebrands ordinary revenue-based finance without confronting governance at all.

This is why definitions, structures, and audits matter. Non-extractive cannot mean “good vibes.” It must mean something testable. Does capital have a capped claim? Is control separated from speculative ownership? Is the mission legally protected? Is repayment tied to durable surplus? Are workers, users, communities, and commons protected from hidden extraction? Is there transparency about fees, return expectations, and governance rights? Can the company be sold against its mission? Can investors force liquidity? What happens after the cap is repaid? Who holds the stewards accountable?

The word must be disciplined by form — not because form guarantees virtue, for it does not, but because without form, moral language will be absorbed by the very system it critiques. Extraction is skilled at wearing the clothes of reform.

sections.title: Failure Modes
paragraphs: Non-Extractive Capital has failure modes of its own, and they should be named honestly.

The first is undercapitalization. If return expectations are too low, liquidity too limited, or investor education too weak, the fund may not raise enough capital to matter; beautiful structures cannot serve companies while they remain theoretical. The second is naive underwriting. A fund that overestimates its companies’ repayment capacity will deplete itself, pressure the companies later, or fail its investors — non-extractive finance still requires rigorous financial analysis. The third is hidden extraction: portfolio companies may generate surplus through practices the fund fails to see, such as worker burnout, customer lock-in, undermaintenance, or commons free-riding.

The fourth is governance rigidity. Mission locks may prevent necessary adaptation, trap companies in obsolete models, or empower guardians who lack competence. The fifth is founder capture: protecting companies from investors may inadvertently protect founders from accountability. The sixth is investor drift, in which future investors, fund managers, or board members slowly reinterpret terms toward higher returns, faster repayment, or more control.

The seventh is moral complacency. Once a structure is labeled non-extractive, participants may assume the moral work is done. The eighth is excessive complexity: a structure too hard for founders, investors, accountants, or lawyers to understand will remain marginal or be implemented badly. The ninth is market isolation — if non-extractive companies cannot access procurement, customers, talent, and ecosystems, capital structure alone will not save them. And the tenth is symbolic scale: the model may remain a niche for a few idealistic companies unless it connects to broader legal, cultural, and policy change.

These failure modes do not invalidate the project. They clarify the work. A serious non-extractive fund must be financially competent, legally careful, morally disciplined, operationally simple, and institutionally humble. It must expect drift, build correction mechanisms in advance, and learn.

sections.title: Non-Extractive Capital as Moral Economy in Practice
paragraphs: Non-Extractive Capital is not the whole moral economy. It will not solve housing financialization by itself. It will not reform all corporations or replace public policy. It will not eliminate greed or make every company virtuous, and it will not abolish the need for regulation, labor organizing, taxation, antitrust, public investment, commons governance, or spiritual conversion.

But it is one concrete answer to a central problem: how can mission-driven enterprises access capital without becoming instruments of extraction? In this sense it is moral economy in practice. It translates ancient and theological questions into contemporary financial design, and the translations run item by item. Aristotle’s “enough” becomes repayment caps. Scholastic proportionality becomes return linked to contribution and risk. Franciscan productive capital becomes patient investment in real enterprise. The Monti’s concern for fund preservation becomes evergreen sustainability without mission drift. Protestant stewardship becomes Steward Ownership and the mission lock. Smith’s suspicion of rent-seeking becomes anti-extraction due diligence. Catholic Social Teaching becomes governance ordered toward dignity, subsidiarity, solidarity, and the common good. Sphere sovereignty becomes plural institutional forms. Ordoliberalism becomes market rules that prevent domination. The cooperative and mutualist traditions become stakeholder participation and shared surplus.

Non-Extractive Capital does not merely speak these ideas. It tries to build them. That is why it matters. A moral economy cannot remain a library of beautiful principles. It must become term sheets, statutes, bylaws, repayment formulas, reporting dashboards, governance bodies, procurement relationships, investor agreements, and operating practices. The question “what is an economy for?” eventually becomes a plainer one: what is in the contract?

sections.title: Capital That Serves Life
paragraphs: Capital that serves life must be converted from master to servant, and the conversion has a long bill of requirements. It requires limits, purpose, governance, patience, and moral accounting; investors willing to accept fair return rather than maximum extraction; founders willing to accept stewardship rather than personal sovereignty; companies willing to generate surplus without hidden depletion; legal forms capable of protecting mission; and markets that reward service rather than capture. Non-Extractive Capital is one attempt to make the conversion real.

Its goal is not to end capital but to redeem capital’s role. Capital is stored capacity. It can help human beings build across time, fund what does not yet exist, preserve what would otherwise be lost, and support enterprise, infrastructure, technology, and common life. It can serve — but only if it is ordered.

Unordered capital seeks more; stewarded capital asks what it is for. Unordered capital demands control; stewarded capital accepts limits. Unordered capital seeks exit; stewarded capital supports continuity. Where unordered capital extracts from dependency, stewarded capital strengthens agency. And where unordered capital treats mission as subordinate, stewarded capital receives mission as the reason the enterprise exists.
That is the difference between capital that feeds on life and capital that serves life.

sections.title: The Transition to the Digital Commons
paragraphs: The next chapter tests this framework in the defining terrain of our time: digital infrastructure, open source, AI, and the commons. The digital world intensifies every question this book has raised. What is value, when knowledge is shared? What is ownership, when code is collaborative? What is extraction, when user data, public knowledge, and community labor become proprietary systems? What is sovereignty, when public institutions depend on private platforms? What is stewardship, when infrastructure is invisible, global, and fragile? What does it mean for capital to serve life in the age of machines?

Non-Extractive Capital can help answer these questions. It cannot answer them alone. The digital commons requires legal forms, funding models, public procurement, open standards, community governance, institutional stewardship — perhaps new forms of ownership we have not yet fully developed. It requires a moral economy capable of seeing shared digital infrastructure not as raw material for enclosure but as a common good.

If this chapter has shown how capital might be redesigned, the next asks what must be protected. Because in the digital age, the old question returns with new urgency: will the infrastructure of civilization be stewarded as a commons, or enclosed as capital’s next frontier?

chapters.title: Chapter 17: The Digital Commons and the Future of Civilization

paragraphs: Every age believes its machines have changed everything, and ours believes it with unusual conviction. The digital world comes wrapped in novelty — new technologies, platforms, and markets; new forms of intelligence, labor, and infrastructure; new powers of computation, prediction, automation, and coordination — and much of the billing is deserved. The digital age really has transformed how human beings communicate, work, learn, organize, govern, remember, trade, pray, create, and imagine. Software mediates daily life. Data shapes decisions. Platforms govern visibility, cloud infrastructure hosts institutions, algorithms sort attention, and artificial intelligence has walked into domains once reserved for human judgment, craft, writing, analysis, and care.
But beneath the novelty sits the question this book has been asking from the beginning. Will the infrastructure of human life be stewarded as a common good, or enclosed as a source of extraction?

Every tradition we have visited feared a version of the same detachment. Aristotle feared money detached from use, and Christianity feared wealth detached from charity. The Scholastics feared gain detached from justice; the Franciscans feared finance detached from need. Salamanca watched markets coordinate value without ever fully escaping empire. Protestant stewardship warned that entrusted goods must not become instruments of domination, and Smith warned that wealth and commercial power corrupt moral judgment. Modern economics gained analytical power and lost the confidence to ask about ends. Financialized capitalism showed how capital can turn everything into future claim.

The digital age gives this old drama a new stage. Code can serve life or feed on it. Data can serve understanding or become surveillance. Platforms can coordinate or dominate; AI can augment human agency or concentrate power; open source can become shared infrastructure or the raw material for enclosure; digital sovereignty can protect public freedom or dwindle into one more market slogan.
The central question has not changed: what is this economy for?

sections.title: What Makes Infrastructure a Commons
paragraphs: Infrastructure is what other things depend on. Roads, ports, bridges, schools, hospitals, water systems, electrical grids, legal systems, languages, scientific knowledge, public health systems, monetary systems — these make other forms of life possible. They are not merely products; they are conditions of participation. Infrastructure that works well has a way of becoming invisible: people build on it, trust it, rely on it, and organize their lives around it.

Digital infrastructure now plays this role. Operating systems, programming languages, encryption libraries, cloud services, identity systems, payment rails, communication protocols, open-source packages, search engines, app stores, mapping systems, data standards, AI models, and platform APIs shape what can be built, who can participate, and who controls access. Ownership is a patchwork: some private, some public, some open source; some maintained by communities, some governed by standards bodies, some controlled by corporations, some barely governed at all.

The moral question is not simply who owns it. The question is what kind of good it has become. A good becomes commons-like when many depend on it, many contribute to it, and its value exceeds any single owner’s contribution. And a commons is not merely an unowned resource. It is a shared field of use, requiring governance, maintenance, access rules, contribution norms, and protection against depletion and capture.

Nowhere is this clearer than in open-source software. A small library maintained by volunteers can become a dependency for thousands of companies. A programming language becomes the foundation for entire industries; a protocol enables communication across borders; a database quietly holds together public services. A security tool may protect public institutions, hospitals, activists, journalists, and businesses all at once — as the world learned in April 2014, when the Heartbleed flaw was found in OpenSSL, the encryption library guarding much of the world’s web traffic, maintained by a handful of developers, one of them full-time, on about two thousand dollars a year in donations. The value is distributed; the dependency is broad; the burden of maintenance may fall on almost no one.
Infrastructure like this cannot be understood well as private property, volunteer hobby, or market input. It is a commons. And commons require stewardship.

sections.title: Open Source as Stewardship in Practice
paragraphs: Open source is one of the clearest contemporary examples of capital that serves life — when it is well governed and sustained — and the idea has founding dates. In September 1983 Richard Stallman of MIT announced GNU, an operating system anyone would be free to run, study, change, and share — the four freedoms the free-software movement would later codify. In August 1991 a twenty-one-year-old student in Helsinki named Linus Torvalds posted a note about a system of his own — “just a hobby,” he promised, “won’t be big and professional like gnu.” The hobby became Linux, which now runs most of the internet’s servers and, via Android, most of the world’s smartphones. As forecasts go, it was not his best work.

At its best, open source embodies several virtues of moral economy. It lets knowledge circulate. It enables people to inspect, modify, improve, and share tools. It lowers barriers to entry, prevents unnecessary duplication, and supports education, innovation, security, interoperability, and public trust. It can reduce dependency on proprietary vendors, and it allows communities to build together across institutional boundaries.

Open source can be a form of digital hospitality. It says: here is a tool, a library, a protocol, a method, a piece of infrastructure — use it, learn from it, improve it, adapt it, build with it. The gift does not disappear when shared; often it becomes more valuable.

This is not romanticism. Open-source communities can be difficult, exclusionary, underfunded, badly governed, or dominated by powerful actors. Maintainers burn out. Security vulnerabilities persist. Corporate free-riding is common, community conflict can be severe, and some projects answer to no one — including the users who depend on them. Openness alone does not guarantee justice.

But open source still reveals something important: not all value creation requires enclosure. Modern capitalism assumes value must be captured to be sustained; open source is a standing counterexample. Shared creation can generate enormous value. Collaboration, reputation, curiosity, public spirit, technical excellence, need, and plain vocation can all motivate productive work. A commons can be more generative than a closed system.

It also reveals the weakness of gift without institution. A gift can be received without gratitude, and a commons used without contribution. A maintainer can be praised and unpaid. A community can create value while corporations capture it, and a public dependency can rest, invisibly, on private exhaustion.

This is where stewardship must become structural. Open source needs funding models, governance bodies, maintenance institutions, procurement policies, contribution norms, security support, legal protection, and business models that do not simply enclose the commons they depend on. Without these, open source becomes an invisible subsidy to extractive capital.

The moral problem is not that companies use open source — use is the point of the gift. The problem is when use becomes extraction: when corporations build profitable services on shared infrastructure while contributing little, capture community value into proprietary platforms, turn open ecosystems into funnels for closed products, or depend on maintainers without helping to sustain them. Open source is stewardship in practice only when the gift is protected from becoming raw material for extraction.

sections.title: Digital Infrastructure and Dependency
paragraphs: Digital dependency is the sort of thing nobody notices until it breaks. A public agency discovers it cannot migrate from a proprietary vendor. A hospital depends on software it is not allowed to inspect. A small business loses access to its customers overnight when a platform changes its rules; a developer’s product collapses when an API is withdrawn. A school becomes dependent on cloud tools whose governance sits on another continent, a city cannot operate critical services without foreign-owned systems, a government’s data lives inside infrastructure it does not control. A company built on open-source software discovers the maintainer burned out long ago.

Occasionally the lesson arrives with comic clarity. In March 2016 a developer named Azer Koçulu, after a dispute with the registry hosting his work, deleted his packages — among them left-pad, eleven lines of code that padded the left side of a string — and build systems seized up across the software industry within minutes. Eleven lines, it turned out, were load-bearing.

Digital infrastructure creates relationships of dependence, and dependence creates moral obligations. This does not make every dependency bad. Human life is interdependent; technical systems require specialization; no institution can build everything itself. Outsourcing can be reasonable, cloud services efficient, proprietary products excellent. Platforms can solve real coordination problems.

The question is whether dependency remains accountable — whether users can leave; whether data can move and systems interoperate; whether public institutions can audit what they rely on; whether communities can influence governance, maintainers can be supported, and security can be verified; whether access can be withdrawn arbitrarily, or prices raised once the lock-in is complete; whether a private firm can quietly change the terms of public life.

Dependency becomes extractive when exit is impossible, governance is distant, and access is monetized through lock-in. This is the digital form of rent. The platform, cloud provider, app store, payment processor, identity provider, or AI infrastructure company becomes the gate through which others must pass. It may have created real value; it may still provide real service. But once the dependency is established, the temptation is to convert access into recurring claim.

And this is not simply a competition problem — it is a sovereignty problem. Who governs the infrastructure on which public and economic life depends? If the answer is “whoever owns the platform,” then private capital has acquired public power.

sections.title: AI and the Concentration of Power
paragraphs: Artificial intelligence sharpens every question in this chapter, because it concentrates several forms of power at once. AI systems require data, compute, talent, capital, infrastructure, and distribution, and none of these is evenly distributed. The most powerful systems tend to be built by institutions with enormous financial resources, access to specialized hardware, large data pipelines, cloud capacity, and platform reach.

The result is a flywheel. More data and users make the systems better; better systems draw more users; more users bring more data, revenue, and bargaining power; more capital buys more compute; and more compute makes it ever harder for anyone else to compete. AI therefore risks becoming not merely a technology but an infrastructure of dependency.

The danger is not only that AI will automate tasks. The deeper danger is that it will reorganize access to knowledge, work, creativity, administration, education, and decision-making around a small number of privately governed systems.

This is a new chrematistics. Shared human knowledge becomes training material; public language becomes model input; creative work becomes dataset; user interaction becomes refinement signal. Compute becomes chokepoint, models become infrastructure, infrastructure becomes subscription, subscription becomes dependency — and dependency, as ever, becomes power.

None of this makes AI inherently extractive. It can assist research, medicine, education, accessibility, translation, administration, security, creativity, and scientific discovery; it can let small teams do work that once required large institutions, reduce drudgery, reveal patterns humans miss, and genuinely augment human agency.

But whether AI serves life depends on how it is governed, owned, financed, deployed, and made accountable. A system trained on shared knowledge but governed as a private empire raises serious moral questions. One that displaces workers while concentrating the gains in capital raises questions of justice; one used by public institutions without transparency, of democratic accountability; one that mediates education, health, law, or welfare, of dignity and responsibility; one that deepens dependency on a few vendors, of sovereignty.
Nobody doubts that AI is powerful. The question worth asking is what the power is for.

sections.title: AI as a New Chrematistics
paragraphs: Aristotle’s name for the art of accumulation without limit was chrematistics, and AI becomes its newest instrument when intelligence itself is reorganized around extraction — not intelligence in the older human sense of wisdom, virtue, judgment, and contemplation, but computational capability pointed at prediction, optimization, capture, and monetization. A system that can optimize anything will optimize whatever it is aimed at: advertising, pricing, surveillance, labor discipline, addiction, persuasion, financial trading, regulatory evasion — extraction, in whatever form its owners request.

The moral danger is not that machines think. The danger is that machines intensify the aims of the systems that deploy them. A life-serving institution may use AI to reduce burdens, improve care, increase accessibility, support learning, or protect infrastructure. An extractive institution may use it to manipulate behavior, eliminate workers, capture attention, deepen dependency, or expand control. The tool amplifies the telos.

So the question of purpose is unavoidable. An institution deploying AI should be able to say what the system is for; who owns it, who governs it, and who is accountable for its harms; who benefits from its productivity gains; whose labor trained it, whose knowledge it absorbed, and whose dependency it creates; which human capacities it strengthens or weakens, which forms of judgment it replaces, and which forms of life it makes easier or harder to live.

Without moral economy, AI will be governed by the default answers of financialized capitalism — scale, growth, engagement, efficiency, market share, defensibility, and return on capital — and those answers are not enough. AI needs a richer moral architecture, because it touches not only production but cognition, attention, trust, speech, authority, and imagination. If capital that feeds on life gains control over artificial intelligence, extraction becomes faster, smarter, and harder to see.

sections.title: Digital Sovereignty
paragraphs: Digital sovereignty is usually discussed as geopolitics. Can Europe control its own digital infrastructure? Can states avoid dependency on foreign cloud providers? Can public institutions protect data, security, and democratic authority? Can societies govern technology by their own laws and values, rather than those of dominant corporations or rival states?

Important questions all. But digital sovereignty is not only geopolitical; it is also moral-economic. A society is not digitally sovereign if it merely swaps foreign dependency for domestic extraction. It is not sovereign if its public institutions depend on private platforms whose governance they cannot influence, or if its citizens’ data becomes raw material for corporate profit. It is not sovereign if open-source maintainers supply essential infrastructure without support, if AI systems shape public life without accountability, or if procurement locks governments into vendors they cannot leave.

Sovereignty requires stewardship of shared infrastructure — which means, unglamorously, capacity: technical competence in public institutions; procurement models that support open standards and interoperability; funding for open-source infrastructure; governance arrangements that prevent capture; data rules that protect persons and communities; alternatives to platform dependency; and forms of partnership with companies that preserve public authority rather than surrender it.

Digital sovereignty also requires pluralism: not everything should be state-run, corporate, or left to volunteers. A healthy digital ecosystem needs public institutions, mission-driven companies, cooperatives, open-source foundations, universities, civil society, standards bodies, steward-owned firms, and private enterprise. Each has a role, and no single sphere should dominate the others.

Here the principles of the previous chapters file back in, each with its question. Subsidiarity asks which decisions should be made closest to the technical and social knowledge. Solidarity asks who bears the risks of digital dependency and exclusion. The common good asks whether the infrastructure serves public flourishing. Sphere sovereignty asks whether technology, state, market, science, civil society, and community each retain their proper integrity. Ordoliberalism asks what legal order prevents the concentration of power; the cooperative and mutualist traditions ask how users and workers can participate in governance; and Non-Extractive Capital asks how companies serving digital infrastructure can be financed without being forced toward enclosure or exit.
Digital sovereignty, in other words, is not only a strategy. It is a moral economy of infrastructure.

sections.title: The Governance of Shared Resources
paragraphs: Commons do not govern themselves. The point bears dwelling on, because romantic language about the commons obscures the hard work of stewardship. Shared resources require rules, roles, maintenance, boundaries, conflict resolution, contribution systems, accountability, and renewal. Without governance, a commons can be depleted, captured, neglected, or quietly dominated by informal elites.

Nobody established this more patiently than Elinor Ostrom. The ecologist Garrett Hardin’s famous 1968 essay “The Tragedy of the Commons” had argued that shared resources were doomed — every herdsman, reasoning selfishly, overgrazes the common pasture to ruin. Ostrom did what economists of the era rarely did: she went and looked. In Swiss alpine pastures, Spanish irrigation communities, Japanese village forests, and coastal fisheries she found commons governed successfully for centuries, and in “Governing the Commons” (1990) she distilled the survivors’ design principles: clear boundaries, rules fitted to local conditions, a say in the rules for those they bind, accountable monitoring, graduated sanctions, cheap conflict resolution, and a recognized right to organize at all. The work won her the 2009 Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences, the first to a woman. Her finding cut both ways: commons are neither doomed, as Hardin feared, nor self-sustaining, as romantics hope. They are governed, or they die.

Digital commons are no different. An open-source project needs maintainers, and maintainers need authority — to accept, reject, review, and release code. Communities need norms; security vulnerabilities need response; funding needs allocating; conflicts need resolving. Corporate contributors need boundaries, users need clarity, dependencies need maintenance, and the whole arrangement must balance openness with responsibility.

Commons have power; the question is whether the power is accountable. A commons can fail by being too open to capture, and it can fail by becoming closed, hostile, or unresponsive. It can be dominated by whoever has the most time, money, technical expertise, or institutional backing. It can exclude newcomers without meaning to; it can burn out its maintainers by expecting infinite unpaid labor; it can mistake the absence of formal structure for freedom.

Stewardship requires form. Digital commons may need foundations, cooperatives, trusts, public funding, membership governance, contributor agreements, licenses, procurement commitments, security funds, maintainer salaries, documentation budgets, and procedures for conflict. These are not bureaucratic distractions. They are the infrastructure of the infrastructure.

The moral economy question is what forms of governance preserve shared use without enabling extraction, and the answer will differ from good to good. Some digital commons should be governed by nonprofit foundations; some by cooperatives of users or maintainers; some by public-interest institutions, steward-owned companies, or open standards bodies; some by hybrid arrangements pairing public procurement with private service provision; some by mission-locked firms financed through capped returns. There is no single model. But the principles are constant: preserve access, fund maintenance, prevent enclosure, make power accountable, protect contributors, support users, and ensure that those who profit from the commons contribute to its ongoing life.
The digital commons does not need sentimentality. It needs governance.

sections.title: Non-Extractive Business Models for Digital Infrastructure
paragraphs: One of the hardest questions is how a digital commons is to be paid for. Pure volunteerism is fragile. Philanthropy is inconsistent. Government funding can be slow or politically constrained. Corporate sponsorship creates its own dependency; conventional venture capital pushes toward enclosure; subscription models can wall off access. Consulting distracts from maintenance, open-core models can split community from enterprise, and cloud providers have a documented talent for capturing the value of other people’s maintenance.

There is no perfect model. But some models are more aligned with stewardship than others. A steward-owned open-source company can provide services, support, hosting, security, training, integration, or enterprise features while protecting the core commons from sale or enclosure. Repayment-capped finance can fund growth without requiring acquisition by a proprietary platform. Public procurement can create reliable demand for open infrastructure, and maintenance subscriptions can let users contribute in proportion to their reliance. Foundations can hold trademarks, governance rights, or core assets; cooperatives can give users or maintainers a seat in governance; dual licensing, structured carefully, can avoid turning community work into investor property; and public-interest cloud providers can offer hosted services without monopolistic lock-in.

The key is to align revenue with service rather than dependency. A life-serving digital business model asks: how can we be paid to maintain, improve, support, secure, and responsibly steward this infrastructure? An extractive digital business model asks: how can we control access, capture data, create lock-in, and monetize dependency?

The difference is not always obvious from the outside. Hosted services can serve users or entrap them. Enterprise support can fund the commons or redirect attention to paying customers only. Premium features can sustain development or hollow out the open core; data can improve a service or enable surveillance; AI can assist maintainers or appropriate their work.

This is why governance and accounting matter. A digital infrastructure company that claims to be non-extractive should be able to answer, in public and without fidgeting: What remains open, and what may never be enclosed? How are maintainers paid, and how do corporate users contribute? Can customers leave with their data and workflows? Are open standards supported? Are investors capped? Can the company be sold, and who protects the mission? How is community voice represented, and what dependency risks are being created?
Without answers, “open” becomes a marketing word.

sections.title: AI, Labor, and the Distribution of Surplus
paragraphs: AI raises a profound question about surplus: if these systems increase productivity, who receives the gains? Technology alone will not decide. Ownership, governance, labor law, bargaining power, public policy, procurement, intellectual property, data rights, and capital structure will.

If AI is governed by extractive capital, the gains will likely flow upward. Workers face displacement, surveillance, speed-up, deskilling, or wage pressure. Users grow dependent on proprietary systems, and creators watch their work absorbed into models without meaningful compensation. Public institutions pay recurring fees for systems built partly on public knowledge. The surplus generated by collective human culture is captured by a few owners of compute, models, and platforms.

Governed by stewardship, the same technology could distribute its gains very differently. AI could reduce drudgery while preserving worker dignity, and support professionals rather than replace their judgment. It could improve public services without privatizing public capacity, and hand small organizations tools once available only to large firms. It could support disabled users, translators, teachers, doctors, researchers, and maintainers. It could strengthen commons rather than enclose them — and its surplus could fund shorter work time, better care, public goods, and shared infrastructure.

What separates the two futures is not the model alone. It is the political economy around the model. Which returns us, once more, to the firm. Who owns the AI company, and what return does its capital demand? Can users leave? Are workers represented, data sources acknowledged, public institutions locked in? Are the models open, auditable, or governed? Can productivity gains be shared, or are harms externalized? Does the company’s governance even allow it to choose human flourishing over maximum extraction?

AI makes the question of capital structure urgent because the technology magnifies whatever logic governs it. Governed by exit-seeking capital, AI will seek scale, lock-in, defensibility, data capture, and market dominance — because that is what exit-seeking capital seeks. Governed by public-interest institutions, steward-owned firms, cooperatives, commons, and mission-locked structures, it might become part of a life-serving digital ecosystem. The future of AI is therefore not only technical. It is institutional.

sections.title: Human Judgment in the Age of Machines
paragraphs: Moral economy cannot delegate judgment to machines. AI systems can classify, predict, generate, summarize, optimize, recommend, and automate. They can assist judgment, reveal patterns, and improve decision-making when they are used well. What they cannot do is answer the foundational question of this book — what is an economy for? — because that is not a technical question. It is a moral, philosophical, theological, political, and stubbornly human one. It requires discernment about ends and wisdom about goods; attention to persons, communities, history, vulnerability, dignity, and the common good. It requires responsibility. AI can help calculate means. It cannot define ultimate ends.

This matters because institutions often adopt technology precisely in order to avoid responsibility. An algorithm recommends; a model scores; a system ranks; a dashboard displays; a risk tool flags. The decision becomes “data-driven,” and the human actor claims merely to have followed the system. This is moral outsourcing. A bank denies credit because the model says so. A platform suspends a user, a company fires workers, a government allocates services, a hospital prioritizes patients, a fund invests — because the system, the optimization, the algorithm, the model, the score said so. Notice who has quietly left the room in each of these sentences.

Tools can inform decisions. Responsibility cannot be transferred to the tool. A moral economy of AI must preserve human accountability — which does not mean every decision must be made by hand. It means that institutions using AI remain answerable for the ends, data, design, deployment, errors, exclusions, and consequences of their systems, and that the more powerful the system, the greater the obligation. AI should not become a new invisible hand, invoked to absolve human beings from judgment.

sections.title: Digital Asceticism and the Formation of Desire
paragraphs: The digital economy does not only reorganize infrastructure. It forms desire. Platforms compete for attention; recommendation systems shape curiosity; social media trains comparison; notifications fragment time. Search engines guide inquiry, advertising follows behavior, feeds cultivate appetite, and metrics teach people what matters: likes, shares, views, clicks, rankings, streaks, followers, conversions, engagement. Digital capitalism is a school of desire.

Every moral tradition in this book saw the problem coming, each in its own vocabulary. Aristotle worried about desire without limit. Christianity worried about avarice, vanity, and the deformation of the soul. Protestantism worried about discipline, vocation, and Sabbath; Smith worried about admiration and the corruption of the moral sentiments. The digital economy intensifies all of these concerns at once. It makes vanity measurable and comparison constant. It makes attention monetizable and rest difficult, outrage profitable and addiction scalable, identity performative and every unguarded moment available for capture.

A moral economy of digital life therefore needs ascetic practices — not rejection of technology, not nostalgia, not purity, but disciplined limits that protect attention, contemplation, friendship, worship, family, craft, and embodied life from total colonization. Old disciplines acquire new point. Sabbath becomes newly important, and so do silence, unmonetized time, local community, and tools built to human scale. Digital minimalism stops being merely a lifestyle and becomes a form of moral resistance.

But personal discipline is not enough, and it is a little rich to demand it alone. Platforms should not be allowed to build addiction machines and then advise their users to practice self-control. Moral responsibility belongs also to designers, companies, investors, regulators, and governance structures. If a business model depends on capturing attention against the user’s good, it is extractive. If engagement grows by degrading agency, the growth is disordered.
Capital that serves life must respect human attention. Attention is not raw material. It is part of the soul’s capacity to love, think, pray, learn, and perceive reality.

sections.title: The Return of the Commons
paragraphs: The commons returns in the digital age because knowledge behaves differently from scarce material goods. If I give you a loaf of bread, I no longer have it. If I teach you an idea, we may both have it. If I share code, you can use it without preventing me from using it, and if many people improve shared software, the whole ecosystem may benefit. Knowledge can be non-rivalrous, cumulative, and generative — which is how a volunteer-written encyclopedia, launched in January 2001 on the improbable premise that anyone on earth could edit it, became in Wikipedia the largest reference work ever assembled and one of the most visited places on the internet.

This does not mean knowledge costs nothing. Creating, maintaining, verifying, securing, documenting, and teaching knowledge all require labor. Commons do not abolish scarcity: maintainers have bodies, servers need energy, researchers need salaries, communities need governance. But the logic of knowledge goods differs from the logic of ordinary commodities.

Enclosing knowledge can sometimes support its creation — intellectual property, trade secrets, and proprietary products may incentivize investment. But excessive enclosure can slow learning, concentrate power, duplicate effort, and create dependency. When knowledge becomes infrastructure, enclosure can become domination.

The digital age therefore forces us to rethink property. Not abolish it — rethink it. What should be privately owned, and what publicly governed? What should be open, what shared under conditions, what held by foundations or trusts, what stewarded by communities? What should be protected from enclosure, what funded as public infrastructure, and what may be commercial but mission-locked? A moral economy cannot answer all these questions with one principle; different goods require different regimes. But it can reject the assumption that maximum private enclosure is the natural endpoint of value creation. A commons, rightly understood, is not the absence of ownership. It is ownership, governance, or stewardship ordered toward shared use.

This is why the digital commons matters for the future of civilization. If knowledge infrastructure is enclosed by a small number of firms, then education, research, governance, creativity, security, and public life all become dependent on private capital. If digital commons are sustained, pluralism and democratic agency remain more possible. The future of freedom may depend on whether we can govern knowledge as a shared inheritance.

sections.title: Capital, Commons, and the Common Good
paragraphs: Capital and commons need not be enemies. Properly ordered, capital can help sustain a commons: it can fund maintainers, security, documentation, hosting, governance, community management, usability, compliance, and long-term development. It can make open-source projects reliable enough for public institutions to build on. It can help mission-driven companies build services around shared infrastructure, and help digital commons become durable.

But capital must be limited. If capital funds a commons in order to enclose it, the commons becomes a pipeline into extraction. If capital requires exit, the commons may eventually be sold. If capital demands defensibility, openness starts to look like a weakness to be corrected. If capital seeks uncapped upside, shared value will sooner or later be converted into proprietary claim.

This is why Non-Extractive Capital is relevant to the digital commons. It offers a way for capital to participate without dominating. Investors can receive return for real risk; companies can build sustainable services; maintainers can be paid; infrastructure can improve. And mission locks, stewardship governance, repayment caps, and commons commitments can prevent capital from converting shared value into private sovereignty.

A digital commons economy will require many funding sources — public money, philanthropic money, cooperative contributions, procurement, membership models, service revenue, and non-extractive investment. The point is not to make everything investable. Some goods should be funded publicly or philanthropically; some should be maintained by communities; some can support companies. The point is to match the financial form to the moral nature of the good. A commons should not be financed in a way that requires enclosure. A public good should not be funded in a way that creates private dependency. A mission-driven company should not be capitalized in a way that forces exit, and a digital infrastructure provider should not be governed by return alone.
Capital that serves the commons must accept that not all the value it helps create belongs to it.

sections.title: What Moral Economy Offers the Age of Machines
paragraphs: What does a tradition that began with Aristotle’s worries about coinage have to offer an age of server farms? Several gifts, as it happens.
First, the question of purpose. Technology is not self-justifying; AI, platforms, data systems, and digital infrastructure must be judged by the forms of life they create.
Second, the distinction between use and accumulation. Digital tools can serve real human use, or they can become engines for the unlimited capture of attention, data, dependency, and claim.
Third, the language of stewardship. Digital infrastructure is entrusted power, to be governed for those who depend on it and not merely for those who own it.
Fourth, the critique of usury and rent. Recurring claims built on dependency, lock-in, and access are old financial sins in new clothes.
Fifth, the concept of the commons. Shared knowledge, code, protocols, and public digital infrastructure require governance beyond both private extraction and state bureaucracy.
Sixth, the category of moral formation. Technologies form desire, attention, judgment, and community; they are not neutral tools used by unchanged persons.

Seventh, the principle of enough. Not every system should maximize engagement. Not every dataset should be captured, nor every process automated, nor every public good monetized, nor every human capacity optimized for productivity.
Eighth, the demand for institutional design. Good intentions are not enough; governance, ownership, finance, law, and accounting must be structured toward life.

These gifts do not add up to a complete policy program. They are something prior: a moral grammar. And a grammar is what we need most, because the digital age is moving faster than our moral language. We reach for words like innovation, disruption, safety, efficiency, intelligence, alignment, sovereignty, openness, and progress. These words matter, but they are not enough. We need older and deeper words — justice, stewardship, commons, vocation, Sabbath, dignity, solidarity, limits, common good. Without such words, we will not know whether our machines serve us, or whether we have quietly reorganized ourselves to serve them.

sections.title: The Return of the Founding Question
paragraphs: We have reached the end of the book’s main argument, and it ends where it began. The opening chapter asked what an economy is for. The question now returns in digital dress: what is software for? What are data and AI for, and open source, and infrastructure? What are innovation and intelligence for? What, finally, is capital for?

If the answer is extraction, the digital future will intensify the disorders of financialized capitalism. Platforms will become sovereign, and AI will concentrate power. Commons will be enclosed; public institutions will become dependent; human attention will be harvested. Work will be reorganized around capital’s needs, and knowledge will become proprietary infrastructure. The world will become more connected and less free.

If the answer is stewardship, another future remains possible. Digital infrastructure can be governed as a common good. Open source can be funded and protected. AI can augment human agency rather than replace responsibility. Public institutions can build and procure in ways that preserve sovereignty; companies can be mission-locked and steward-owned; capital can receive a fair return without enclosing the future. Users, workers, maintainers, communities, and citizens can have a voice. Technology can serve life.

This will not happen automatically. The default trajectory of financialized capitalism is enclosure: shared value becomes private asset, dependency becomes recurring revenue, infrastructure becomes moat, intelligence becomes product, human life becomes data. Stewardship requires interruption. It requires saying no — to some forms of scale, some forms of capital, some forms of convenience, some forms of automation, some forms of enclosure. And it requires saying yes: to shared infrastructure, patient funding, public capacity, open standards, mission-locked enterprise, digital commons, and human dignity in the age of machines.
The future of civilization will not be determined by technology alone. It will be determined by the moral architecture through which technology is owned, financed, governed, and loved.

sections.title: Toward an Economy Ordered to Human Flourishing
paragraphs: The digital commons brings the whole argument of this book into focus. Capital that serves life builds and sustains the conditions of shared flourishing. It funds what is useful. It accepts limits. It strengthens agency, preserves commons, respects human attention, and supports work as vocation. It allows institutions to endure, and it knows that not everything valuable should be owned, monetized, or optimized.

Capital that feeds on life sees the digital world as an infinite frontier of capture: more data, more engagement, more dependency, more enclosure, more automation, more leverage, more subscription, more control, more claim.

The choice before us is not technology or no technology. It is not market or state, and it is not innovation or stagnation. The choice is what kind of moral economy will govern the tools that now govern us. If we recover the question of purpose, digital technology can be placed back within human ends. If we recover stewardship, ownership can become responsibility. If we recover the commons, shared infrastructure can be protected from enclosure. If we recover limits, capital can be prevented from devouring the very life it depends on.

The founding question is not behind us. It is ahead of us. Every new technology will ask it again. Every platform will ask it again; every AI system will ask it again; every investment, every procurement decision, every license, every data structure, every governance model, every act of enclosure or sharing will ask it again.
What is this for?
The answer will determine whether the digital future becomes another chapter in the history of extraction, or the beginning of an economy ordered again toward life.

chapters.title: Conclusion: Toward an Economy Ordered to Human Flourishing

paragraphs: We began with a question.
What is an economy for?

It is a question a child can ask, and one that whole civilizations have managed to bury — this book has spent its pages watching the burial and doing some of the digging. The question lies beneath every market, every firm, every loan, every investment, every technology, every ownership structure, every policy, every household budget, every act of production and exchange. And it gets answered whether or not anyone asks it: not only in books but in law and contracts, in capital flows and procurement rules, in accounting systems, board decisions, software licenses, debt covenants, land titles, labor practices, platform architectures, and the daily disciplines of work and desire.
An economy is never only a mechanism. It is a moral order.

It teaches people what to love. It rewards some forms of life and punishes others; it shapes households, firms, cities, landscapes, institutions, and souls; it decides what counts as success and what disappears as cost. It can honor work or degrade it, build trust or dissolve it, strengthen agency or monetize dependency, preserve common goods or enclose them. It can make capital a servant, or crown it as master.
This book has argued that we cannot understand our present crisis until we recover an older distinction: between wealth that serves life and wealth that feeds on it.

That distinction has worn many names on the road here. Aristotle called us back from chrematistics to oikonomia — from accumulation without limit to householding ordered toward life. Christianity placed wealth under the judgment of charity and mercy, avarice, sin, and salvation. The Scholastics asked whether an exchange preserved commutative justice. The Franciscans asked whether capital could be productive without becoming usurious, and whether a financial institution could serve the poor without depleting itself or drifting into extraction. Salamanca revealed the coordinating power of markets, and with it the moral ambiguity of trade in a world of empire. Protestant vocation dignified ordinary work and stewardship while exposing the danger of discipline without Sabbath. Adam Smith watched markets through moral psychology — sympathy, admiration, self-command, corruption, and the ever-present danger of rent-seeking. Modern economics gained analytical power and too often lost the confidence to ask what the economy is finally for. Financialized capitalism showed us what happens when capital becomes sovereign. And the digital age now asks whether knowledge, code, data, AI, and infrastructure will be stewarded as commons or enclosed as capital’s next frontier.
Across that whole story, one question keeps returning.
Does this serve life?

sections.title: Commerce After Extraction
paragraphs: To recover moral economy is not to reject commerce.

Commerce can be honorable — this book has been saying so since Bernardino stood up for the good merchant. Markets can coordinate human need and creativity. Prices can communicate knowledge. Entrepreneurs can discover possibilities no planner could foresee. Firms can gather people around useful work, finance can move resources across time, and profit can sustain an enterprise, reward service, compensate risk, and fund future goods.

Commerce as such is not the problem; the problem is commerce detached from its proper end. Commerce turns extractive when gain no longer corresponds to service, when contract hides domination, when market price masks necessity, when ownership loses responsibility, when finance claims more than it contributes, when platforms monetize dependency, when firms are governed as assets before they are understood as communities of production, when growth is achieved through depletion — and when the category of enough quietly disappears.

An economy after extraction would not abolish markets but re-embed them in moral life, asking a second question wherever we now ask only the first. Whether something can be sold, and whether it should be sold in that way. Whether a price can be charged, and whether the price reflects justice or power. Whether an investment can generate a return, and whether the return is proportionate to contribution. Whether a company can grow, and whether the growth strengthens its purpose or deforms it. Whether a technology can scale, and whether the scaling increases human agency or human dependency.

Call it commerce under discipline. It is not a sentimental program: it knows that scarcity is real, that incentives matter, that coordination is genuinely difficult; it knows that good intentions can fail and that institutions must be financially viable. But it declines to confuse viability with virtue, or profitability with legitimacy. Commerce after extraction asks of every enterprise something older than economics and harder than accounting — an account of its gain.

sections.title: Freedom Without Domination
paragraphs: Modern economies talk constantly of freedom: freedom to choose, to contract, to invest, to start a company, to buy and sell, freedom from state overreach, freedom to innovate.

These freedoms matter. An economy with no room for initiative goes stagnant and coercive. People need space to build, trade, cooperate, create, and respond to what they know locally, and markets protect a genuine pluralism by letting many plans coexist without one centralized vision commanding them all.

But freedom grows thin when it is separated from power. A borrower is free to accept a predatory loan when no better option exists. A worker is free to accept degrading conditions because the rent is due. A founder is free to accept extractive capital because the company will otherwise die; a tenant is free to leave the unaffordable apartment and lose her community with it; a user is free to exit a platform only by abandoning a social, professional, and economic network; a public institution is free to choose its vendor after decades of underinvestment have destroyed its internal capacity. Formal choice is not always real freedom.

Freedom worth the name requires protection from domination: real alternatives, intelligible terms, accountable power, and institutions that keep vulnerability from becoming another person’s business model — the Franciscan refusal, still on duty. It requires markets, and also laws, norms, commons, unions, cooperatives, public institutions, mission-locked firms, Steward Ownership, antitrust, social protections, and moral culture.

Capital that serves life increases agency. It helps people, communities, and institutions become more capable; it supports productive work, resilient households, durable firms, open infrastructure, and future possibility. Capital that feeds on life narrows agency: it deals in dependency, lock-in, debt pressure, opacity, artificial scarcity, and forced exit, offering choice inside a cage it helped construct.
An economy ordered toward human flourishing must therefore seek freedom without domination.

sections.title: Entrepreneurship as Stewardship
paragraphs: Entrepreneurship has spent decades being celebrated as disruption, and sometimes disruption is exactly what a situation needs. Existing systems can be unjust, stagnant, inefficient, captured, or simply unable to meet real needs. Entrepreneurs can reveal new possibilities; they can build tools, organizations, services, and infrastructure that improve life, take risks others avoid, and coordinate resources in ways nobody had tried.
But disruption is not a moral category.

Some disruption heals and some destroys; some liberates and some encloses. It can serve an unmet need or manufacture a dependency, challenge an unjust incumbent or merely install a new form of domination where the old one stood. The word tells you something broke. It does not tell you whether the breaking served life.
Entrepreneurship needs a better name for its highest form, and the tradition has been holding one for us all along: stewardship.

The steward-entrepreneur asks a different set of questions. Not only “What can I build?” but “What has been entrusted to me?” Not only “Can this scale?” but “What happens if it does?” Not only “Can this attract capital?” but “What kind of capital can this accept without losing its soul?” Not only “Can this market be captured?” but “Should this good be captured at all?”

None of that makes entrepreneurship timid; it makes it accountable. A steward can be ambitious. A steward can build institutions, technologies, companies, funds, platforms, and infrastructure. But a steward knows that power is entrusted for a purpose — that control exists for service, and surplus exists for mission, resilience, generosity, and future capacity. Growth must remain ordered. Exit is not the highest proof of success. The company is not prey, the commons is not raw material, users are not resources to be harvested, workers are not costs to be minimized. The future is not collateral.

Entrepreneurship as stewardship is one of the central tasks of the age. It needs founders who can resist ego and extraction both, investors who will accept a fair return rather than an infinite claim, governance forms that protect a mission beyond the founder’s personality, and cultures that honor durability, service, and restraint as much as speed, scale, and valuation.
It requires us, in short, to admire different things.

sections.title: Markets Re-Embedded Within Moral Life
paragraphs: Markets cannot answer the question of the good by themselves. They can reveal willingness to pay, but not whether the desire behind it is well ordered. They can coordinate scarcity without determining justice, reward innovation without telling us whether the innovation serves life, and generate prices without deciding what should never be priced at all. They discipline waste — and also punish care. They reward service — and also reward manipulation.

So markets need moral surroundings, and the surroundings are unglamorous: families that form trust and schools that teach judgment; religious and philosophical traditions that discipline desire; laws that restrain fraud, coercion, monopoly, and externalization; public institutions that protect common goods. They need workers with voice and communities with agency, accounting that reveals hidden depletion, governance that protects purpose, capital structures that define enough, commons that cannot be casually enclosed, and Sabbath practices that interrupt the total claim of production.

None of this is anti-market; it is the condition for markets that remain humane. A market without moral culture becomes predatory, and a moral culture without economic competence becomes ineffective. A state without subsidiarity grows overbearing; subsidiarity without solidarity leaves the weak exposed. Solidarity without institutional form is only sentiment, institutional form without conversion is only bureaucracy, and conversion without structure is fragile. Moral economy needs every link in that chain, held in relation.

That is why no single tradition is enough. Catholic Social Teaching brings dignity, subsidiarity, solidarity, structural sin, and the common good. Protestant vocation and stewardship bring work as calling and property as entrusted responsibility. Neo-Calvinist sphere sovereignty protects institutional plurality; ordoliberalism reminds us that markets require a moral and legal order; the cooperative and mutualist traditions show that ownership can be shared and purpose-built; Austrian economics warns against centralized arrogance and teaches the value of dispersed knowledge; and the commons tradition remembers that not every good should be governed by private capture.
The task is not to collapse these traditions into one system but to let them correct and deepen one another. A mature moral economy will be plural, disciplined, and constructive.

sections.title: Recovering the Question of the Good
paragraphs: “What is an economy for?” is not the kind of question a formula answers once and for all. It has to be asked again and again, at every level of economic life — of this household and this company, this investment and this technology, this platform, this law, this fund, this market; of the surplus, of the growth, of the freedom itself.

And the answer cannot be merely more. More growth, more return, more scale, more efficiency, more engagement, more liquidity, more innovation, more choice, more data, more automation, more speed. More is not an end. It is a direction. It has no telos of its own, and without one it can become monstrous.

The good of an economy is human flourishing — not as a vague slogan but as a governing standard. Human flourishing includes material sufficiency and meaningful work; freedom from domination; stable households and trustworthy institutions; ecological responsibility and civic participation; spiritual life, friendship, beauty, knowledge, and rest; and the capacity of communities to shape their own futures.

No economy will realize all of this perfectly, because no structure outruns the people inside it: greed, vanity, fear, laziness, pride, envy, and self-deception do not disappear through better institutional design. But structures can make some vices easier and some virtues harder. They can reward extraction or stewardship, train appetite or restraint, make justice ordinary or heroic.

The question of the good, in other words, is architectural. It becomes law and governance, ownership and accounting, procurement and lending terms; it becomes software licenses, board duties, labor protections, data rights, repayment caps, mission locks, and commons governance. The good, if it is to govern anything, must take form.

sections.title: Capital That Serves Life
paragraphs: The title of this book is a claim and a hope: capital can serve life.

After so many chapters spent diagnosing extraction, it would be easy to talk as if capital itself were the enemy. It would also be false. Capital is stored capacity, the ability to build across time. It can fund what does not yet exist and preserve what would otherwise collapse. It can help people take risks, cultivate land, create tools, build institutions, maintain infrastructure, support workers, develop technology, and pass gifts to generations not yet born.
Capital is dangerous because it is powerful. But power can be ordered.

Capital serves life when it stays connected to real goods, governed by purpose, limited by justice, accountable to communities, patient with time, and willing to receive a fair return without claiming sovereignty. It feeds on life when it extracts from dependency, dominates through debt, encloses commons, externalizes harm, captures public value, manipulates desire, demands liquidity at any cost, and treats every form of life as material for return.

From the outside the difference is not always obvious. It is written into structure — which is why you find it by asking structural questions. Who controls? Who benefits? Who bears the risk, and who can leave? Who decides when enough has been reached? Who protects the mission, who accounts for hidden harm, who speaks for the commons? Who receives the surplus, and who is responsible after capital exits?
Answer those, and you know whether the capital in front of you is servant or master.

Non-Extractive Capital is one attempt to answer them institutionally — not the only answer, and not a complete one, but a demonstration of the kind of work required. Moral economy has to become concrete enough to enter term sheets, bylaws, repayment schedules, governance rights, ownership forms, and reporting systems. A beautiful principle that cannot survive contact with capital markets is not yet enough. The principle has to be built.

sections.title: The Work Ahead
paragraphs: Recovering moral economy will take many kinds of work, and the list is humbling.

It will take intellectual work: retrieving forgotten traditions, clarifying concepts, distinguishing legitimate profit from extraction, developing a richer theory of productive capital. Legal work, to create and protect forms of enterprise in which mission, stewardship, and ownership can be structured differently. Financial work — funds, repayment mechanisms, risk-sharing instruments, and accounting systems designed so that capital can receive a fair return without dominating the enterprise. Entrepreneurial work: companies that serve real needs without surrendering to exit logic. Political work, resisting monopoly, rent-seeking, regulatory capture, housing speculation, ecological externalization, and digital dependency. Technological work: open, interoperable, accountable infrastructure, sustained open source, and the governance of AI as a matter of public and moral concern. Cultural work, perhaps the slowest of all — changing what we admire, what we celebrate, what we teach, what we fund, and what we call success. And spiritual work: recovering limits, Sabbath, humility, gratitude, mercy, and freedom from the endless hunger for more.

No one person can do all this, and neither can any firm, fund, church, state, movement, or discipline. That is why moral economy must be an ecology. It needs philosophers and accountants, theologians and lawyers, founders and workers, investors and regulators, engineers and monks, policymakers and maintainers, households and universities, churches and cooperatives, public institutions and private enterprises — all the places where economic life is actually made.

sections.title: The Final Question
paragraphs: The old question returns at the end because it was never only old. It waits beneath the next investment and the next company, the next platform and the next AI system, the next housing policy and the next public procurement decision, the next open-source license, the next debt instrument, the next acquisition offer, the next fund structure, the next board meeting, the next ordinary act of work.
What is this for?

If the answer is extraction, we should not be surprised by the world that follows: more activity and less life, more connection and less communion, more intelligence and less wisdom, more wealth and less security, more innovation and less agency, more growth and less flourishing.

But if the answer is life, another economy becomes possible. Not a perfect one, or a pure one, or one free of conflict, scarcity, failure, and sin — but an economy better ordered, more truthful, more accountable, more humane. An economy in which capital is useful but not ultimate and markets coordinate but do not rule; in which ownership carries obligation and work can be a vocation; in which technology remains a servant, the commons are protected, and finance is disciplined by surplus and by limits; in which enterprise is a form of stewardship, and human beings are not required to justify their existence before the demands of return.
That is the economy this book has tried to imagine.
An economy ordered toward human flourishing.
An economy in which capital serves life.
